$$C^{d} \equiv M \cdot 1^{k} \equiv M \pmod{n} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Tip:** Examiners often test the case $\gcd(M, n) \ne 1$. When $M$ is a multiple of $p$ or $q$, the proof uses **Fermat's Little Theorem** and **Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT)** instead. Be ready to write this.

## 2.4 Security Argument

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is RSA Hard to Break?**
> - An attacker sees $(e, n)$ and $C$.
> - To recover $M$, attacker must solve $C = M^{e} \pmod n$ — equivalent to computing the **$e$-th modular root**.
> - The fastest classical method is to **factor $n$ into $p \cdot q$** (allowing $\phi(n)$ to be reconstructed, then $d$).
> - **Best known factoring algorithm (GNFS)**: sub-exponential but super-polynomial time.
> - **Recommended modulus size today**: $n \ge 2048$ bits (NIST SP 800-57).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n = p \cdot q$ | RSA modulus |
| 2 | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Euler's totient for semiprime |
| 3 | $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ | Public exponent coprimality |
| 4 | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | Key inversion relation |
| 5 | $C = M^{e} \bmod n$ | RSA encryption |
| 6 | $M = C^{d} \bmod n$ | RSA decryption |
| 7 | $a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ | Euler's theorem (validity) |
| 8 | $a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ | Fermat's Little Theorem |
| 9 | $\lambda(n) = \text{lcm}(p-1, q-1)$ | Carmichael function (optimized) |
| 10 | $\text{Bit Security} \approx 0.5 \cdot \log_2 n$ | Symmetric equivalent strength |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write $\phi(n) = p \cdot q$ (forgetting the $-1$s). This is a **direct 2-mark deduction** in valuation. Always write $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **TLS Handshake (RSA mode):** Client encrypts a pre-master secret with the server's RSA public key.
- **PGP / GPG email encryption:** RSA encrypts the symmetric session key.
- **Code & firmware signing:** RSA-PSS or RSA-PKCS#1 v1.5 signatures verify software integrity.
- **EMV Chip Cards (banking):** RSA-based authentication protocols.
- **Blockchain / Cryptocurrency:** Early Bitcoin addresses used RSA-like ECDSA.
- **CPS Device Identity (X.509 certificates):** Every IoT gateway in a smart factory carries an RSA-signed certificate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Worked Example — Full Key Generation, Encryption, Decryption

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example Setup:**
> Let $p = 3$ and $q = 11$ (small primes for pedagogical clarity). Alice generates her RSA keys, Bob encrypts a message, and Alice decrypts it.

### Step A — Key Generation (Alice)

**Step 1:** Choose two distinct primes.
$$p = 3, \quad q = 11$$

**Step 2:** Compute the modulus $n$.
$$n = p \cdot q = 3 \cdot 11 = 33$$

**Step 3:** Compute Euler's totient $\phi(n)$.
$$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = (3-1)(11-1) = 2 \cdot 10 = 20$$

**Step 4:** Choose public exponent $e$ such that $1 < e < 20$ and $\gcd(e, 20) = 1$.
- Test: $\gcd(3, 20) = 1$ ✓
- Choose $e = 3$.

**Step 5:** Compute private exponent $d$ such that $3 \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{20}$.

Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:
$$20 = 6 \cdot 3 + 2$$
$$3 = 1 \cdot 2 + 1$$
$$2 = 2 \cdot 1 + 0$$

Back-substitution:
$$1 = 3 - 1 \cdot 2 = 3 - 1 \cdot (20 - 6 \cdot 3) = 7 \cdot 3 - 1 \cdot 20$$

Therefore $3 \cdot 7 \equiv 1 \pmod{20}$, giving $d = 7$.

**Step 6:** Publish the key pair.
- **Public key:** $(e, n) = (3, 33)$
- **Private key:** $(d, n) = (7, 33)$

### Step B — Encryption (Bob)

Suppose Bob wishes to send the plaintext integer $M = 4$ to Alice.

**Step 1:** Verify that $0 \le M < n$: indeed $0 \le 4 < 33$ ✓, and $\gcd(4, 33) = 1$ ✓.

**Step 2:** Compute the ciphertext.
$$C = M^{e} \bmod n = 4^{3} \bmod 33$$

Evaluate:
$$4^{2} = 16$$
$$4^{3} = 4^{2} \cdot 4 = 16 \cdot 4 = 64$$
$$64 \bmod 33 = 64 - 1 \cdot 33 = 31$$

Therefore $C = 31$. Bob transmits $C = 31$ to Alice.

### Step C — Decryption (Alice)

**Step 1:** Alice receives $C = 31$ and applies her private key.
$$M = C^{d} \bmod n = 31^{7} \bmod 33$$

**Step 2:** Reduce the base first to simplify.
$$31 \equiv -2 \pmod{33}$$

So:
$$31^{7} \equiv (-2)^{7} \pmod{33}$$

**Step 3:** Compute powers of $-2$ modulo 33.
$$(-2)^{1} \equiv -2 \equiv 31 \pmod{33}$$
$$(-2)^{2} \equiv 4 \pmod{33}$$
$$(-2)^{3} \equiv -8 \equiv 25 \pmod{33}$$
$$(-2)^{4} \equiv 16 \pmod{33}$$
$$(-2)^{5} \equiv -32 \equiv 1 \pmod{33}$$

**Step 4:** Use the order-5 cycle to reduce the exponent $7$.
$$(-2)^{7} = (-2)^{5} \cdot (-2)^{2} \equiv 1 \cdot 4 \equiv 4 \pmod{33}$$

**Step 5:** Recovered plaintext:
$$M = 4 \quad \checkmark$$

The original message is recovered successfully.

### Step D — Verification via Euler's Theorem (Sanity Check)

By construction, $e \cdot d = 3 \cdot 7 = 21 = 1 + 1 \cdot 20 = 1 + \phi(n)$.

Therefore:
$$C^{d} \equiv M^{e \cdot d} = M^{1 + \phi(n)} = M \cdot M^{\phi(n)} \equiv M \cdot 1 \equiv M \pmod{n} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Second Worked Example — Larger Primes, $e$ Inversion with More Arithmetic

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** $p = 61$, $q = 53$, plaintext $M = 65$.

**Step 1 — Modulus:**
$$n = 61 \cdot 53 = 3233$$

**Step 2 — Totient:**
$$\phi(n) = 60 \cdot 52 = 3120$$

**Step 3 — Public exponent:** Choose $e = 17$ (since $\gcd(17, 3120) = 1$).

**Step 4 — Find $d$ via Extended Euclidean Algorithm.**

We need $17d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$.

Run the forward Euclidean chain:
$$3120 = 183 \cdot 17 + 9$$
$$17 = 1 \cdot 9 + 8$$
$$9 = 1 \cdot 8 + 1$$
$$8 = 8 \cdot 1 + 0$$

Back-substitute to isolate $1$:
$$1 = 9 - 1 \cdot 8$$
$$1 = 9 - 1 \cdot (17 - 1 \cdot 9) = 2 \cdot 9 - 1 \cdot 17$$
$$1 = 2 \cdot (3120 - 183 \cdot 17) - 1 \cdot 17 = 2 \cdot 3120 - 367 \cdot 17$$

Therefore $17 \cdot (-367) \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$, so:
$$d \equiv -367 \equiv 3120 - 367 = 2753 \pmod{3120}$$

**Verification:** $17 \cdot 2753 = 46801 = 15 \cdot 3120 + 1 = 46800 + 1$ ✓

**Step 5 — Public key:** $(17, 3233)$ ; **Private key:** $(2753, 3233)$.

**Step 6 — Encryption:**
$$C = 65^{17} \bmod 3233$$

Using repeated squaring (mod 3233):
$$65^{2} = 4225 \bmod 3233 = 992$$
$$65^{4} = 992^{2} = 984064 \bmod 3233$$
$$984064 / 3233 \approx 304.4, \quad 304 \cdot 3233 = 982832, \quad 984064 - 982832 = 1232$$
$$65^{4} \equiv 1232 \pmod{3233}$$
$$65^{8} = 1232^{2} = 1517824 \bmod 3233$$
$$1517824 / 3233 \approx 469.4, \quad 469 \cdot 3233 = 1516277, \quad 1517824 - 1516277 = 1547$$
$$65^{8} \equiv 1547 \pmod{3233}$$
$$65^{16} = 1547^{2} = 2393209 \bmod 3233$$
$$2393209 / 3233 \approx 740.2, \quad 740 \cdot 3233 = 2392420, \quad 2393209 - 2392420 = 789$$
$$65^{16} \equiv 789 \pmod{3233}$$

Now $17 = 16 + 1$, so:
$$65^{17} = 65^{16} \cdot 65 \equiv 789 \cdot 65 = 51285 \bmod 3233$$
$$51285 / 3233 \approx 15.86, \quad 15 \cdot 3233 = 48495, \quad 51285 - 48495 = 2790$$
$$C = 2790$$

**Step 7 — Decryption:**
$$M = 2790^{2753} \bmod 3233$$

This is computed using **CRT-RSA optimization** by splitting into two congruences:

Modulo 61:
$$2790 \bmod 61 = 2790 - 45 \cdot 61 = 2790 - 2745 = 45$$
$$45^{2753} \bmod 61$$

By Fermat's Little Theorem, $45^{60} \equiv 1 \pmod{61}$. Reduce exponent:
$$2753 = 60 \cdot 45 + 53 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 45^{2753} \equiv 45^{53} \pmod{61}$$
$$45 \equiv -16 \pmod{61}$$
$$45^{53} \equiv (-16)^{53} \pmod{61}$$

Modulo 53:
$$2790 \bmod 53 = 2790 - 52 \cdot 53 = 2790 - 2756 = 34$$
$$34^{2753} \bmod 53$$
$$34^{52} \equiv 1 \pmod{53}, \quad 2753 = 52 \cdot 52 + 49$$
$$34^{2753} \equiv 34^{49} \pmod{53}$$

After both congruences are solved, CRT combines them to yield $M = 65$ ✓.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Trap Questions

| Trap Pattern | Common Mistake | Correct Approach |
|---|---|---|
| Confusing $\phi(n)$ with $n$ | Writing $\phi(n) = p \cdot q$ | Always $(p-1)(q-1)$ |
| Wrong direction of inversion | Solving $d \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod n$ | Should be $\pmod{\phi(n)}$ |
| Exponent reduction | Reducing exponent mod $n$ | Reduce mod $\phi(n)$ (or $\lambda(n)$) |
| $\gcd(M, n) \ne 1$ | Claiming RSA fails | It still works (Fermat + CRT) |
| Choosing $e$ | Picking $e = 1$ or $e$ not coprime to $\phi(n)$ | $e$ must be in $(1, \phi(n))$ with $\gcd = 1$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RSA End-to-End Communication Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    aliceKeyGen["Alice: Key Generation"] --> stepA1["Choose primes p, q"]
    stepA1 --> stepA2["Compute n = p * q"]
    stepA2 --> stepA3["Compute phi_n = p-1 times q-1"]
    stepA3 --> stepA4["Pick e with gcd e phi_n = 1"]
    stepA4 --> stepA5["Compute d with e * d congruent 1 mod phi_n"]
    stepA5 --> stepA6["Publish public key e n"]
    stepA5 --> stepA7["Keep private key d n secret"]

    bobPrep["Bob: Message Preparation"] --> stepB1["Convert plaintext M to integer in 0 to n-1"]
    stepB1 --> stepB2["Compute C = M to power e mod n"]
    stepB2 --> transmit["Transmit ciphertext C over insecure channel"]

    stepA6 --> transmit
    transmit --> aliceDecrypt["Alice: Decryption"]
    aliceDecrypt --> stepD1["Compute M = C to power d mod n"]
    stepD1 --> stepD2["Convert M back to plaintext"]

    stepA7 --> aliceDecrypt
```

## 4.2 RSA Mathematical Foundation Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    primeChoice["Large Primes p and q"] --> modulusCalc["Modulus n = p * q"]
    modulusCalc --> totientCalc["Totient phi n = p-1 q-1"]
    totientCalc --> exponentChoice["Public Exponent e coprime to phi n"]
    exponentChoice --> inverseCalc["Private Exponent d = e inverse mod phi n"]
    inverseCalc --> keyPair["Key Pair: Public e n and Private d n"]

    eulerThm["Euler's Theorem a to power phi n congruent 1 mod n"] -.-> correctness["Correctness Proof"]
    fermatThm["Fermat's Little Theorem a to power p-1 congruent 1 mod p"] -.-> correctness
    crtThm["Chinese Remainder Theorem"] -.-> crtRsa["CRT-RSA Speedup"]

    keyPair --> correctness
    keyPair --> crtRsa
    crtRsa --> decrypt["Faster Decryption"]
    correctness --> decrypt
```

## 4.3 RSA Functional Architecture — Block-Level CPS View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph cpSensor["Cyber-Physical Sensor Node"]
        sensorData["Telemetry Data"] --> mConverter["Message-to-Integer Converter"]
        mConverter --> encModule["RSA Encryption Engine: C = M^e mod n"]
    end

    encModule --> channel["Insecure Wireless Channel"]

    channel --> decModule["RSA Decryption Engine: M = C^d mod n"]
    decModule --> verify["Signature / Integrity Check"]
    verify --> cloud["Cloud Controller / SCADA Master"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A, Q.1.a]**
> **Q1. (3 marks)** What are the three basic algorithms that define the RSA cryptosystem? State the correctness condition that must hold between them.

**Model Answer:**
The RSA cryptosystem is defined by three probabilistic polynomial-time algorithms:
- $\text{KeyGen}(\lambda)$: generates a public key $pk$ and private key $sk$.
- $\text{Enc}(pk, M)$: encrypts plaintext $M$ into ciphertext $C$.
- $\text{Dec}(sk, C)$: decrypts ciphertext $C$ back to plaintext $M$.

The correctness condition is:
$$\text{Dec}\bigl(sk, \text{Enc}(pk, M)\bigr) = M$$
for every message $M$ in the message space.

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B, Q.1.b]**
> **Q2. (3 marks)** State **Euler's Theorem** and explain its role in proving the correctness of RSA decryption.

**Model Answer:**
**Euler's Theorem:** If $\gcd(a, n) = 1$, then $a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$.

**Role in RSA:** Since $e \cdot d = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)$, decrypting yields:
$$C^{d} \equiv (M^{e})^{d} \equiv M^{1 + k\phi(n)} \equiv M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^{k} \equiv M \cdot 1^{k} \equiv M \pmod{n}$$
Hence decryption recovers the original plaintext, given $\gcd(M, n) = 1$.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Q.4 from Module 3]**
>
> **Question A (14 marks):**
> In an RSA cryptosystem, the public key is $(e, n) = (5, 119)$. You intercept the ciphertext $C = 23$ sent to the owner. Perform the following:
> **(a)** Factor $n$ to recover the primes $p$ and $q$, and determine the private key $d$. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Decrypt the ciphertext and recover the plaintext $M$. Also, encrypt the recovered plaintext back to verify correctness. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** Factor $n = 119$.

Test divisibility by small primes:
$$119 / 7 = 17 \quad \text{so} \quad 119 = 7 \times 17$$

Therefore $p = 7$ and $q = 17$.

Compute Euler's totient:
$$\phi(n) = (7-1)(17-1) = 6 \cdot 16 = 96$$

Find $d$ such that $5d \equiv 1 \pmod{96}$ using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:
$$96 = 19 \cdot 5 + 1$$
$$5 = 5 \cdot 1 + 0$$

Back-substitute:
$$1 = 96 - 19 \cdot 5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 5 \cdot (-19) \equiv 1 \pmod{96}$$
$$d = -19 \equiv 96 - 19 = 77 \pmod{96}$$

**Private key:** $d = 77$. (Verify: $5 \cdot 77 = 385 = 4 \cdot 96 + 1$ ✓)

**Part (b):** Decrypt $C = 23$.
$$M = C^{d} \bmod n = 23^{77} \bmod 119$$

**Optimization using CRT** (recommended for speed):

Modulo 7:
$$23 \equiv 2 \pmod 7, \quad 2^{77} \bmod 7$$
By Fermat: $2^{6} \equiv 1 \pmod 7$, $77 = 12 \cdot 6 + 5$, so $2^{77} \equiv 2^{5} = 32 \equiv 4 \pmod 7$.

Modulo 17:
$$23 \equiv 6 \pmod{17}, \quad 6^{77} \bmod 17$$
By Fermat: $6^{16} \equiv 1 \pmod{17}$, $77 = 4 \cdot 16 + 13$, so $6^{77} \equiv 6^{13} \pmod{17}$.
$$6^{2} = 36 \equiv 2 \pmod{17}$$
$$6^{4} \equiv 4 \pmod{17}$$
$$6^{8} \equiv 16 \pmod{17}$$
$$6^{13} = 6^{8} \cdot 6^{4} \cdot 6^{1} \equiv 16 \cdot 4 \cdot 6 = 384 \equiv 384 - 22 \cdot 17 = 384 - 374 = 10 \pmod{17}$$

**Combine via CRT:** find $M$ such that $M \equiv 4 \pmod 7$ and $M \equiv 10 \pmod{17}$.

Let $M = 10 + 17k$. Then $10 + 17k \equiv 4 \pmod 7$, i.e., $3 + 3k \equiv 4 \pmod 7$, so $3k \equiv 1 \pmod 7$.
Inverse of 3 mod 7 is 5 (since $3 \cdot 5 = 15 \equiv 1$). So $k \equiv 5 \pmod 7$, take $k = 5$.
$$M = 10 + 17 \cdot 5 = 10 + 85 = 95$$

**Re-encrypt to verify:** $C' = 95^{5} \bmod 119$.
$$95 \equiv -24 \equiv -24 + 119 = 95 \pmod{119}$$
Use CRT again. Modulo 7: $95 \equiv 4 \pmod 7$, so $4^{5} = 1024 \equiv 1024 - 146 \cdot 7 = 1024 - 1022 = 2 \pmod 7$.
Modulo 17: $95 \equiv 10 \pmod{17}$, so $10^{5} = 100000$. $100000 \bmod 17$: $17 \cdot 5882 = 99994$, so $100000 - 99994 = 6 \pmod{17}$.

Combine: $M \equiv 2 \pmod 7$, $M \equiv 6 \pmod{17}$. Let $M = 6 + 17k$, then $6 + 3k \equiv 2 \pmod 7$, so $3k \equiv -4 \equiv 3 \pmod 7$, $k \equiv 1 \pmod 7$. So $M = 6 + 17 = 23$ ✓

**Recovered plaintext: $M = 95$.**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Q.5 from Module 3 — Alternative Choice]**
>
> **Question B (14 marks):**
> For the RSA system with $p = 13$ and $q = 17$:
> **(a)** Generate the complete key pair (public and private) and explain each step with appropriate justification. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Encrypt the plaintext $M = 100$ using the public key, then decrypt it using the private key. Show every modular arithmetic step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Key Generation**

**Step 1:** Primes chosen — $p = 13$, $q = 17$.

**Step 2:** Compute modulus $n$:
$$n = p \cdot q = 13 \cdot 17 = 221$$

**Step 3:** Compute Euler's totient:
$$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 12 \cdot 16 = 192$$

**Step 4:** Choose public exponent $e$. Try $e = 5$: $\gcd(5, 192) = 1$ ✓.
(Smaller valid $e$ is acceptable in textbook problems.)

**Step 5:** Find $d$ such that $5d \equiv 1 \pmod{192}$.

Extended Euclidean Algorithm:
$$192 = 38 \cdot 5 + 2$$
$$5 = 2 \cdot 2 + 1$$
$$2 = 2 \cdot 1 + 0$$

Back-substitute:
$$1 = 5 - 2 \cdot 2 = 5 - 2 \cdot (192 - 38 \cdot 5) = 77 \cdot 5 - 2 \cdot 192$$

Therefore $5 \cdot 77 \equiv 1 \pmod{192}$, giving $d = 77$.

**Verification:** $5 \cdot 77 = 385 = 2 \cdot 192 + 1$ ✓

**Public key:** $(e, n) = (5, 221)$.
**Private key:** $(d, n) = (77, 221)$.

**Part (b): Encryption & Decryption**

**Encryption of $M = 100$:**
$$C = M^{e} \bmod n = 100^{5} \bmod 221$$

Repeated squaring:
$$100^{2} = 10000 \bmod 221$$
$$221 \cdot 45 = 9945, \quad 10000 - 9945 = 55$$
$$100^{2} \equiv 55 \pmod{221}$$
$$100^{4} \equiv 55^{2} = 3025 \bmod 221$$
$$221 \cdot 13 = 2873, \quad 3025 - 2873 = 152$$
$$100^{4} \equiv 152 \pmod{221}$$
$$100^{5} \equiv 152 \cdot 100 = 15200 \bmod 221$$
$$221 \cdot 68 = 15028, \quad 15200 - 15028 = 172$$
$$C = 172$$

**Decryption of $C = 172$:**
$$M = C^{d} \bmod n = 172^{77} \bmod 221$$

Use **CRT** to split into mod 13 and mod 17.

**Modulo 13:**
$$172 \equiv 172 - 13 \cdot 13 = 172 - 169 = 3 \pmod{13}$$
$$3^{77} \bmod 13$$
By Fermat: $3^{12} \equiv 1 \pmod{13}$, $77 = 6 \cdot 12 + 5$, so $3^{77} \equiv 3^{5} = 243 \equiv 243 - 18 \cdot 13 = 243 - 234 = 9 \pmod{13}$.

**Modulo 17:**
$$172 \equiv 172 - 10 \cdot 17 = 172 - 170 = 2 \pmod{17}$$
$$2^{77} \bmod 17$$
By Fermat: $2^{16} \equiv 1 \pmod{17}$, $77 = 4 \cdot 16 + 13$, so $2^{77} \equiv 2^{13} \pmod{17}$.
$$2^{4} = 16 \equiv -1 \pmod{17}$$
$$2^{8} \equiv 1 \pmod{17}$$
$$2^{13} = 2^{8} \cdot 2^{4} \cdot 2^{1} \equiv 1 \cdot (-1) \cdot 2 = -2 \equiv 15 \pmod{17}$$

**Combine via CRT:** find $M$ with $M \equiv 9 \pmod{13}$ and $M \equiv 15 \pmod{17}$.

Let $M = 9 + 13k$. Then $9 + 13k \equiv 15 \pmod{17}$, i.e., $13k \equiv 6 \pmod{17}$.
$13 \equiv -4 \pmod{17}$, so $-4k \equiv 6 \pmod{17}$, $k \equiv -6/4 \pmod{17}$.
$4^{-1} \pmod{17}$: $4 \cdot 13 = 52 = 3 \cdot 17 + 1$, so $4^{-1} \equiv 13 \pmod{17}$.
$k \equiv -6 \cdot 13 = -78 \equiv -78 + 5 \cdot 17 = -78 + 85 = 7 \pmod{17}$.

So $k = 7$:
$$M = 9 + 13 \cdot 7 = 9 + 91 = 100 \quad \checkmark$$

**Recovered plaintext: $M = 100$.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $-1$s in $\phi(n)$:** Writing $\phi(n) = p \cdot q$ instead of $(p-1)(q-1)$. **−2 marks.**
> 2. **Inverting mod $n$ instead of mod $\phi(n)$:** Solving $ed \equiv 1 \pmod n$ gives a *wrong* $d$. **−3 marks.**
> 3. **No verification step:** Examiners reward you for verifying $ed = 1 + k\phi(n)$. **+1 mark bonus.**
> 4. **Plaintext $M$ chosen such that $\gcd(M, n) \ne 1$:** If $M$ is a multiple of $p$ or $q$, you must still apply the algorithm; the result is still $M$ (Fermat's theorem handles it). **−2 marks if you claim RSA fails.**
> 5. **Not using modular reduction during repeated squaring:** Always reduce mod $n$ at *every* step to keep numbers small.
> 6. **Confusing $d$ with $1/e$:** $d$ is the *modular multiplicative inverse*, not the arithmetic reciprocal.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **RSA is asymmetric** — encryption key is public, decryption key is private.
- **Security basis:** Integer factorization of $n = p \cdot q$ is computationally intractable for large primes ($\ge 2048$ bits in production).
- **Modulus:** $n = p \cdot q$.
- **Totient:** $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ (only valid because $n$ is a semiprime).
- **Public exponent $e$:** must satisfy $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.
- **Private exponent $d$:** satisfies $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ (computed via Extended Euclidean Algorithm).
- **Encryption:** $C = M^{e} \bmod n$.
- **Decryption:** $M = C^{d} \bmod n$.
- **Correctness proof:** Depends on Euler's theorem — $M^{e \cdot d} = M^{1 + k\phi(n)} \equiv M \pmod n$.
- **Common exponent choices:** $e = 3, 17, 65537$; the latter is the industry standard for security/efficiency tradeoff.
- **Plaintext restriction:** $0 \le M < n$ and ideally $\gcd(M, n) = 1$.
- **Padding is mandatory in practice:** Real RSA (PKCS#1 v1.5, OAEP) pads $M$ before encryption to prevent chosen-ciphertext attacks.
- **Decryption speedup:** CRT-RSA gives $\sim 4\times$ speedup by computing $C^{d} \bmod p$ and $C^{d} \bmod q$ separately.
- **Carmichael function $\lambda(n) = \text{lcm}(p-1, q-1)$** is sometimes used instead of $\phi(n)$ for smaller $d$.
- **RSA signature:** $\sigma = M^{d} \bmod n$ (sign with private key); verification: $M = \sigma^{e} \bmod n$.
- **Standard key sizes (NIST 2024):** RSA-2048 (minimum), RSA-3072 (recommended for post-2030), RSA-4096 (high-security).
- **Threat model:** Shor's algorithm on a sufficiently large quantum computer breaks RSA in polynomial time — hence the migration to **post-quantum cryptography (PQC)** such as lattice-based schemes (Kyber, Dilithium).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ElGamal Cryptosystem - Key Generation - Encryption - Decryption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT523 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** ElGamal Cryptosystem - Key Generation - Encryption - Decryption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> The **ElGamal Cryptosystem** is an asymmetric (public-key) cryptographic protocol proposed by **Taher ElGamal in 1985**, whose security rests on the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** over a finite multiplicative group. It generalizes the Diffie–Hellman key exchange into a complete public-key encryption and digital signature scheme, producing ciphertexts whose length is **twice** that of the plaintext.

The cryptosystem operates over the multiplicative group $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$, where $p$ is a large prime, and the generator $g$ is a **primitive root** modulo $p$ (i.e., $g$ has multiplicative order $p-1$).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Locked Two-Box Mailbox"

Imagine Alice wants to receive secret letters. She places a **transparent drop-box (public key)** at her door. Anyone can drop a letter inside, but only Alice, who owns the **secret combination (private key)**, can open it.

Here is the clever twist of ElGamal: before dropping the letter, Bob performs a **random magic trick** — he generates a fresh, ephemeral lock for *every single message*. He seals the letter using this throw-away lock, then secretly teleports the combination to Alice through a **mathematical tunnel** (the shared Diffie–Hellman secret). Even if an eavesdropper sees the public box, the ephemeral lock, and the tunnel, they cannot reconstruct the combination because that would require solving the **Discrete Logarithm Problem** — a problem that is computationally infeasible for sufficiently large primes.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Key Insight for KTU Exam**
> The ElGamal system is **probabilistic** (not deterministic). Re-encrypting the same plaintext under the same public key produces a **different ciphertext** every time, because of the fresh randomness $k$ chosen during encryption. This gives it **semantic security** under the Decisional Diffie–Hellman (DDH) assumption.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of ElGamal

> [!NOTE] **Three Operational Phases**
> 1. **Key Generation** — Done once by the receiver (Alice). Produces a public key and private key pair.
> 2. **Encryption** — Done by the sender (Bob) using Alice's public key and a fresh random $k$.
> 3. **Decryption** — Done by the receiver (Alice) using her private key. Recovers the original plaintext.

### 1.4 Key Terminology & Physical Constants

| Term | Meaning | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Prime modulus** | Large safe prime | $p$ |
| **Generator / Primitive root** | Element of order $p-1$ | $g$ |
| **Private key** | Random secret exponent | $x$ |
| **Public key** | Computed from private key | $y = g^x \bmod p$ |
| **Ephemeral key** | Per-message randomizer | $k$ |
| **Shared secret** | Diffie–Hellman component | $s = y^k \bmod p$ |
| **Ciphertext components** | Two-part output | $(c_1, c_2)$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Bulleted Breakdown

#### **Phase 1 — Key Generation (Performed by Receiver Alice)**

- **Step G1:** Select a large prime $p$ such that computing discrete logarithms modulo $p$ is intractable (typically $\geq 2048$ bits in modern security).
- **Step G2:** Choose a primitive root $g$ modulo $p$, where $2 \leq g \leq p-2$.
- **Step G3:** Pick a random integer $x$ as the **private key**, where $1 \leq x \leq p-2$.
- **Step G4:** Compute the public component:
$$y \equiv g^x \pmod{p}$$
- **Step G5:** Publish the **public key** $\mathrm{PK} = (p, g, y)$; keep $x$ as the **private key** $\mathrm{SK} = x$.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why is this hard to invert?**
> Given $y$, $g$, and $p$, recovering $x$ requires solving $x = \log_g y \bmod p$, the **Discrete Logarithm Problem**, which has no known polynomial-time algorithm on classical computers.

---

#### **Phase 2 — Encryption (Performed by Sender Bob)**

- **Step E1:** Obtain Alice's authentic public key $(p, g, y)$.
- **Step E2:** Convert the plaintext message $M$ into an integer with $0 \leq M \leq p-1$.
- **Step E3:** Choose a fresh, random integer $k$ (the **ephemeral key**) with $1 \leq k \leq p-2$, such that $\gcd(k, p-1) = 1$.
- **Step E4:** Compute the first ciphertext component:
$$c_1 \equiv g^k \pmod{p}$$
- **Step E5:** Compute the **shared secret**:
$$s \equiv y^k \pmod{p}$$
- **Step E6:** Compute the second ciphertext component by masking the message:
$$c_2 \equiv M \cdot s \pmod{p}$$
- **Step E7:** Transmit the ciphertext pair $\mathbf{C} = (c_1, c_2)$ to Alice.

> [!NOTE] **Probabilistic Encryption**
> Since $k$ is freshly random for every encryption, encrypting the same $M$ yields a different $(c_1, c_2)$ each time. This is a major security advantage over RSA's deterministic encryption.

---

#### **Phase 3 — Decryption (Performed by Receiver Alice)**

- **Step D1:** Receive ciphertext $(c_1, c_2)$.
- **Step D2:** Recover the shared secret using the private key $x$:
$$s \equiv c_1^{\,x} \pmod{p}$$
- **Step D3:** Compute the **modular inverse** of $s$ modulo $p$:
$$s^{-1} \equiv s^{\,p-2} \pmod{p} \quad \text{(by Fermat's Little Theorem)}$$
- **Step D4:** Recover the plaintext:
$$M \equiv c_2 \cdot s^{-1} \pmod{p}$$

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT] **MASTER THIS TABLE BEFORE THE EXAM**

| # | Formula | Meaning / When Used |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $y \equiv g^x \pmod{p}$ | Public key generation |
| 2 | $c_1 \equiv g^k \pmod{p}$ | First ciphertext component |
| 3 | $s \equiv y^k \equiv g^{xk} \pmod{p}$ | Shared secret at sender |
| 4 | $c_2 \equiv M \cdot s \pmod{p}$ | Second ciphertext component (masked message) |
| 5 | $s \equiv c_1^{\,x} \equiv g^{kx} \pmod{p}$ | Shared secret recovery at receiver |
| 6 | $s^{-1} \equiv s^{\,p-2} \pmod{p}$ | Modular inverse via Fermat |
| 7 | $M \equiv c_2 \cdot s^{-1} \pmod{p}$ | Plaintext recovery |
| 8 | Ciphertext length $= 2 \cdot \vert p \vert$ bits | Bandwidth cost (twice RSA) |
| 9 | $1 \leq x, k \leq p-2$ | Valid range for exponents |
| 10 | $\gcd(k, p-1) = 1$ | Required to ensure $k$ is well-defined |

### 2.3 Why Does Decryption Work? — The Mathematical Truth

The proof of correctness hinges on a beautiful algebraic symmetry:

$$c_1^{\,x} \equiv (g^k)^x \equiv g^{kx} \equiv (g^x)^k \equiv y^k \equiv s \pmod{p}$$

Then:
$$c_2 \cdot s^{-1} \equiv (M \cdot s) \cdot s^{-1} \equiv M \cdot (s \cdot s^{-1}) \equiv M \cdot 1 \equiv M \pmod{p}$$

This uses the **commutativity of exponentiation in the exponent** ($g^{kx} = g^{xk}$) — the same property exploited by Diffie–Hellman.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **TLS / SSL cipher suites** historically used ElGamal variants or Diffie–Hellman (its cousin).
- **GNU Privacy Guard (GPG / PGP)** supports ElGamal as an encryption algorithm.
- **Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA)** is mathematically derived from ElGamal signatures.
- **IoT and Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** lightweight variants use ElGamal over elliptic curves (EC-ElGamal) for low-power authenticated communication.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> [!NOTE] **Worked Problem**
> Use the ElGamal public-key cryptosystem with public parameters $p = 23$ and $g = 5$. Alice's private key is $x = 6$. Bob wishes to send the message $M = 7$ using ephemeral key $k = 5$. Perform key generation, encryption, and decryption in full detail.

---

#### **STEP 1 — Key Generation**

Compute Alice's public key component:
$$y \equiv g^x \pmod{p} \;\Longrightarrow\; y \equiv 5^6 \pmod{23}$$

Decompose the exponent using repeated squaring:
$$5^2 \equiv 25 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}$$
$$5^4 \equiv (5^2)^2 \equiv 2^2 \equiv 4 \pmod{23}$$
$$5^6 \equiv 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \equiv 4 \cdot 2 \equiv 8 \pmod{23}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\;y = 8\;}$$

Public key: $\mathrm{PK} = (23, 5, 8)$; Private key: $\mathrm{SK} = x = 6$.

---

#### **STEP 2 — Encryption**

**Compute $c_1$:**
$$c_1 \equiv g^k \pmod{p} \;\Longrightarrow\; c_1 \equiv 5^5 \pmod{23}$$

Using the previously derived powers:
$$5^5 \equiv 5^4 \cdot 5 \equiv 4 \cdot 5 \equiv 20 \pmod{23}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\;c_1 = 20\;}$$

**Compute the shared secret $s$:**
$$s \equiv y^k \pmod{p} \;\Longrightarrow\; s \equiv 8^5 \pmod{23}$$

Compute by repeated squaring:
$$8^2 \equiv 64 \equiv 64 - 2(23) = 18 \equiv -5 \pmod{23}$$
$$8^4 \equiv (8^2)^2 \equiv (-5)^2 \equiv 25 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}$$
$$8^5 \equiv 8^4 \cdot 8 \equiv 2 \cdot 8 \equiv 16 \pmod{23}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\;s = 16\;}$$

**Compute $c_2$:**
$$c_2 \equiv M \cdot s \pmod{p} \;\Longrightarrow\; c_2 \equiv 7 \cdot 16 \pmod{23}$$
$$c_2 \equiv 112 \pmod{23}$$
$$112 = 4 \cdot 23 + 20 \;\Longrightarrow\; c_2 \equiv 20 \pmod{23}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\;c_2 = 20\;}$$

**Transmitted ciphertext:**
$$\mathbf{C} = (c_1, c_2) = (20, 20)$$

---

#### **STEP 3 — Decryption**

**Recover the shared secret $s$ using private key $x = 6$:**
$$s \equiv c_1^{\,x} \pmod{p} \;\Longrightarrow\; s \equiv 20^6 \pmod{23}$$

Compute $20^6$ modulo 23:
$$20^2 \equiv 400 \equiv 400 - 17(23) = 400 - 391 = 9 \pmod{23}$$
$$20^3 \equiv 20^2 \cdot 20 \equiv 9 \cdot 20 = 180 \equiv 180 - 7(23) = 180 - 161 = 19 \pmod{23}$$
$$20^6 \equiv (20^3)^2 \equiv 19^2 = 361 \equiv 361 - 15(23) = 361 - 345 = 16 \pmod{23}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\;s = 16\;}$$

(Sanity check: matches the sender's $s = 16$. ✓)

**Compute the modular inverse $s^{-1} \bmod 23$:**

We seek $t$ such that $16 t \equiv 1 \pmod{23}$.

Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:
$$23 = 1 \cdot 16 + 7$$
$$16 = 2 \cdot 7 + 2$$
$$7 = 3 \cdot 2 + 1$$
$$2 = 2 \cdot 1 + 0$$

Back-substitution:
$$1 = 7 - 3 \cdot 2$$
$$1 = 7 - 3(16 - 2 \cdot 7) = 7 \cdot 7 - 3 \cdot 16$$
$$1 = 7(23 - 16) - 3 \cdot 16 = 7 \cdot 23 - 10 \cdot 16$$

Therefore:
$$s^{-1} \equiv -10 \equiv 13 \pmod{23}$$

Verification: $16 \cdot 13 = 208 = 9 \cdot 23 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{23}$ ✓

**Recover the plaintext:**
$$M \equiv c_2 \cdot s^{-1} \pmod{p} \;\Longrightarrow\; M \equiv 20 \cdot 13 \pmod{23}$$
$$M \equiv 260 \pmod{23}$$
$$260 = 11 \cdot 23 + 7 \;\Longrightarrow\; M \equiv 7 \pmod{23}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\;M = 7\;}$$

The original message is recovered exactly. ✓

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison with Cousin Schemes

| Property | ElGamal | RSA | Diffie–Hellman |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Based on** | Discrete Logarithm | Integer Factorization | Discrete Logarithm |
| **Probabilistic?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (deterministic) | N/A (key exchange only) |
| **Ciphertext expansion** | $2\times$ | $\sim 1\times$ | N/A |
| **Signature support** | ✅ Yes (basis for DSA) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Forward secrecy** | Possible per message | No | ✅ Yes |
| **Standard key size** | 2048+ bits | 2048+ bits | 2048+ bits |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End ElGamal Process Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Alice wants to receive secure messages"] --> B["Alice picks prime p and primitive root g"]
    B --> C["Alice picks private key x randomly 1 to p-2"]
    C --> D["Alice computes y = g to power x mod p"]
    D --> E["Alice publishes public key PK = p, g, y"]
    E --> F["Bob obtains authentic public key of Alice"]
    F --> G["Bob chooses fresh ephemeral key k"]
    G --> H["Bob computes c1 = g to power k mod p"]
    G --> I["Bob computes shared secret s = y to power k mod p"]
    I --> J["Bob computes c2 = M times s mod p"]
    H --> K["Bob transmits ciphertext pair c1 and c2"]
    J --> K
    K --> L["Alice receives ciphertext pair"]
    L --> M["Alice computes s = c1 to power x mod p"]
    M --> N["Alice computes modular inverse of s mod p"]
    N --> O["Alice recovers M = c2 times s inverse mod p"]
    O --> P["End: Plaintext M is recovered successfully"]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Key, Cipher, Recovery Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KEYGEN["KEY GENERATION MODULE"]
        K1["Prime p generator"]
        K2["Primitive root g selector"]
        K3["Random x sampler"]
        K4["Exponentiation engine: y = g^x mod p"]
    end

    subgraph ENCRYPT["ENCRYPTION MODULE"]
        E1["Public key input: p, g, y"]
        E2["Random k sampler"]
        E3["Compute c1 = g^k mod p"]
        E4["Compute shared s = y^k mod p"]
        E5["Compute c2 = M * s mod p"]
    end

    subgraph DECRYPT["DECRYPTION MODULE"]
        D1["Ciphertext input: c1, c2"]
        D2["Compute s = c1^x mod p using private x"]
        D3["Compute s inverse mod p via Fermat"]
        D4["Recover M = c2 * s inverse mod p"]
    end

    K4 --> E1
    E5 --> D1
    D4 --> P1["Recovered plaintext M"]
```

### 4.3 Shared-Secret Symmetry Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1["Sender side: s = y to power k = g to power xk mod p"]
    R2["Receiver side: s = c1 to power x = g to power kx mod p"]
    R1 --> C["Equal because xk = kx in exponent"]
    R2 --> C
    C --> D["Both sides compute identical shared secret s"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT] **Architectural Insight for CPS**
> In a Cyber-Physical System, Alice might be a **smart meter gateway** and Bob a **field sensor**. Each sensor picks a fresh $k$ per transmission, ensuring **forward secrecy** — compromise of one session leaks no other sessions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the ElGamal Cryptosystem? Briefly explain its key generation phase.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The ElGamal Cryptosystem is a public-key cryptographic scheme proposed by Taher ElGamal in 1985, based on the **Discrete Logarithm Problem**. It provides both encryption and digital signatures.

In the **key generation phase**, the receiver (Alice) performs the following steps:

1. Chooses a large prime $p$ and a primitive root $g$ modulo $p$.
2. Selects a random integer $x$ such that $1 \leq x \leq p-2$, kept as the **private key**.
3. Computes $y \equiv g^x \pmod{p}$.
4. Publishes the public key $\mathrm{PK} = (p, g, y)$ and keeps $x$ secret.

The security relies on the intractability of computing $x = \log_g y \bmod p$.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why is the ElGamal cryptosystem called a *probabilistic* encryption scheme?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

ElGamal is **probabilistic** because a fresh random integer $k$ (the ephemeral key) is chosen independently for **every** encryption operation.

Consequently, encrypting the **same plaintext** $M$ under the **same public key** $(p, g, y)$ multiple times yields **different ciphertext pairs** $(c_1, c_2)$ each time, since:

- $c_1 = g^k \bmod p$ changes with $k$.
- $c_2 = M \cdot y^k \bmod p$ also changes with $k$.

This property provides **semantic security** — an attacker cannot tell whether two ciphertexts encrypt the same message — under the **Decisional Diffie–Hellman (DDH)** assumption.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**(a)** Describe the ElGamal public-key cryptosystem with reference to its **encryption** and **decryption** algorithms. Mention the role of the ephemeral key. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an ElGamal scheme, the public parameters are $p = 43$, $g = 3$, and Alice's public key is $y = 9$. Bob chooses ephemeral key $k = 7$ to encrypt $M = 25$. **Perform the full encryption and decryption**, recovering the plaintext. Use Alice's private key $x = 2$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 marks**

**Encryption Algorithm (Bob):**
1. Obtain Alice's public key $(p, g, y)$.
2. Convert plaintext $M$ into integer $M$ with $0 \leq M \leq p-1$.
3. Choose random integer $k$ (ephemeral key), $1 \leq k \leq p-2$.
4. Compute $c_1 \equiv g^k \pmod{p}$.
5. Compute $s \equiv y^k \pmod{p}$.
6. Compute $c_2 \equiv M \cdot s \pmod{p}$.
7. Send ciphertext $(c_1, c_2)$.

**Decryption Algorithm (Alice):**
1. Receive $(c_1, c_2)$.
2. Compute $s \equiv c_1^{\,x} \pmod{p}$.
3. Compute $s^{-1} \pmod{p}$.
4. Recover $M \equiv c_2 \cdot s^{-1} \pmod{p}$.

**Role of the Ephemeral Key:**
- The ephemeral key $k$ is freshly random for every encryption.
- It introduces randomness, making encryption **probabilistic**.
- It ensures the shared secret $s = y^k \bmod p$ is unique per session, enabling **forward secrecy** per message.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $p = 43$, $g = 3$, $y = 9$, $k = 7$, $M = 25$, $x = 2$.

**Verify:** $y = g^x \bmod p \Rightarrow 3^2 = 9 \bmod 43 = 9$ ✓

**Encryption:**

Compute $c_1$:
$$c_1 \equiv g^k \equiv 3^7 \pmod{43}$$
$$3^2 = 9,\quad 3^4 = 81 \equiv 81 - 43 = 38 \equiv -5 \pmod{43}$$
$$3^6 = 3^4 \cdot 3^2 = (-5)(9) = -45 \equiv -45 + 2(43) = 41 \equiv -2 \pmod{43}$$
$$3^7 = 3^6 \cdot 3 = (-2)(3) = -6 \equiv 37 \pmod{43}$$
$$\boxed{\;c_1 = 37\;}$$

Compute shared secret $s$:
$$s \equiv y^k \equiv 9^7 \pmod{43}$$
$$9^2 = 81 \equiv 38 \equiv -5 \pmod{43}$$
$$9^4 \equiv (-5)^2 = 25 \pmod{43}$$
$$9^6 = 9^4 \cdot 9^2 \equiv 25 \cdot (-5) = -125 \equiv -125 + 3(43) = 4 \pmod{43}$$
$$9^7 = 9^6 \cdot 9 \equiv 4 \cdot 9 = 36 \equiv -7 \pmod{43}$$
$$\boxed{\;s = 36\;}$$

Compute $c_2$:
$$c_2 \equiv M \cdot s \equiv 25 \cdot 36 \pmod{43}$$
$$25 \cdot 36 = 900$$
$$900 \bmod 43 = 900 - 20 \cdot 43 = 900 - 860 = 40$$
$$\boxed{\;c_2 = 40\;}$$

**Ciphertext:** $(c_1, c_2) = (37, 40)$.

**Decryption:**

Recover $s$:
$$s \equiv c_1^{\,x} \equiv 37^2 \pmod{43}$$
$$37 \equiv -6 \pmod{43} \;\Longrightarrow\; 37^2 = 36 \pmod{43}$$
$$s = 36 \;\checkmark$$

Find $s^{-1} \bmod 43$:
$$36 \cdot t \equiv 1 \pmod{43}$$
Using Extended Euclidean Algorithm:
$$43 = 1 \cdot 36 + 7$$
$$36 = 5 \cdot 7 + 1 \;\Longrightarrow\; 1 = 36 - 5 \cdot 7 = 36 - 5(43 - 36) = 6 \cdot 36 - 5 \cdot 43$$
$$t = 6 \;\Longrightarrow\; s^{-1} \equiv 6 \pmod{43}$$

Verification: $36 \cdot 6 = 216 = 5 \cdot 43 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{43}$ ✓

Recover $M$:
$$M \equiv c_2 \cdot s^{-1} \equiv 40 \cdot 6 = 240 \pmod{43}$$
$$240 - 5 \cdot 43 = 240 - 215 = 25$$
$$\boxed{\;M = 25\;}$$

The original plaintext is recovered exactly. ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the **security of the ElGamal Cryptosystem** with reference to the Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP) and the Diffie–Hellman Problem (DHP). Compare its ciphertext expansion with RSA. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With $p = 17$, $g = 3$, and Alice's private key $x = 5$, compute the public key $y$. Bob encrypts $M = 10$ using $k = 4$. Show all intermediate steps and then **decrypt** the ciphertext using Alice's private key to recover $M$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 marks**

**Security Foundation:**
ElGamal's security rests on the presumed intractability of:

1. **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP):** Given $(p, g, y)$, find $x$ such that $y \equiv g^x \pmod{p}$. No polynomial-time classical algorithm is known for large $p$ (e.g., 2048+ bits).

2. **Computational Diffie–Hellman Problem (CDHP):** Given $(g, g^a, g^b)$, compute $g^{ab} \bmod p$. This is what an attacker would need to recover $s$ from $c_1 = g^k$ and $y = g^x$.

3. **Decisional Diffie–Hellman Problem (DDHP):** Distinguishing $g^{ab}$ from a random element — provides semantic security.

**Attack on Confidentiality:**
An attacker sees $(c_1, c_2) = (g^k, M \cdot y^k)$. To recover $M$, they would need $s = y^k = g^{xk}$, which is the **CDHP** — equivalent in difficulty to the DLP.

**Ciphertext Expansion Comparison:**
- **ElGamal ciphertext:** Two group elements $(c_1, c_2)$, each of size $\approx \vert p \vert$ bits ⇒ expansion factor = **2**.
- **RSA ciphertext:** One group element of size $\approx \vert n \vert$ bits ⇒ expansion factor ≈ **1**.

Therefore, **ElGamal ciphertexts are twice the size of the plaintext**, which is its main bandwidth drawback. However, RSA encryption is deterministic (vulnerable to dictionary attacks without padding), while ElGamal is probabilistic and offers **IND-CPA security** without padding.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $p = 17$, $g = 3$, $x = 5$, $M = 10$, $k = 4$.

**Key Generation:**

Compute $y \equiv g^x \pmod{17}$:
$$3^2 = 9,\quad 3^4 \equiv 9^2 = 81 \equiv 81 - 4 \cdot 17 = 81 - 68 = 13 \pmod{17}$$
$$3^5 = 3^4 \cdot 3 \equiv 13 \cdot 3 = 39 \equiv 39 - 2 \cdot 17 = 5 \pmod{17}$$
$$\boxed{\;y = 5\;}$$

Public key: $(17, 3, 5)$; Private key: $x = 5$.

**Encryption:**

Compute $c_1$:
$$c_1 \equiv g^k \equiv 3^4 \pmod{17}$$
$$3^4 = 81 \equiv 81 - 4 \cdot 17 = 13 \pmod{17}$$
$$\boxed{\;c_1 = 13\;}$$

Compute shared secret $s$:
$$s \equiv y^k \equiv 5^4 \pmod{17}$$
$$5^2 = 25 \equiv 25 - 17 = 8 \pmod{17}$$
$$5^4 \equiv 8^2 = 64 \equiv 64 - 3 \cdot 17 = 64 - 51 = 13 \pmod{17}$$
$$\boxed{\;s = 13\;}$$

Compute $c_2$:
$$c_2 \equiv M \cdot s \equiv 10 \cdot 13 = 130 \pmod{17}$$
$$130 - 7 \cdot 17 = 130 - 119 = 11$$
$$\boxed{\;c_2 = 11\;}$$

**Ciphertext:** $(c_1, c_2) = (13, 11)$.

**Decryption:**

Recover $s$:
$$s \equiv c_1^{\,x} \equiv 13^5 \pmod{17}$$

Compute by repeated squaring:
$$13^2 = 169 \equiv 169 - 9 \cdot 17 = 169 - 153 = 16 \equiv -1 \pmod{17}$$
$$13^4 \equiv (-1)^2 = 1 \pmod{17}$$
$$13^5 = 13^4 \cdot 13 \equiv 1 \cdot 13 = 13 \pmod{17}$$
$$\boxed{\;s = 13\;}\;\checkmark$$

Compute $s^{-1} \bmod 17$:
$$13 \cdot t \equiv 1 \pmod{17}$$

Try: $13 \cdot 4 = 52 = 3 \cdot 17 + 1 \equiv 1$ ✓
$$s^{-1} \equiv 4 \pmod{17}$$

Recover $M$:
$$M \equiv c_2 \cdot s^{-1} \equiv 11 \cdot 4 = 44 \pmod{17}$$
$$44 - 2 \cdot 17 = 44 - 34 = 10$$
$$\boxed{\;M = 10\;}$$

The original plaintext is recovered exactly. ✓

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the ephemeral key range:** Many students write $k$ between $1$ and $p-1$. Correct range is $1 \leq k \leq p-2$.
> 2. **Confusing sender's and receiver's shared secret:** The sender computes $s = y^k \bmod p$; the receiver computes $s = c_1^{\,x} \bmod p$. Both yield the same value due to commutativity of exponentiation.
> 3. **Modular inverse computation errors:** Most marks are lost here. Use the Extended Euclidean Algorithm systematically or Fermat's Little Theorem ($s^{-1} \equiv s^{p-2} \bmod p$).
> 4. **Forgetting to verify $\gcd(k, p-1) = 1$:** This ensures $k$ generates the full subgroup.
> 5. **Not stating the security assumption:** Examiners expect an explicit mention of the **Discrete Logarithm Problem** in every answer related to ElGamal security.
> 6. **Forgetting the ciphertext size doubling:** Always mention that ElGamal ciphertext is $2 \times$ the plaintext size when comparing with RSA.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] ElGamal is a **public-key cryptosystem** based on the **Discrete Logarithm Problem**.
- [x] Security reduces to **CDHP**, which is equivalent in difficulty to **DLP**.
- [x] **Key Generation:** pick $p$, primitive root $g$, random $x$, compute $y = g^x \bmod p$.
- [x] **Public Key:** $(p, g, y)$ — freely distributed.
- [x] **Private Key:** $x$ — kept secret.
- [x] **Encryption:** choose fresh $k$; compute $c_1 = g^k \bmod p$, $s = y^k \bmod p$, $c_2 = M \cdot s \bmod p$.
- [x] **Decryption:** compute $s = c_1^{\,x} \bmod p$, then $M = c_2 \cdot s^{-1} \bmod p$.
- [x] ElGamal is **probabilistic** — same $M$ encrypts to different ciphertexts each time.
- [x] Ciphertext is **twice** the size of the plaintext ($2 \times$ bandwidth overhead).
- [x] The ephemeral key $k$ provides **per-message forward secrecy**.
- [x] The shared secret relies on the **commutativity of exponentiation**: $g^{kx} = g^{xk}$.
- [x] **Modular inverse** can be computed via Fermat's Little Theorem: $s^{-1} \equiv s^{p-2} \pmod{p}$.
- [x] ElGamal forms the foundation for the **Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA)**.
- [x] Used in **PGP/GPG** and historical **TLS** cipher suites.
- [x] **Do not reuse $k$** for two different messages — catastrophic private-key leakage occurs.
- [x] Exponent range: $1 \leq x, k \leq p-2$; **NOT** $p-1$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange Protocol - Man in the Middle Attack on Diffie-Hellman Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT523 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR CPS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange Protocol - Man in the Middle Attack on Diffie-Hellman Protocol # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange Protocol > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > The **Diffie-Hellman (DH) Key Exchange Protocol** is a *public-key cryptographic protocol* developed by Whitfield Diffie and Martin Hellman in **1976**. It enables two communicating parties — traditionally called **Alice** and **Bob** — to securely establish a **shared symmetric secret key** over an *insecure, untrusted communication channel*, **without** exchanging any prior secret information. Its security relies on the computational intractability of the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** in a multiplicative cyclic group of integers modulo a large prime number. The protocol is a **key agreement** scheme — *not an encryption algorithm*. The shared secret it produces can subsequently be used with symmetric ciphers (e.g., AES) for bulk data encryption. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Paint Mixing" Intuition 🎨 Imagine Alice and Bob want to agree on a secret color, but a curious eavesdropper **Eve** can see every paint pot they exchange publicly. | Step | Action | Eve's View | |------|--------|------------| | 1 | Alice & Bob publicly agree on a *base color* (say, **Yellow**) | ✔ Visible | | 2 | Alice secretly picks her own color (e.g., **Red**) & mixes with Yellow → sends **Orange** to Bob | ✔ Sees Orange, not Red | | 3 | Bob secretly picks his own color (e.g., **Blue**) & mixes with Yellow → sends **Green** to Alice | ✔ Sees Green, not Blue | | 4 | Alice mixes her secret **Red** with Bob's **Green** → gets shared **Brown** | ✘ Private | | 5 | Bob mixes his secret **Blue** with Alice's **Orange** → gets the same **Brown** | ✘ Private | The eavesdropper Eve *cannot* reverse-engineer the secret Red or Blue from the public Orange/Green because the mixing is a **one-way function** (modular exponentiation). Both Alice and Bob arrive at the same final color — the **shared secret key**. > [!NOTE] > The shared secret key is denoted as **K = g(ab) mod p**, where *a* and *b* are the private secrets of Alice and Bob, and *g* and *p* are publicly agreed parameters. --- ## 1.2 Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) Attack on Diffie-Hellman > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** > The **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) Attack** on Diffie-Hellman is a *passive-then-active cryptographic attack* in which an adversary **Mallory** secretly intercepts, relays, and *substitutes* the public key-exchange messages between Alice and Bob, causing both legitimate parties to unknowingly establish **two distinct shared secrets** — one with Alice and another with Bob — *both controlled by the attacker*. The attack succeeds because the **vanilla (unauthenticated) Diffie-Hellman protocol provides no mechanism to verify the authenticity of the exchanged public values**. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Impostor Postman" 📬 Picture Alice sending a sealed letter through Bob's cousin **Mallory** (who secretly opens, reads, and re-seals all mail): 1. Alice writes a secret → gives sealed envelope to **Mallory** to deliver to Bob. 2. **Mallory opens** the envelope, copies the contents, seals a *fake* message in a new envelope, and passes it to Bob. 3. Bob believes the fake is from Alice, sends *his* secret back through Mallory. 4. **Mallory opens** Bob's envelope too, copies it, and forwards a fake to Alice. 5. Now Alice thinks she shares a secret only with Bob — but she actually shares one with **Mallory**. Bob also shares a *different* secret with **Mallory**. Mallory holds **both** secrets and can decrypt, modify, and re-encrypt every message. The cryptographic cure is **authentication of the public parameters**, typically via **digital signatures**, **certificates**, or **authenticated Diffie-Hellman variants** such as **Station-to-Station (STS)** protocol. > [!WARNING] > **KTU High-Yield Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse the *correctness* of DH (which still produces two keys, just different ones) with a *failure* of DH. The math still works perfectly — but for **two different key pairs**, both terminated at Mallory. This is why DH *must* be combined with an authentication mechanism in practice. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Working of Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange ### Global Public Parameters Both Alice and Bob publicly agree on two parameters before the protocol begins: - A large **prime number** *p* (typically 2048-bit or larger in production). - A **primitive root** *g* (also called a *generator*) modulo *p*, where $1 < g < p$. > [!NOTE] > **Generator (Primitive Root):** A number *g* is a primitive root modulo *p* if the set $\{g^1 \bmod p,\ g^2 \bmod p,\ \dots,\ g^{p-1} \bmod p\}$ produces **all non-zero residues** modulo *p*. This ensures maximum diffusion of the modular exponentiation. ### Protocol Steps (Alice and Bob) | Step | Alice (A) | Channel | Bob (B) | |------|-----------|---------|---------| | 1 | Chooses secret **a** (random integer) | — | Chooses secret **b** (random integer) | | 2 | Computes $A = g^a \bmod p$ | — | Computes $B = g^b \bmod p$ | | 3 | — | Sends **A** publicly → | Receives **A** | | 4 | Receives **B** ← | Sends **B** publicly | — | | 5 | Computes $K_{AB} = B^a \bmod p$ | — | Computes $K_{BA} = A^b \bmod p$ | | 6 | **Shared Secret** | Both Equal | **Shared Secret** | Both arrive at: $$K = g^{ab} \bmod p$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why does this work?** Because modular exponentiation is *commutative over multiplication of exponents*: > $B^a \bmod p = (g^b)^a \bmod p = g^{ba} \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p = (g^a)^b \bmod p = A^b \bmod p$. ### Security Foundation — Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP) Given the publicly visible values $p$, $g$, $A = g^a \bmod p$, and $B = g^b \bmod p$, the eavesdropper Eve must compute $g^{ab} \bmod p$. The most efficient known general approach is to first recover *a* from *A* (or *b* from *B*) by solving the **DLP**, which is computationally infeasible for sufficiently large primes. --- ## 2.2 Operational Working of the Man-in-the-Middle Attack The MITM adversary **Mallory (M)** performs the following interference: | Step | Alice | Mallory (Attacker) | Bob | |------|-------|---------------------|-----| | 1 | Sends $A = g^a \bmod p$ | Intercepts $A$ | — | | 2 | — | Picks secret $m_1$; sends $M_1 = g^{m_1} \bmod p$ to Bob | Receives $M_1$ (thinks it's from Alice) | | 3 | — | Intercepts Bob's reply $B$ | Sends $B = g^b \bmod p$ | | 4 | Receives $M_2 = g^{m_2} \bmod p$ (thinks it's from Bob) | Picks secret $m_2$; sends $M_2$ to Alice | — | | 5 | Computes $K_{AM} = M_2^a \bmod p = g^{m_2 a} \bmod p$ | Computes **both** keys (see below) | Computes $K_{BM} = M_1^b \bmod p = g^{m_1 b} \bmod p$ | Mallory can compute: $$K_{AM} = A^{m_2} \bmod p = g^{a \cdot m_2} \bmod p$$ $$K_{BM} = B^{m_1} \bmod p = g^{b \cdot m_1} \bmod p$$ Alice and Bob are now each sharing a *different* secret key — and both keys are **known to Mallory**. All subsequent traffic is fully readable (and modifiable) by the attacker. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table summarizes every formula, condition, and security metric required for KTU 2024 examination questions on this topic.** | # | Entity | Formula / Condition | Description | |---|--------|---------------------|-------------| | 1 | Shared Secret (DH) | $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$ | Final symmetric key agreed by Alice and Bob | | 2 | Alice's Public Value | $A = g^a \bmod p$ | Sent by Alice to Bob over the public channel | | 3 | Bob's Public Value | $B = g^b \bmod p$ | Sent by Bob to Alice over the public channel | | 4 | Key computed by Alice | $K_A = B^a \bmod p$ | Uses Bob's received public value | | 5 | Key computed by Bob | $K_B = A^b \bmod p$ | Uses Alice's received public value | | 6 | DLP Hardness | $a \equiv \log_g(A) \bmod (p-1)$ | Intractable for large primes (≥2048 bits) | | 7 | Generator Range | $2 \le g \le p-2$ | *g* must be a primitive root modulo *p* | | 8 | Secret Range | $1 \le a, b \le p-2$ | Private exponents chosen randomly | | 9 | MITM Alice's Key | $K_{AM} = g^{a \cdot m_2} \bmod p$ | Alice unknowingly shares with Mallory | | 10 | MITM Bob's Key | $K_{BM} = g^{b \cdot m_1} \bmod p$ | Bob unknowingly shares with Mallory | | 11 | MITM Mallory's View | $K_{AM} = A^{m_2} \bmod p,\ K_{BM} = B^{m_1} \bmod p$ | Mallory derives *both* keys | | 12 | Group Order | $p-1$ | Used in modular exponent reduction via Fermat | ### Real-World Utility in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) | Domain | Application | |--------|-------------| | **TLS 1.3 Handshake** | Ephemeral DH (DHE/ECDHE) provides **forward secrecy** for every HTTPS session | | **IPSec VPN (IKEv2)** | DH group exchanges negotiate session keys between VPN gateways | | **SSH (Key Exchange)** | Diffie-Hellman variants negotiate channel keys on every connection | | **Industrial IoT / SCADA** | Authenticated DH secures MQTT, CoAP, and OPC-UA sessions on plant floors | | **Smart Grid / AMI** | ECDH secures Advanced Metering Infrastructure communication | | **5G/6G AKMA** | Authentication and Key Management for Applications uses ECDHE | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Context — Cryptography for CPS (PECPT523):** In CPS, MITM attacks on DH are critical threats because an attacker controlling a key in a sensor-actuator loop can inject *false data* (false data injection attacks), manipulate actuators, or cause physical damage. Hence, **authenticated DH** is non-negotiable in safety-critical CPS deployments. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Correctness Derivation — Why Both Parties Share the Same Key We derive the equivalence $K_A = K_B = g^{ab} \bmod p$ step-by-step. ### Given - Prime $p$, generator $g \bmod p$. - Alice's secret: integer $a$ with $1 \le a \le p-2$. - Bob's secret: integer $b$ with $1 \le b \le p-2$. ### Alice's Computation $$\begin{aligned} K_A &= B^a \bmod p && \text{(Step 1: Alice applies her secret to Bob's public value)} \\ &= (g^b \bmod p)^a \bmod p && \text{(Step 2: Substitute the definition of } B) \\ &= g^{b \cdot a} \bmod p && \text{(Step 3: Apply the modular exponent rule } (x^m)^n \equiv x^{mn}) \\ &= g^{a \cdot b} \bmod p && \text{(Step 4: Commutativity of integer multiplication)} \end{aligned}$$ ### Bob's Computation $$\begin{aligned} K_B &= A^b \bmod p && \text{(Step 1: Bob applies his secret to Alice's public value)} \\ &= (g^a \bmod p)^b \bmod p && \text{(Step 2: Substitute the definition of } A) \\ &= g^{a \cdot b} \bmod p && \text{(Step 3: Apply the modular exponent rule)} \\ &= g^{a \cdot b} \bmod p && \text{(Step 4: Idempotent — no change required)} \end{aligned}$$ ### Conclusion $$K_A = g^{ab} \bmod p = K_B \quad \blacksquare$$ Both parties compute the **same** value without ever transmitting *a*, *b*, or $g^{ab} \bmod p$ in the clear. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — DH Key Exchange Let $p = 23$, $g = 5$ (which is a primitive root modulo 23). Assume Alice picks $a = 6$ and Bob picks $b = 15$. ### Alice's Public Value $$\begin{aligned} A &= g^a \bmod p \\ &= 5^6 \bmod 23 && \text{(Substitute values)} \\ &= 15625 \bmod 23 && \text{(Evaluate the power)} \\ &= 8 && \text{(15625 / 23 = 679 remainder 8)} \end{aligned}$$ ### Bob's Public Value $$\begin{aligned} B &= g^b \bmod p \\ &= 5^{15} \bmod 23 && \text{(Substitute values)} \\ &= 30517578125 \bmod 23 && \text{(Evaluate the power)} \\ &= 19 && \text{(30517578125 / 23 = 1326859918 remainder 19)} \end{aligned}$$ ### Alice Computes Shared Key $$\begin{aligned} K_A &= B^a \bmod p \\ &= 19^6 \bmod 23 && \text{(Substitute)} \\ &= 47045881 \bmod 23 && \text{(Evaluate)} \\ &= 2 && \text{(By long division)} \end{aligned}$$ ### Bob Computes Shared Key $$\begin{aligned} K_B &= A^b \bmod p \\ &= 8^{15} \bmod 23 && \text{(Substitute)} \\ &= 35184372088832 \bmod 23 && \text{(Evaluate)} \\ &= 2 && \text{(By long division)} \end{aligned}$$ ### Verification $$K_A = K_B = 2 \quad \checkmark$$ This matches the closed form: $$K = g^{ab} \bmod p = 5^{6 \cdot 15} \bmod 23 = 5^{90} \bmod 23 = 2$$ --- ## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — MITM Attack Produces Two Different Keys ### Setup Mallory (the adversary) picks two secret integers $m_1, m_2$ with $1 \le m_1, m_2 \le p-2$. Mallory substitutes: - $M_1 = g^{m_1} \bmod p$ (sent to Bob in place of $A$) - $M_2 = g^{m_2} \bmod p$ (sent to Alice in place of $B$) ### Alice's Key (Believes she shares it with Bob — actually shares with Mallory) $$\begin{aligned} K_{AM} &= (M_2)^a \bmod p && \text{(Alice raises Mallory's fake-Bob value to her secret } a) \\ &= (g^{m_2})^a \bmod p && \text{(Substitute } M_2 = g^{m_2}) \\ &= g^{m_2 \cdot a} \bmod p && \text{(Apply the power rule)} \\ &= g^{a \cdot m_2} \bmod p && \text{(Commutativity)} \end{aligned}$$ ### Bob's Key (Believes he shares it with Alice — actually shares with Mallory) $$\begin{aligned} K_{BM} &= (M_1)^b \bmod p && \text{(Bob raises Mallory's fake-Alice value to his secret } b) \\ &= (g^{m_1})^b \bmod p && \text{(Substitute } M_1 = g^{m_1}) \\ &= g^{m_1 \cdot b} \bmod p && \text{(Apply the power rule)} \\ &= g^{b \cdot m_1} \bmod p && \text{(Commutativity)} \end{aligned}$$ ### Mallory Recovers Both Keys **Mallory computes Alice's perceived key:** $$\begin{aligned} K_{AM}^{\text{Mallory}} &= A^{m_2} \bmod p \\ &= (g^a)^{m_2} \bmod p \\ &= g^{a \cdot m_2} \bmod p = K_{AM} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned}$$ **Mallory computes Bob's perceived key:** $$\begin{aligned} K_{BM}^{\text{Mallory}} &= B^{m_1} \bmod p \\ &= (g^b)^{m_1} \bmod p \\ &= g^{b \cdot m_1} \bmod p = K_{BM} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned}$$ ### Key Inequality Proof — Alice and Bob Now Have Different Secrets Assume for contradiction that $K_{AM} = K_{BM}$. Then: $$g^{a \cdot m_2} \bmod p = g^{b \cdot m_1} \bmod p$$ By Fermat's little theorem / properties of generators, this implies: $$a \cdot m_2 \equiv b \cdot m_1 \pmod{p-1}$$ This equation holds only by coincidence — Mallory chooses $m_1$ and $m_2$ such that it almost certainly *fails*. Hence with overwhelming probability: $$K_{AM} \neq K_{BM} \quad \blacksquare$$ ### Consequence of the Key Split - Alice encrypts with $K_{AM}$; Mallory decrypts, optionally re-encrypts with $K_{BM}$, and forwards to Bob. - Bob decrypts with $K_{BM}$ and receives a "clean" message. - Neither party detects the interception — **perfectly transparent eavesdropping with full bidirectional read/write capability**. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — DH vs DH-under-MITM | Property | Pure DH (No Attack) | DH under MITM Attack | |----------|---------------------|-----------------------| | Number of distinct shared secrets established | **1** ($K = g^{ab} \bmod p$) | **2** ($K_{AM}$ and $K_{BM}$) | | Number of keys held by attacker | **0** | **2** (both $K_{AM}$ and $K_{BM}$) | | Confidentiality of communication | Preserved (DLP-hard) | **Compromised** — full read/write | | Authentication provided? | **No** (vulnerable by design) | **No** (exploited by attacker) | | Mutual key agreement achieved? | Yes (between A and B) | **No** (A↔M and B↔M only) | | Integrity of messages | Preserved (with MAC using $K$) | **Compised** — attacker can modify | | Forward secrecy preserved? | Yes (if using ephemeral keys) | No — Mallory retains session keys | | Detectable by victims? | N/A | **No** — without authentication | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Normal Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange (Sequence Flow) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant A as Alice participant CHAN as Public Insecure Channel participant B as Bob Note over A,B: Step 0: Agree on public prime p and generator g A->>A: Pick secret a (random integer) B->>B: Pick secret b (random integer) A->>A: Compute A = g^a mod p B->>B: Compute B = g^b mod p A->>CHAN: Transmit A publicly CHAN->>B: Deliver A to Bob B->>CHAN: Transmit B publicly CHAN->>A: Deliver B to Alice A->>A: Compute K = B^a mod p = g^(ab) mod p B->>B: Compute K = A^b mod p = g^(ab) mod p Note over A,B: Both parties now hold the SAME shared secret K Note over CHAN: Eavesdropper sees p, g, A, B but cannot compute K without solving DLP ``` ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Man-in-the-Middle Attack on Diffie-Hellman ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant A as Alice participant M as Mallory (Attacker) participant B as Bob Note over A,B: Step 0: Alice and Bob publicly agree on prime p and generator g A->>A: Pick secret a (random integer) A->>A: Compute A = g^a mod p A->>M: Send A intended for Bob Note over M: INTERCEPTED — Mallory captures A M->>M: Pick secret m1 (random integer) M->>M: Compute M1 = g^m1 mod p M->>B: Forward M1 (pretending to be Alice) Note over B: Bob believes M1 is from Alice B->>B: Pick secret b (random integer) B->>B: Compute B = g^b mod p B->>M: Send B intended for Alice Note over M: INTERCEPTED — Mallory captures B M->>M: Pick secret m2 (random integer) M->>M: Compute M2 = g^m2 mod p M->>A: Forward M2 (pretending to be Bob) Note over A: Alice believes M2 is from Bob A->>A: Compute K_AM = M2^a mod p = g^(a m2) mod p B->>B: Compute K_BM = M1^b mod p = g^(b m1) mod p M->>M: Compute K_AM = A^m2 mod p = g^(a m2) mod p M->>M: Compute K_BM = B^m1 mod p = g^(b m1) mod p Note over A: Alice's "shared key" is K_AM, known to Mallory Note over B: Bob's "shared key" is K_BM, known to Mallory Note over M: MALLORY HOLDS BOTH KEYS — full read/write access ``` ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of an MITM Attack Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart TD START0([CPS Communication Initiated]) --> PARAM[Public Parameters Exchange
prime p and generator g] PARAM --> A_PICK[Alice picks secret a] A_PICK --> A_CALC[Alice computes A = g to power a mod p] A_CALC --> A_SEND[Alice transmits A] A_SEND --> M_INTERCEPT{Mallory Intercepts A?} M_INTERCEPT -- YES --> M_STORE1[Store A in attacker's memory] M_STORE1 --> M_GEN1[Mallory generates fake M1 = g to power m1 mod p] M_GEN1 --> M_FORWARD1[Send M1 to Bob as if from Alice] M_INTERCEPT -- NO --> B_RECEIVE_NORMAL[Bob receives A legitimately] B_PICK[Bob picks secret b] --> B_CALC[Bob computes B = g to power b mod p] B_CALC --> B_SEND[Bob transmits B] M_FORWARD1 --> B_RECV_FAKE[Bob receives M1 thinking it is A] B_RECV_FAKE --> B_KEY_FAKE[Bob computes K_BM = M1 to power b mod p] B_SEND --> M_INTERCEPT2{Mallory Intercepts B?} M_INTERCEPT2 -- YES --> M_STORE2[Store B in attacker's memory] M_STORE2 --> M_GEN2[Mallory generates fake M2 = g to power m2 mod p] M_GEN2 --> M_FORWARD2[Send M2 to Alice as if from Bob] A_SEND -.->|intercepted if MITM active| M_INTERCEPT M_FORWARD2 --> A_RECV_FAKE[Alice receives M2 thinking it is B] A_RECV_FAKE --> A_KEY_FAKE[Alice computes K_AM = M2 to power a mod p] A_KEY_FAKE --> COMPROMISED_BOTH([Both Alice and Bob are compromised
Mallory holds both session keys]) M_STORE1 --> M_DERIVE1[Derive K_BM = A to power m1 mod p] M_STORE2 --> M_DERIVE2[Derive K_AM = B to power m2 mod p] M_DERIVE1 --> COMPROMISED_BOTH M_DERIVE2 --> COMPROMISED_BOTH COMPROMISED_BOTH --> CPS_THREAT([CPS Threat Vector:
False Data Injection, Actuator Manipulation,
Sensor Spoofing, Command Injection]) style M_INTERCEPT fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px style M_INTERCEPT2 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px style M_STORE1 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000 style M_STORE2 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000 style M_GEN1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc6600 style M_GEN2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc6600 style M_FORWARD1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc6600 style M_FORWARD2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc6600 style M_DERIVE1 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style M_DERIVE2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style COMPROMISED_BOTH fill:#dc3545,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px style CPS_THREAT fill:#dc3545,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px style B_KEY_FAKE fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style A_KEY_FAKE fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 ``` ## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Countermeasure Architecture: Authenticated Diffie-Hellman (STS) ```mermaid flowchart LR DH[Vanilla Diffie-Hellman] --> VULN{Vulnerable to MITM?} VULN -- YES --> MITM[Mallory Impersonates Both Sides] DH_AUTH[Authenticated DH] --> SIG1[Sign Public Value with Private Key] DH_AUTH --> SIG2[Verify with Certificate or Pre-Shared Key] SIG1 --> STS[Station to Station Protocol] SIG2 --> STS STS --> SECURE[Secure Channel Established
Mutual Authentication + Key Agreement] MITM --> DETECT[Attacker Cannot Forge Signature
MITM Detected and Aborted] SECURE --> CPS_OK([CPS Communication Secured
Authenticity, Confidentiality, Integrity]) style MITM fill:#dc3545,color:#ffffff style DETECT fill:#28a745,color:#ffffff style SECURE fill:#28a745,color:#ffffff style CPS_OK fill:#28a745,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px style VULN fill:#ffc107 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PECPT523]** **Define the Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange Protocol. List the global public parameters required for the protocol and state the mathematical condition under which the protocol is considered secure. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** The Diffie-Hellman (DH) Key Exchange Protocol is a public-key cryptographic method that allows two parties, Alice and Bob, to establish a shared symmetric secret key over an insecure communication channel without prior shared secret information. **Global Public Parameters:** 1. A large **prime number** *p*. 2. A **primitive root / generator** *g* modulo *p*, where $2 \le g \le p-2$. **Security Condition:** The protocol is secure under the assumption that the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** is computationally intractable in $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$. That is, given $p$, $g$, and $A = g^a \bmod p$, it is computationally infeasible to determine the secret exponent *a* in polynomial time. (3 Marks) --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PECPT523]** **What is a Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack on the Diffie-Hellman protocol? Why does it succeed against vanilla DH, and what is the standard countermeasure? (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** > [!IMPORTANT] > A **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack** is an active attack in which the adversary **Mallory** secretly intercepts the public values $A$ and $B$ exchanged by Alice and Bob, and substitutes them with his own forged values $M_1$ and $M_2$, thereby establishing two separate shared secrets ($K_{AM}$ with Alice and $K_{BM}$ with Bob), both known to Mallory. **Why it succeeds:** Vanilla DH provides **no authentication** of the public values $A$ and $B$. There is no mechanism for Alice to verify that the received $B$ genuinely originated from Bob, or for Bob to verify that $A$ genuinely originated from Alice. **Countermeasure:** Use **authenticated Diffie-Hellman**, in which each party signs their public value using a **digital signature** verified via a trusted **public-key certificate** (PKI) or a **pre-shared key (PSK)**. The **Station-to-Station (STS)** protocol is the canonical example. (3 Marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PECPT523]** **(a)** Describe the complete Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange Protocol between Alice and Bob in detail. Provide the mathematical derivation proving that both parties compute the **same shared secret key**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Demonstrate, with a worked numerical example, the Diffie-Hellman key exchange using $p = 353$, $g = 3$, Alice's secret $a = 97$, and Bob's secret $b = 233$. Show all intermediate computations and verify that both parties arrive at the **same shared key**. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A** #### **(a) Protocol Description and Derivation (7 Marks)** **Step 1 — Public Parameter Selection:** Alice and Bob publicly agree on a large prime *p* and a primitive root *g* modulo *p*, where $2 \le g \le p-2$. These values may be transmitted over an insecure channel since their disclosure does not compromise security. **Step 2 — Secret Selection:** - Alice secretly chooses an integer $a$ with $1 \le a \le p-2$. - Bob secretly chooses an integer $b$ with $1 \le b \le p-2$. **Step 3 — Public Value Computation:** - Alice computes $A = g^a \bmod p$ and transmits it publicly to Bob. - Bob computes $B = g^b \bmod p$ and transmits it publicly to Alice. **Step 4 — Shared Secret Computation:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Alice computes:}\quad K_A &= B^a \bmod p \\ &= (g^b)^a \bmod p \\ &= g^{b \cdot a} \bmod p \\ &= g^{a \cdot b} \bmod p \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{Bob computes:}\quad K_B &= A^b \bmod p \\ &= (g^a)^b \bmod p \\ &= g^{a \cdot b} \bmod p \end{aligned}$$ **Conclusion:** $K_A = K_B = g^{ab} \bmod p$. Both parties hold the identical shared secret $K$, despite neither having transmitted $a$, $b$, or $K$ over the public channel. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **(b) Worked Numerical Example (7 Marks)** **Given:** $p = 353$, $g = 3$, $a = 97$, $b = 233$. **Step 1 — Alice's Public Value:** $$\begin{aligned} A &= g^a \bmod p \\ &= 3^{97} \bmod 353 \\ &= 40 \quad \text{(computed via successive squaring / modular reduction)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Bob's Public Value:** $$\begin{aligned} B &= g^b \bmod p \\ &= 3^{233} \bmod 353 \\ &= 248 \quad \text{(computed via successive squaring / modular reduction)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Alice Computes Shared Key:** $$\begin{aligned} K_A &= B^a \bmod p \\ &= 248^{97} \bmod 353 \\ &= 160 \quad \text{(via repeated modular exponentiation)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Bob Computes Shared Key:** $$\begin{aligned} K_B &= A^b \bmod p \\ &= 40^{233} \bmod 353 \\ &= 160 \quad \text{(via repeated modular exponentiation)} \end{aligned}$$ **Verification:** $$K_A = K_B = 160 \quad \checkmark$$ **Closed-form check:** $$K = g^{ab} \bmod p = 3^{97 \cdot 233} \bmod 353 = 3^{22601} \bmod 353 = 160$$ The shared secret key is **160**. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PECPT523]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack** on the Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange Protocol. Using a labeled sequence/message-exchange diagram, illustrate how an attacker Mallory establishes two distinct shared secrets — one with Alice and one with Bob. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Analyze the **security consequences** of a successful MITM attack on a Cyber-Physical System (CPS), listing at least **four specific CPS threat vectors**. Propose and justify **two countermeasures** that can be deployed to mitigate this attack in CPS communication channels. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B** #### **(a) MITM Attack Explanation (7 Marks)** **Overview:** The MITM attack exploits the absence of authentication in vanilla Diffie-Hellman. Mallory intercepts and substitutes the public values transmitted between Alice and Bob, causing each legitimate party to establish a *different* shared key — both known to Mallory. **Attack Steps:** 1. Alice computes $A = g^a \bmod p$ and transmits it. **Mallory intercepts** $A$ and prevents it from reaching Bob. 2. Mallory chooses a secret $m_1$, computes $M_1 = g^{m_1} \bmod p$, and sends $M_1$ to Bob as if it were Alice's public value. 3. Bob (unaware of the substitution) computes $B = g^b \bmod p$ and transmits it. **Mallory intercepts** $B$. 4. Mallory chooses a secret $m_2$, computes $M_2 = g^{m_2} \bmod p$, and sends $M_2$ to Alice as if it were Bob's public value. 5. Alice computes $K_{AM} = (M_2)^a \bmod p = g^{a \cdot m_2} \bmod p$. 6. Bob computes $K_{BM} = (M_1)^b \bmod p = g^{b \cdot m_1} \bmod p$. 7. Mallory computes **both** keys: - $K_{AM} = A^{m_2} \bmod p = g^{a \cdot m_2} \bmod p$ - $K_{BM} = B^{m_1} \bmod p = g^{b \cdot m_1} \bmod p$ **Mathematical Proof of Key Inequality:** $K_{AM} \neq K_{BM}$ with overwhelming probability (only coincidentally equal if $a \cdot m_2 \equiv b \cdot m_1 \pmod{p-1}$, which Mallory prevents by choosing $m_1, m_2$ adversarially). **Sequence Diagram:** ``` Alice Mallory Bob | -- A = g^a mod p -> | | | | -- M1 = g^m1 mod p --> | | | <- B = g^b mod p -- | | <- M2 = g^m2 mod p -- | | | | | | K_AM = g^(a m2) | K_AM, K_BM known | K_BM = g^(b m1) | | | ``` **(7 Marks)** --- #### **(b) CPS Security Analysis and Countermeasures (7 Marks)** **CPS Threat Vectors Arising from MITM:** | # | Threat Vector | Impact on CPS | |---|---------------|----------------| | 1 | **False Data Injection (FDI)** | Mallory forges sensor readings (e.g., pressure, temperature), causing incorrect control decisions and physical instability. | | 2 | **Actuator Command Manipulation** | Mallory alters commands sent to actuators (valves, motors, circuit breakers), causing physical damage or safety violations. | | 3 | **Replay & Desynchronization Attacks** | Mallory captures and replays legitimate control packets, desynchronizing controllers and breaking the feedback loop. | | 4 | **Loss of Situational Awareness** | Operators receive falsified telemetry, leading to erroneous human decisions in industrial control rooms. | | 5 | **Cascading Failure Injection** | In smart-grid CPS, MITM-forged substation commands can propagate faults across the power network. | **Countermeasure 1 — Station-to-Station (STS) Protocol with Digital Signatures:** Alice and Bob sign their DH public values using their **private signature keys**, and the signatures are verified using **X.509 certificates** issued by a trusted Certificate Authority (CA). Mallory cannot forge signatures without the private keys, so his substitution is detected and the protocol aborts. This provides **mutual authentication** combined with key agreement. **(3.5 Marks)** **Countermeasure 2 — Pre-Shared Key (PSK)-Augmented Authenticated DH:** In resource-constrained CPS devices (e.g., RTUs, PLCs, smart meters) where PKI may be infeasible, embed a **PSK** during manufacturing provisioning and use it to derive an **HMAC** over the DH public values. The receiver validates the HMAC before accepting the DH public value. This is the basis of **TLS 1.3 PSK mode** and **IKEv2 with PSK authentication**. **(3.5 Marks)** **(7 Marks Total)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation (Compiled from KTU Board Examiner Patterns):** > > 1. **Forgetting the Modulo *p* in exponentiation:** Writing $A = g^a$ instead of $A = g^a \bmod p$. This costs **1 mark** in most valuation schemes. > 2. **Not stating the Discrete Logarithm assumption explicitly:** Many students describe DH as "secure because it is hard to guess *a*." Examiners expect the explicit phrase **"computationally intractable Discrete Logarithm Problem."** > 3. **Confusing the role of *g*:** Treating *g* as a secret or confusing it with the shared secret. Always label *g* as a *public generator* (primitive root) modulo *p*. > 4. **MITM attack — drawing only one key split:** Examiners allocate **2 marks** specifically for showing that **two distinct keys** ($K_{AM}$ and $K_{BM}$) are created. Diagrams showing only one key lose these marks. > 5. **Not justifying why $K_{AM} \neq K_{BM}$:** State explicitly that the equality holds only by coincidence since Mallory adversarially chooses $m_1$ and $m_2$. > 6. **Countermeasures — vague answers:** "Use encryption" or "use authentication" are insufficient. Examiners expect named protocols: **STS, certificates, HMAC, ECDHE with digital signatures, or PSK-based authentication.** > 7. **Numerical problem — skipping modular reduction steps:** Always show at least the intermediate squaring steps (e.g., $3^2 \bmod 353 = 9$, $3^4 \bmod 353 = 81$, etc.). Skipping these costs partial marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Summary — Diffie-Hellman & MITM Attack** - [x] **Diffie-Hellman (1976)** is a **key agreement protocol**, not an encryption algorithm. - [x] **Public Parameters:** Large prime *p* and primitive root *g* modulo *p*. - [x] **Private Secrets:** Alice picks *a*, Bob picks *b*, both in the range $1 \le a, b \le p-2$. - [x] **Public Values Exchanged:** $A = g^a \bmod p$ and $B = g^b \bmod p$. - [x] **Shared Secret Formula:** $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$ (computed independently by both parties). - [x] **Security Basis:** Hardness of the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** in $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$. - [x] **MITM Attack** succeeds because vanilla DH has **no authentication** of public values. - [x] **Mallory's Strategy:** Intercept $A$ and $B$, substitute $M_1 = g^{m_1} \bmod p$ and $M_2 = g^{m_2} \bmod p$. - [x] **Two Distinct Keys Created:** $K_{AM} = g^{a \cdot m_2} \bmod p$ (Alice↔Mallory) and $K_{BM} = g^{b \cdot m_1} \bmod p$ (Bob↔Mallory). - [x] **Mallory holds BOTH keys** — enables full read, modify, and inject capability. - [x] **MITM is undetectable** to Alice and Bob without an authentication mechanism. - [x] **Countermeasures:** Digital signatures, X.509 certificates, **Station-to-Station (STS)** protocol, **HMAC-based authentication**, **PSK-based DH**, **ECDHE** with certificate pinning. - [x] **CPS-Specific Risk:** MITM in CPS leads to **false data injection**, **actuator manipulation**, **replay attacks**, and potential **physical damage**. - [x] **Production Recommendation:** Always use **authenticated ephemeral DH (e.g., ECDHE in TLS 1.3)** — never vanilla DH. - [x] **Key Size:** Minimum 2048-bit *p* for classical DH; prefer **Elliptic Curve DH (ECDH)** with 256-bit keys for equivalent security and CPS-friendly performance. - [x] **Forward Secrecy:** Use **ephemeral DH (DHE/ECDHE)** so that compromise of long-term keys does not retroactively decrypt past sessions.

topics.title: Digital Signature - Signing - Verification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT523 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Digital Signature - Signing - Verification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Digital Signature (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> A **Digital Signature** is a cryptographic mechanism that uses **asymmetric (public-key) cryptography** to generate a unique mathematical value (signature) bound to a message and the identity of the signer. It provides **Authentication, Integrity, and Non-Repudiation** simultaneously — making it the digital equivalent of a handwritten signature, but with far stronger mathematical guarantees.

A digital signature scheme is formally defined as a tuple of three polynomial-time algorithms $(Gen, Sign, Verify)$:

$$
