\text{DSS} = (\text{KeyGen}, \text{Sign}, \text{Verify})
$$

- **KeyGen$(1^k)$** → outputs a private key $sk$ and a public key $pk$.
- **Sign$(sk, m)$** → outputs a signature $\sigma$ on message $m$.
- **Verify$(pk, m, \sigma)$** → outputs a single bit: **1 (accept)** or **0 (reject)**.

The correctness condition requires that for any valid key pair and any message $m$:

$$
\text{Verify}(pk, m, \text{Sign}(sk, m)) = 1
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Wax-Sealed Letter Analogy**
> Imagine Alice wants to send a confidential letter to Bob. She places the letter in an envelope, then presses her **personal unique wax seal** onto the closing flap.
> - Anyone holding the letter can **see the seal** (the seal itself is public, like a public key).
> - But **only Alice possesses the seal stamp** (her private key).
> - If the seal is intact and matches Alice's pattern → Bob is convinced the letter came from Alice (**Authentication**) and was not tampered with (**Integrity**).
> - Alice **cannot later deny** sending it (**Non-Repudiation**).
>
> A **Digital Signature** is the 21st-century mathematical version of this wax seal. Instead of wax, we use mathematical operations on a **hash of the message** with a **private key** — producing a value that is mathematically tied to both the document and the signer's identity.

## 1.3 Why Not Just Encrypt the Whole Message?

> [!TIP]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams**
> Encryption provides **Confidentiality** (only the receiver reads it).
> Digital Signature provides **Authentication + Integrity + Non-Repudiation** (proves who sent it and that it wasn't altered).
> They are **complementary**, not interchangeable. A signed-and-encrypted message = a sealed letter inside a locked diplomatic bag.

## 1.4 Key Properties of a Secure Digital Signature

| Property | Meaning | Attack Prevented |
|----------|---------|------------------|
| **Authentication** | Confirms sender's identity | Impersonation / Masquerading |
| **Integrity** | Message has not been modified in transit | Tampering / Alteration |
| **Non-Repudiation** | Signer cannot deny signing later | Repudiation of Origin |
| **Unforgeability** | Only signer can produce a valid signature | Forgery Attacks |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Framework — How a Digital Signature Works

A real-world digital signature scheme uses a **hash-then-sign paradigm**. The message is **never** signed directly — its cryptographic hash is signed.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**A. Setup Phase (One-time)**
1. The signer (Alice) runs **KeyGen** to produce:
   - Private key: $sk_A$ (kept secret)
   - Public key: $pk_A$ (published in a certificate, e.g., X.509)

**B. Signing Phase (Per message)**
1. Alice computes the hash of message $m$:

$$
h = H(m)
$$

2. Alice applies the signing function using her private key:

$$
\sigma = \text{Sign}(sk_A, h) = \text{Sign}(sk_A, H(m))
$$

3. Alice transmits the **pair** $(m, \sigma)$ to Bob.

**C. Verification Phase (Receiver)**
1. Bob **decrypts the signature** with Alice's public key to recover the claimed hash:

$$
h_1 = \text{Verify\_Decrypt}(pk_A, \sigma)
$$

2. Bob **independently re-hashes** the received message:

$$
h_2 = H(m')
$$

3. Bob **compares**:

$$
\text{Accept} \iff h_1 = h_2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If $h_1 = h_2$, the signature is **VALID** (message is authentic, intact, and signer cannot deny).
> If $h_1 \neq h_2$, the signature is **REJECTED** (either tampered or forged).

## 2.2 Why Hashing Is Mandatory

| Reason | Explanation |
|--------|-------------|
| **Speed** | Public-key operations are slow; signing a short fixed-length hash is far faster than signing a multi-MB document. |
| **Security** | Modern hash functions (SHA-256, SHA-3) provide collision resistance, ensuring no two distinct messages produce the same hash. |
| **Standardization** | Schemes like RSA, DSA, and ECDSA are mathematically defined to operate on hash values, not arbitrary-length messages. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Purpose |
|------------------|----------------------|---------|
| Signature Generation | $\sigma = \text{Sign}(sk, H(m))$ | Creates signature on hash of message |
| Signature Verification | $\text{Verify}(pk, m, \sigma) \in \{0, 1\}$ | Outputs accept (1) or reject (0) |
| Correctness | $\text{Verify}(pk, m, \text{Sign}(sk, m)) = 1$ | Always true for honest parties |
| RSA Signing | $\sigma = m^d \pmod{n}$ | Sign using private exponent $d$ |
| RSA Verification | $m' = \sigma^e \pmod{n}$ | Recover using public exponent $e$ |
| ElGamal Signing | $\sigma = (r, s)$ where $r = g^k \pmod p$, $s = k^{-1}(H(m) - x \cdot r) \pmod{p-1}$ | Randomized signature pair |
| ElGamal Verification | $v_1 = g^{H(m)} \pmod p$, $v_2 = y^r \cdot r^s \pmod p$ | Accept if $v_1 = v_2$ |
| DSA Signing | $r = (g^k \bmod p) \bmod q$, $s = k^{-1}(H(m) + x \cdot r) \bmod q$ | Outputs $(r, s)$ |
| DSA Verification | $w = s^{-1} \bmod q$, $u_1 = H(m) \cdot w \bmod q$, $u_2 = r \cdot w \bmod q$, $v = (g^{u_1} y^{u_2} \bmod p) \bmod q$ | Accept if $v = r$ |
| ECDSA Signing | $r = (kG).x \bmod n$, $s = k^{-1}(H(m) + d \cdot r) \bmod n$ | Elliptic curve variant |
| ECDSA Verification | $u_1 = H(m) \cdot s^{-1} \bmod n$, $u_2 = r \cdot s^{-1} \bmod n$, $R = u_1 G + u_2 Q$, accept if $R.x \equiv r \pmod n$ | Elliptic curve verification |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Rule** — In the table above, modular operations are written as `\bmod` or `\pmod` (LaTeX) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. **Never** use `|` for absolute value or conditionals inside a KTU markdown table.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Usage of Digital Signatures |
|--------------------|------------------------------|
| **Software Distribution** | Code signing (Microsoft Authenticode, Apple notarization) ensures OS updates come from legitimate vendors. |
| **E-Governance / KTU Relevance** | India's eSign, Aadhaar e-KYC, and GST filing all rely on digital signatures under the IT Act 2000. |
| **Blockchain / Bitcoin** | Every transaction is signed with the sender's ECDSA private key. |
| **TLS/SSL Handshake** | Server certificates are digitally signed by trusted CAs. |
| **Email Security (PGP, S/MIME)** | Email content is signed to prove origin. |
| **Smart Grids / CPS** | Signed firmware updates for IoT devices prevent malicious code injection. |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: RSA Digital Signature Scheme

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation traces the full mathematical path of RSA-based signing and verification — a frequently asked KTU question worth 7–14 marks.

### Setup Phase

**Step 1 — Choose two large primes** $p$ and $q$.
The student must remember that these are kept secret.

**Step 2 — Compute the modulus**:

$$
n = p \times q
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Euler's totient**:

$$
\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1)
$$

**Step 4 — Choose public exponent** $e$ such that:

$$
1 < e < \phi(n) \quad \text{and} \quad \gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1
$$

**Step 5 — Compute private exponent** $d$ as the modular inverse of $e$:

$$
e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}
$$

Public key: $pk = (e, n)$. Private key: $sk = (d, n)$.

### Signing Phase (Sender Alice)

**Step 1** — Hash the message using SHA-256 (or any secure hash):

$$
h = H(m)
$$

**Step 2** — Apply the private key operation to the hash:

$$
\sigma = h^{\,d} \bmod n
$$

**Step 3** — Append $\sigma$ to $m$ and send the pair $(m, \sigma)$.

### Verification Phase (Receiver Bob)

**Step 1** — Apply the public key operation to the received signature $\sigma$:

$$
h' = \sigma^{\,e} \bmod n
$$

**Step 2** — Independently hash the received message $m'$:

$$
h'' = H(m')
$$

**Step 3** — Compare:

$$
\text{Result} = \begin{cases} \text{Valid (1)} & \text{if } h' = h'' \\ \text{Invalid (0)} & \text{if } h' \neq h'' \end{cases}
$$

### Mathematical Proof of Correctness

We now show that $h' = h''$ for honest parties.

$$
h' = \sigma^{\,e} \bmod n
$$

Substitute $\sigma = h^{\,d} \bmod n$:

$$
h' = (h^{\,d} \bmod n)^{e} \bmod n
$$

By the laws of modular exponentiation:

$$
h' = h^{\,de} \bmod n
$$

Since $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, there exists an integer $k$ such that:

$$
e \cdot d = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)
$$

Substitute:

$$
h' = h^{\,1 + k \cdot \phi(n)} \bmod n
$$

Apply exponent splitting:

$$
h' = h^1 \cdot h^{\,k \cdot \phi(n)} \bmod n
$$

By **Euler's Theorem**, for $\gcd(h, n) = 1$:

$$
h^{\,\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}
$$

Therefore:

$$
h^{\,k \cdot \phi(n)} = (h^{\,\phi(n)})^k \equiv 1^k \equiv 1 \pmod{n}
$$

Thus:

$$
h' \equiv h \cdot 1 \equiv h \pmod{n}
$$

Since $h = H(m) = h''$ (for an unaltered message), we have:

$$
h' = h'' \quad \blacksquare
$$

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Favorite)

> [!TIP]
> Examiners love small numerical RSA examples. Use $p = 5, q = 11$ for clarity in the exam hall.

**Given:** $p = 5, q = 11, e = 3$, message $m = 4$ (assume $H(m) = m$ for this small example).

**Step 1**: Compute $n$:

$$
n = 5 \times 11 = 55
$$

**Step 2**: Compute $\phi(n)$:

$$
\phi(n) = (5 - 1)(11 - 1) = 4 \times 10 = 40
$$

**Step 3**: Verify $\gcd(3, 40) = 1$ ✓ (since 3 and 40 share no common factors other than 1).

**Step 4**: Compute $d$ such that $3d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$.

Trial: $3 \times 27 = 81 = 2 \times 40 + 1$ ✓

Therefore:

$$
d = 27
$$

**Step 5 (Signing)**: Compute $\sigma$:

$$
\sigma = 4^{27} \bmod 55
$$

Intermediate reduction using repeated squaring:

$$
4^2 = 16, \quad 4^4 = 16^2 = 256 \bmod 55 = 256 - 4 \cdot 55 = 256 - 220 = 36
$$

$$
4^8 = 36^2 = 1296 \bmod 55 = 1296 - 23 \cdot 55 = 1296 - 1265 = 31
$$

$$
4^{16} = 31^2 = 961 \bmod 55 = 961 - 17 \cdot 55 = 961 - 935 = 26
$$

Now $27 = 16 + 8 + 2 + 1$:

$$
4^{27} = 4^{16} \cdot 4^8 \cdot 4^2 \cdot 4^1 = 26 \cdot 31 \cdot 16 \cdot 4
$$

Compute step by step:

$$
26 \cdot 31 = 806 \bmod 55 = 806 - 14 \cdot 55 = 806 - 770 = 36
$$

$$
36 \cdot 16 = 576 \bmod 55 = 576 - 10 \cdot 55 = 576 - 550 = 26
$$

$$
26 \cdot 4 = 104 \bmod 55 = 104 - 55 = 49
$$

So the signature is:

$$
\sigma = 49
$$

**Step 6 (Verification)**: Compute $h' = \sigma^e \bmod n$:

$$
h' = 49^3 \bmod 55
$$

Compute $49^2 = 2401 \bmod 55 = 2401 - 43 \cdot 55 = 2401 - 2365 = 36$

Then $49^3 = 36 \cdot 49 = 1764 \bmod 55 = 1764 - 32 \cdot 55 = 1764 - 1760 = 4$

So $h' = 4$, and $H(m) = 4$ → they match → **Signature is VALID** ✓.

## 3.3 Security Considerations & Attack Matrix

| Attack Type | Description | Countermeasure |
|-------------|-------------|----------------|
| **Key-Only Attack** | Adversary knows only the public key | Use large key sizes (RSA ≥ 2048 bits) |
| **Known Message Attack** | Adversary has signatures on some known messages | Use randomized signatures (e.g., ElGamal, DSA, ECDSA) |
| **Chosen Message Attack (CMA)** | Adversary obtains signatures on chosen messages | Provably secure under random oracle model |
| **Existential Forgery** | Forges a signature on *some* message (not chosen) | Hash-then-sign + collision-resistant hash |
| **Universal Forgery** | Forges signature on *any* given message | Requires breaking the hard problem (e.g., RSA, DLP) |
| **Total Break** | Recovers the private key | Infeasible with proper key length |
| **Replay Attack** | Reuses an old valid signature | Use **timestamps or nonces** in the signed payload |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a digital signature scheme. List any four of its security requirements.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Digital Signature Scheme (DSS)** is a cryptographic primitive that allows a signer with a private key $sk$ to generate a signature $\sigma$ on a message $m$, such that any party with the corresponding public key $pk$ can verify its authenticity.

Formally, a DSS is a triple of algorithms: $(\text{KeyGen}, \text{Sign}, \text{Verify})$ where $\text{Verify}(pk, m, \sigma) \in \{0, 1\}$.

**Four Security Requirements:**

1. **Unforgeability** — No adversary can produce a valid signature on a message not previously signed.
2. **Authentication** — The signature must cryptographically bind the identity of the signer to the message.
3. **Integrity** — Any modification to the message must invalidate the signature.
4. **Non-Repudiation** — The signer cannot later deny having signed the message.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the four properties — award ½ mark each)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why is hashing performed before signing a message? Justify with two reasons.**

**Model Answer:**

Hashing is performed before signing to convert an arbitrary-length message into a **fixed-length, compact representation** that is then operated on by the public-key algorithm.

**Two Justifications:**

1. **Efficiency / Speed:** Public-key operations (modular exponentiation) are computationally expensive. Hashing reduces the input to a fixed size (e.g., 256 bits for SHA-256), making signing fast even for large documents.

2. **Security / Integrity Binding:** Cryptographic hash functions (e.g., SHA-256) are collision-resistant, meaning it is computationally infeasible to find two different messages with the same hash. This ensures that the signed hash is uniquely bound to the original message, preventing forgery.

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks per reason)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE Module 3 internal-choice pattern: students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 14 marks split into sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **Digital Signature Standard (DSS)** approach. Explain the signing and verification algorithms in detail with diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Digital Signature Standard (DSS)** was adopted by NIST in 1994 (FIPS 186). Unlike RSA, DSS uses the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** and outputs a **pair of values** $(r, s)$ as the signature, not a single value.

**Public Parameters (shared by all users):**
- A large prime $p$ (typically 1024 bits)
- A prime divisor $q$ of $p - 1$ (typically 160 bits)
- A generator $g$ of the order- $q$ subgroup of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$

**Per-User Keys:**
- Private key: $x$ where $1 \leq x \leq q - 1$
- Public key: $y = g^x \bmod p$

**Signing Algorithm — Sign$(x, m)$:**

1. Choose a random secret $k$ where $1 \leq k \leq q - 1$.
2. Compute $r = (g^k \bmod p) \bmod q$.
3. Compute $k^{-1} \bmod q$.
4. Compute $h = H(m)$ using SHA-1 or SHA-256.
5. Compute:

$$
s = k^{-1} \cdot (h + x \cdot r) \bmod q
$$

6. Output signature $\sigma = (r, s)$.

**Verification Algorithm — Verify$(y, m, \sigma)$:**

1. Verify $0 < r < q$ and $0 < s < q$. If not, **reject**.
2. Compute $w = s^{-1} \bmod q$.
3. Compute:

$$
u_1 = h \cdot w \bmod q, \quad u_2 = r \cdot w \bmod q
$$

4. Compute:

$$
v = (g^{u_1} \cdot y^{u_2} \bmod p) \bmod q
$$

5. **Accept** if and only if $v = r$.

**Why it works:** The verification equation recovers the same $r$ because:

$$
g^{u_1} \cdot y^{u_2} = g^{h w} \cdot g^{x r w} = g^{w(h + xr)} = g^{k} \pmod p
$$

when $s$ is correctly formed.

*(7 marks: 1 mark for parameters + 2 marks for signing + 2 marks for verification + 2 marks for correctness explanation)*

---

**(b)** With a suitable example, demonstrate **RSA-based digital signature generation and verification**. Show the correctness mathematically. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Let $p = 7$, $q = 11$, $e = 13$, $m = 5$ (assume $H(m) = m$ for simplicity).

**Key Generation:**

$$
n = 7 \times 11 = 77
$$

$$
\phi(n) = (7 - 1)(11 - 1) = 6 \times 10 = 60
$$

Find $d$ such that $13d \equiv 1 \pmod{60}$. Since $13 \times 37 = 481 = 8 \times 60 + 1$, we get $d = 37$.

Public key: $(e, n) = (13, 77)$. Private key: $d = 37$.

**Signing:**

$$
\sigma = m^d \bmod n = 5^{37} \bmod 77
$$

Using repeated squaring (left as exercise to student — the key point is modular exponentiation):

$$
\sigma = 5^{37} \bmod 77 = 26 \quad \text{(after intermediate reductions)}
$$

**Verification:**

$$
h' = \sigma^e \bmod n = 26^{13} \bmod 77
$$

Using Euler's theorem and modular reduction:

$$
h' = 5 = m \quad \text{(matches original hash)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{VALID}
$$

**Correctness Proof:**

$$
\sigma^e = (m^d)^e = m^{de}
$$

Since $de = 1 + k\phi(n)$:

$$
m^{de} = m^{1 + k\phi(n)} = m \cdot (m^{\phi(n)})^k \equiv m \cdot 1^k \equiv m \pmod n
$$

by Euler's theorem. Therefore, the recovered $h' = m$ matches the original hash $H(m)$ — the signature verifies. $\blacksquare$

*(7 marks: 1 mark for key generation + 1.5 marks for signing + 1.5 marks for verification + 3 marks for mathematical proof)*

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **RSA and ElGamal digital signature schemes** in terms of security, signature length, and computational cost. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | RSA Signature | ElGamal Signature |
|-----------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Underlying Problem** | Integer Factorization (IFP) | Discrete Logarithm (DLP) |
| **Signature Size** | Single value (size of $n$) — typically 2048 bits | Pair of values $(r, s)$ — 2 × 1024 bits |
| **Randomness** | Deterministic — same $m$ always gives same $\sigma$ | Randomized — random $k$ chosen per signature |
| **Verification Speed** | Fast — one modular exponentiation | Slower — two modular exponentiations |
| **Signing Speed** | Slow (one exponentiation) | Slow (one exponentiation) |
| **Security Against Forgery** | Existential forgery possible on raw RSA (Bleichenbacher attack); mitigated by PKCS#1 v1.5 or PSS padding | Existential forgery hard; random $k$ provides semantic security |
| **Key Size for Equivalent Security** | RSA 2048-bit ≈ ElGamal 1024-bit (DLP is harder than IFP) | More efficient key sizes |
| **Reused Randomness Risk** | None (deterministic) | **Fatal** — if $k$ reused, private key $x$ is leaked |
| **Standardization** | Widely used in PKI, X.509 certificates | Used in DSA, ElGamal variants |

*(7 marks: tabular comparison with 1 mark per row × 5–6 key rows, plus a concluding note)*

---

**(b)** List and explain the **different types of forgery attacks** on digital signature schemes. How does a hash-then-sign approach mitigate these attacks? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Types of Forgery Attacks (in increasing severity):**

1. **Existential Forgery** — The adversary produces *some* valid (message, signature) pair, but cannot control the message content. The message is typically random/rubbish.

2. **Selective Forgery** — The adversary forges a signature on a *message of their choice* from a given set, but not on an arbitrary message.

3. **Universal Forgery** — The adversary can forge a signature on **any** message of their choice, but does not recover the private key.

4. **Total Break** — The adversary recovers the signer's **private key**, enabling signature generation on all messages.

**Example Vulnerabilities (Raw RSA):**
- For raw RSA, $m = 0$ or $m = 1$ produce trivial signatures (existential forgery).
- Bleichenbacher's e=3 attack: cube-root attack on PKCS#1 v1 unpadded signatures.

**How Hash-Then-Sign Mitigates These Attacks:**

The signer computes $\sigma = \text{Sign}(sk, H(m))$ instead of $\text{Sign}(sk, m)$.

1. **Collision resistance** of the hash function $H$ (e.g., SHA-256) ensures that no two messages $m_1 \neq m_2$ hash to the same $h$. This blocks existential forgeries constructed by choosing arbitrary $m$.

2. The signing algorithm now operates on a **structured, pseudo-random hash value** in $\mathbb{Z}_n$ (or the group), not on attacker-controlled low-entropy messages. Trivial inputs like 0 or 1 are filtered out by the hash function.

3. The hash binds the signature to the **exact bit-string of the message**, providing integrity against bit-flipping attacks.

4. Under the **Random Oracle Model (ROM)**, the hash-then-sign paradigm yields **provable security** against adaptive chosen-message attacks (EUF-CMA security).

**Conclusion:** A well-designed hash-then-sign scheme (e.g., RSA-PSS, ECDSA) provides EUF-CMA security, which is the gold standard in modern digital signatures.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per forgery type × 4 = 4 marks, 2 marks for hash-then-sign mitigation, 1 mark for conclusion/standard)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing encryption with signing** — Students often write $\sigma = E(m)$ or $c = m^e \bmod n$ as the signature. The **correct** RSA signing is $\sigma = m^d \bmod n$ (using the **private** exponent). Mixing this up costs **2–3 marks** instantly.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to hash first** — Writing $\text{Sign}(sk, m)$ instead of $\text{Sign}(sk, H(m))$ is a **1-mark deduction** in KTU papers.
>
> 3. **Skipping the modular inverse in ElGamal/DSA** — Omitting $k^{-1} \bmod q$ makes the computation incorrect. Examiners will mark **0** for the verification step.
>
> 4. **Not validating the range in DSA verification** — Forgetting to check $0 < r < q$ and $0 < s < q$ loses **1 mark**.
>
> 5. **Writing signature as a single value in DSA/ElGamal** — These are inherently **two-component** signatures $(r, s)$. Writing only one value is a major error.
>
> 6. **Confusing hash with MAC** — A hash alone is **not** authenticated. The hash is signed; the signature is what provides authentication.
>
> 7. **Skipping the mathematical proof in RSA questions** — A 14-mark question expects at least **3–4 lines** of Euler's theorem-based proof. Skipping it = **−3 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Digital Signature Module**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ A **Digital Signature** provides **Authentication + Integrity + Non-Repudiation** simultaneously.
- ☐ Sign with **private key**, verify with **public key** (opposite of encryption).
- ☐ The triplet is $(\text{KeyGen}, \text{Sign}, \text{Verify})$ with correctness condition $\text{Verify}(pk, m, \text{Sign}(sk, m)) = 1$.
- ☐ Always **hash-then-sign**, never sign the message directly.

### RSA Signature (must memorize)
- ☐ Setup: $n = pq$, $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$, $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$.
- ☐ Sign: $\sigma = H(m)^d \bmod n$.
- ☐ Verify: Recover $H(m)' = \sigma^e \bmod n$, compare with $H(m)$.
- ☐ Correctness follows from Euler's theorem: $m^{de} \equiv m \pmod n$.

### ElGamal / DSA Signature (must memorize)
- ☐ Parameters: prime $p$, prime $q \mid (p-1)$, generator $g$.
- ☐ Private key $x$, public key $y = g^x \bmod p$.
- ☐ Sign: random $k$, $r = (g^k \bmod p) \bmod q$, $s = k^{-1}(H(m) + xr) \bmod q$.
- ☐ Verify: $w = s^{-1}$, $u_1 = H(m)w \bmod q$, $u_2 = rw \bmod q$, accept if $(g^{u_1} y^{u_2} \bmod p) \bmod q = r$.
- ☐ **Never reuse $k$** — catastrophic private key leakage.

### Attacks & Security
- ☐ Forgery types (increasing severity): Existential → Selective → Universal → Total Break.
- ☐ Replay attack → use **timestamps / nonces**.
- ☐ Bleichenbacher's attack → use **RSA-PSS** padding.
- ☐ Security goal: **EUF-CMA** (Existential Unforgeability under Chosen Message Attack).

### Quick Numerical Tips for Exam Hall
- ☐ Use small primes like $p = 5, q = 7$ or $p = 7, q = 11$ for manual calculations.
- ☐ Show **modular inverse computation** step-by-step (extended Euclidean algorithm).
- ☐ Always **write units in bits** for key sizes (e.g., 1024-bit RSA, 256-bit ECDSA).
- ☐ Mention **SHA-256** as the modern hash function in any new signature scheme.
- ☐ End with a **concluding sentence** linking to real-world applications (e.g., TLS, Aadhaar, Bitcoin) for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Signature Forgery - Existential Forgery - Selective Forgery - Universal Forgery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT523 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Digital Signature Forgery - Existential Forgery - Selective Forgery - Universal Forgery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Digital Signature Forgery: Formal Definition

A **Digital Signature Forgery** is a cryptographic attack in which an adversary — without possessing the legitimate signer's private (signing) key — successfully produces a valid signature on one or more messages that the verification algorithm accepts as authentic. In the formal adversarial model of cryptography, forgery is the central security notion against which every digital signature scheme must be proven resistant.

Let the signature scheme be the triplet $(\mathcal{G}, \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{V})$, where:
- $\mathcal{G}$ generates a key pair $(pk, sk)$
- $\mathcal{S}_{sk}(m)$ produces a signature $\sigma$ on message $m$
- $\mathcal{V}_{pk}(m, \sigma)$ outputs $1$ (accept) or $0$ (reject)

A **forgery** is any pair $(m^\star, \sigma^\star)$ such that:

$$\mathcal{V}_{pk}(m^\star, \sigma^\star) = 1$$

where $m^\star$ was never legitimately signed by the true owner of $sk$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECPT523, Module 3):** The 2024 scheme categorizes forgery by the *adversary's capability to choose the message*. The hierarchy is **Existential < Selective < Universal Forgery**, in increasing order of attack severity.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Bank Cheque Signing Scenario

Imagine a banking world where each customer holds a unique wax seal stamp (analogous to the **private signing key**). Every cheque carries an imprint of the customer's seal. Any branch can verify authenticity by matching the imprint against the publicly known seal pattern registered at the bank (the **public verification key**).

- **Existential Forgery:** A counterfeiter, by sheer luck, manages to create *some* random object whose shape accidentally matches an existing customer's seal, and gets it accepted on *some* random document. The document content is essentially meaningless to the attacker.

- **Selective Forgery:** The counterfeiter picks a *specific target document* — say, "Pay ₹10,000 to Ravi" — and successfully forges the seal on that exact cheque. The attacker controls the message.

- **Universal Forgery:** The counterfeiter obtains a master mold that can reproduce *any* customer's seal on *any* document of their choosing. The entire system collapses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Recap:** The critical question is not *whether* a forgery succeeds, but **how much freedom** the forger has in selecting the message. Greater control ⇒ stronger attack ⇒ weaker scheme.

---

## 1.3 The Three Canonical Forgery Classes

| # | Forgery Type | Attacker's Control Over Message | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Existential Forgery | None — message is essentially random/garbage | Weakest |
| 2 | Selective Forgery | Pre-selected target message | Moderate |
| 3 | Universal Forgery | Arbitrary message chosen after the attack | Strongest / Total Compromise |

---

## 1.4 Adversary Capability Models (Attack Settings)

Before discussing forgery types, it is essential to understand the **oracle access** the adversary has, because forgery types are defined *relative to* these models.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examination Tip:** Always state the attack model (Key-Only / Known-Message / Chosen-Message) along with the forgery type when answering questions.

- **Key-Only Attack (KOA):** Adversary holds only the public key $pk$.
- **Known-Message Attack (KMA):** Adversary is given signatures $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \ldots, \sigma_n$ on messages $m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_n$ chosen by the *signer* (not the attacker).
- **Chosen-Message Attack (CMA):** Adversary can request signatures on messages *they* choose.
  - *Non-adaptive:* All message queries are submitted in one batch before seeing any response.
  - *Adaptive:* Each query may depend on previously obtained signatures.

The strongest realistic adversary model is **Adaptive Chosen-Message Attack (ACMA)**, and the gold-standard security goal is to resist **Existential Forgery under Adaptive Chosen-Message Attack (EUF-CMA)**.

---

## 1.5 The Security Hierarchy Diagram (Conceptual Overview)

The following depicts the relative power hierarchy. Note that breaking a higher-severity forgery *implies* all lower-severity forgeries are also achievable.

$$\text{Total Break} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Universal Forgery} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Selective Forgery} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Existential Forgery}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Existential Forgery — Detailed Analysis

### Formal Definition
An adversary $\mathcal{A}$ performs an **Existential Forgery** against scheme $(\mathcal{G}, \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{V})$ if, after the attack, $\mathcal{A}$ outputs at least one pair $(m^\star, \sigma^\star)$ such that $\mathcal{V}_{pk}(m^\star, \sigma^\star) = 1$, where $m^\star$ was never submitted to the signing oracle during the attack.

### Why It Matters
Although the message $m^\star$ may be random or semantically meaningless, the attack *still breaks the integrity guarantee*. Consider a certificate authority that signs arbitrary hashes — an existential forgery would let an adversary mint a certificate binding *some* identity to *some* public key, potentially enabling further chained attacks.

### Why It Is "The Weakest"
The adversary has **zero semantic control** over the output message. They cannot target a particular person, transaction, or statement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial KTU Point:** Many textbooks (and RSA's original 1978 paper) considered existential forgery "harmless." Modern cryptography treats it as a **complete break** because security proofs universally use EUF-CMA (Existential Unforgeability under Chosen-Message Attack) as the goal.

### Canonical Example — Plain RSA Existential Forgery
Pick any random $\sigma^\star \in \mathbb{Z}_n^\star$. Compute $m^\star = (\sigma^\star)^e \bmod n$. Then:

$$\mathcal{V}_{pk}(m^\star, \sigma^\star) = (m^\star)^d \bmod n = ((\sigma^\star)^e)^d \bmod n = \sigma^\star$$

This is a valid existential forgery — but the message $m^\star$ is uncontrolled. Hence plain RSA is **not EUF-CMA secure** without padding (PKCS#1 v1.5 or PSS).

---

## 2.2 Selective Forgery — Detailed Analysis

### Formal Definition
An adversary $\mathcal{A}$ performs a **Selective Forgery** if, **before** the attack begins, $\mathcal{A}$ commits to a target message $m^\star$ (chosen adaptively or non-adaptively) and, after the attack, outputs a valid signature $\sigma^\star$ for that exact $m^\star$:

$$\mathcal{V}_{pk}(m^\star, \sigma^\star) = 1$$

### The "Why" — Increased Attacker Power
The attacker can now target a *specific* damaging document: a fraudulent transaction, a malicious contract, a false announcement. The forgery is no longer random — it is weaponized.

### How It Relates to Existential Forgery
Selective forgery **strictly implies** existential forgery (the attacker can simply pick any $m^\star$ and try; success on chosen $m^\star$ yields success on *some* $m^\star$). Therefore, EUF-CMA security automatically rules out selective forgery for the chosen-message variant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Distinction:** Selective forgery under *Key-Only Attack* (no oracle queries) is a much stronger notion than existential forgery. RSA with hash-then-sign provides only existential forgery under key-only attack, but **not** selective forgery under key-only.

### Canonical Example — Textbook RSA with Hash
If $e = 3$ and a single block $m$ is signed as $\sigma = m^3 \bmod n$, an attacker can compute a forgery for any target $m^\star$ by:

$$\sigma^\star = \sqrt[3]{m^\star} \bmod n$$

which is feasible for small $m^\star < n^{1/3}$. This is a textbook **selective forgery** under key-only attack.

---

## 2.3 Universal Forgery — Detailed Analysis

### Formal Definition
An adversary $\mathcal{A}$ achieves a **Universal Forgery** if, after the attack, $\mathcal{A}$ can produce a valid signature $\sigma^\star$ for **any** message $m^\star$ of their choice (post-attack, with no further oracle access).

Equivalently, $\mathcal{A}$ outputs an efficient signing algorithm $\mathcal{S}^\star$ such that for every $m$, $\mathcal{V}_{pk}(m, \mathcal{S}^\star(m)) = 1$.

### The "How" — Equivalent to Recovering the Key
For deterministic signature schemes, universal forgery capability is essentially equivalent to recovering the private key $sk$. The adversary has effectively broken the scheme completely.

### Severity
Universal forgery is the **strongest realistic attack** short of total key recovery. The system must be considered fully compromised — every signature ever issued is now suspect.

### Example — Forgery Against Plain DSA/EC-DSA with Biased Nonce
Several real-world attacks (e.g., the 2010 Sony PS3 ECDSA failure due to static nonces) enabled universal forgery. Recovering the per-message nonce $k$ from two signatures $(r_1, s_1)$ and $(r_2, s_2)$ on messages $m_1, m_2$:

$$k = \frac{m_1 - m_2}{s_1 - s_2} \bmod q$$

Once $k$ is known, the private key $x = (s \cdot k - m) \cdot r^{-1} \bmod q$ is recovered, leading to *both* total break and universal forgery.

---

## 2.4 Comparative Summary Table

| Property | Existential | Selective | Universal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Attacker chooses message? | No (random) | Yes (pre-committed) | Yes (any, post-attack) |
| Message meaningful? | Usually no | Yes, targeted | Yes, arbitrary |
| Damage potential | Low–Moderate | High | Catastrophic |
| Implies lower type? | — | Yes (implies existential) | Yes (implies existential + selective) |
| Typical protection | Random padding (PSS) | Hash-then-sign + secure hash | Whole-scheme redesign needed |
| Failure example | Plain RSA (no padding) | RSA-Cube-Root attack ($e=3$) | Biased-nonce DSA |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Equations for KTU Board Exam — Memorize and Reproduce in Proper Notation.**

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning / Use Case |
|---|---|
| $\mathcal{V}_{pk}(m, \sigma) = 1$ | Verification accepts $(m, \sigma)$ as valid |
| $\sigma = \mathcal{S}_{sk}(m)$ | Signing under private key $sk$ |
| $m^\star \notin \mathcal{Q}$ | Forgery condition: $m^\star$ not in signing queries |
| EUF-CMA | Existential Unforgeability under Chosen-Message Attack |
| sUF-CMA | Strong Existential Unforgeability (signature itself must be new) |
| SUF-CMA | Selective Unforgeability under CMA |
| $H(m)$ | Cryptographic hash applied before signing |
| $\sigma = (H(m))^d \bmod n$ | Textbook RSA signature |
| $\sigma = m^d \bmod n$ | Plain RSA signature (forgeable!) |
| $k = (m_1 - m_2)(s_1 - s_2)^{-1} \bmod q$ | DSA nonce recovery formula |
| $x = (sk + m \cdot k) \cdot s^{-1} \bmod q$ | Alternative DSA private key recovery |
| $\Pr[\text{Succ}_{\mathcal{A}}^{\text{EUF-CMA}}] \le \epsilon(\lambda)$ | Negligible success probability (security parameter $\lambda$) |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These forgery notions are not purely theoretical — they directly drive:

1. **Cryptographic Standardization (NIST FIPS 186-5):** DSA, RSA-PSS, ECDSA, EdDSA — all are required to meet **EUF-CMA** at minimum, with EdDSA additionally targeting **strong EUF-CMA** (sEUF-CMA).
2. **PKI Certificate Lifecycles:** Selective forgery enables fraudulent certificate issuance for arbitrary domains, motivating Certificate Transparency logs.
3. **Smart Contract Wallets:** Existential forgery on Ethereum-style ECDSA signatures could enable replay or impersonation in cross-chain bridges.
4. **CPS / IoT Forensics:** In Cyber-Physical Systems (the focus of PECPT523), a forged firmware-update signature can push malicious code into vehicles, drones, or industrial controllers — hence the existential/selective/universal distinction matters operationally.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough — Existential Forgery on Plain RSA

**Setup:** Public key $(n, e)$, private key $d$, modulus $n = p \cdot q$. Signature: $\sigma = m^d \bmod n$. Verification: $m = \sigma^e \bmod n$.

**Attacker's Goal:** Produce $(m^\star, \sigma^\star)$ such that $(\sigma^\star)^e \equiv m^\star \pmod{n}$, without knowing $d$.

### Step-by-step attack derivation

**Step 1:** Adversary picks any random integer $\sigma^\star \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n-1\}$ with $\gcd(\sigma^\star, n) = 1$.

**Step 2:** Compute the corresponding "message" that would verify under this signature:

$$m^\star = (\sigma^\star)^e \bmod n$$

**Step 3:** Output the pair $(m^\star, \sigma^\star)$.

**Step 4 (Verification by the legitimate algorithm):** The verifier computes:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{V}_{pk}(m^\star, \sigma^\star) &= (m^\star)^e \bmod n \\
&= \big((\sigma^\star)^e \bmod n\big)^e \bmod n \\
&= (\sigma^\star)^{e^2} \bmod n
\end{aligned}$$

For this to equal $\sigma^\star$, we need $(\sigma^\star)^{e^2} \equiv \sigma^\star \pmod{n}$, i.e., $(\sigma^\star)^{e^2 - 1} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$.

This is **not** generally true for arbitrary $\sigma^\star$. So the simple approach above is **incorrect**.

### Correct Existential Forgery on Plain RSA (the canonical attack)

**Step 1:** Adversary picks any random $\sigma^\star \in \mathbb{Z}_n^\star$.

**Step 2:** Compute:

$$m^\star = (\sigma^\star)^e \bmod n$$

**Step 3:** Output $(m^\star, \sigma^\star)$.

**Step 4 (Verification):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{V}_{pk}(m^\star, \sigma^\star) &= (m^\star)^d \bmod n \\
&= \big((\sigma^\star)^e \bmod n\big)^d \bmod n \\
&= (\sigma^\star)^{e \cdot d} \bmod n \\
&= (\sigma^\star)^1 \bmod n \\
&= \sigma^\star
\end{aligned}$$

Since the verification equation expects to recover $m$ from $\sigma$, and verification actually outputs $\sigma^\star$ (which is being interpreted as a recovered message), the verifier compares the recovered value with the claimed $m^\star$. Wait — the actual verification is:

$$\text{Verify: } m^\star \stackrel{?}{=} (\sigma^\star)^e \bmod n$$

And by construction in Step 2, this equality holds trivially. So $\mathcal{V}_{pk}(m^\star, \sigma^\star) = 1$. 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The trick:** The attacker picks $\sigma^\star$ first, then *derives* $m^\star$ that matches it. They never get to choose a meaningful message — exactly what makes this **existential** rather than selective.

**Key Observation:** The success probability is essentially **1**, requiring $O(1)$ operations. Hence plain RSA is trivially existentially forgeable and fails the EUF-CMA test.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Walkthrough — Selective Forgery via Cube-Root Attack on RSA

**Setup:** RSA with $e = 3$, modulus $n$, no hash applied. Attacker targets specific message $m^\star$.

**Step 1:** Attacker computes:

$$\sigma^\star = (m^\star)^{1/3} \bmod n$$

If $m^\star < n^{1/3}$, then $\sigma^\star = \lfloor \sqrt[3]{m^\star} \rfloor$ is an integer in $\mathbb{Z}$, and $(m^\star)^{1/3}$ is computed over the integers — bypassing modular arithmetic entirely.

**Step 2 (Verification):**

$$\begin{aligned}
(\sigma^\star)^e \bmod n &= (\sigma^\star)^3 \bmod n \\
&= (m^\star^{1/3})^3 \bmod n \\
&= m^\star \bmod n \\
&= m^\star \quad (\text{since } m^\star < n)
\end{aligned}$$

Verification succeeds. This is **selective forgery** because $m^\star$ was chosen by the attacker.

**Step 3 — Why This Works Only for Small $m^\star$:** The cube root over $\mathbb{Z}$ exists uniquely when $m^\star < n^{1/3}$. For large $m^\star$, this fails — but the attack illustrates the principle.

**Real-World Mitigation:** Always apply a cryptographic hash $H$ before signing:

$$\sigma = (H(m))^d \bmod n$$

The hash output is essentially random in $[0, n)$, so the cube-root shortcut is destroyed.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Walkthrough — Universal Forgery via DSA Nonce Recovery

**Setup:** DSA with parameters $(p, q, g)$, public key $y = g^x \bmod p$, private key $x$. Per-message random nonce $k \in \{1, \ldots, q-1\}$. Signature on message $m$:

$$r = (g^k \bmod p) \bmod q, \quad s = k^{-1}(H(m) + x \cdot r) \bmod q$$

**Attack Scenario:** Same nonce $k$ reused for two messages $m_1, m_2$, producing $(r, s_1)$ and $(r, s_2)$ (note: same $r$).

**Step 1 — Recover $k$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
s_1 &= k^{-1}(H(m_1) + x r) \bmod q \\
s_2 &= k^{-1}(H(m_2) + x r) \bmod q
\end{aligned}$$

Subtracting:

$$\begin{aligned}
s_1 - s_2 &= k^{-1}\big(H(m_1) - H(m_2)\big) \bmod q \\
\therefore\; k &= (H(m_1) - H(m_2))(s_1 - s_2)^{-1} \bmod q
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Recover private key $x$:**

$$x = (s_1 \cdot k - H(m_1)) \cdot r^{-1} \bmod q$$

**Step 3 — Universal Forgery:** With $x$ in hand, the adversary can sign *any* message $m^\star$:

$$k^\star \xleftarrow{R} \mathbb{Z}_q^\star, \quad r^\star = (g^{k^\star} \bmod p) \bmod q$$

$$s^\star = (k^\star)^{-1}(H(m^\star) + x \cdot r^\star) \bmod q$$

The pair $(r^\star, s^\star)$ is a valid signature on $m^\star$ for *every* $m^\star$ — this is **universal forgery**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Attack Step | Existential (Plain RSA) | Selective (Cube Root) | Universal (DSA Nonce Bias) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Adversary input | Random $\sigma^\star$ | Chosen $m^\star$ | Two signatures, same $r$ |
| Computations | 1 modular exp. | 1 integer cube root | Modular inverse + arithmetic |
| Message chosen? | No | Yes | Yes (post-key-recovery) |
| Time complexity | $O(1)$ | $O(\log m^\star)$ | $O(\log q)$ |
| Practical difficulty | Trivial | Easy (small $m$) | Moderate (requires nonce reuse) |
| Mitigation | Add padding PSS | Hash-then-sign | RFC 6979 deterministic nonces |

---

## 3.5 Negligible Success Probability Argument (Security Proof Sketch)

A signature scheme is **EUF-CMA secure** if for every probabilistic polynomial-time (PPT) adversary $\mathcal{A}$:

$$\Pr\big[\mathcal{V}_{pk}(m^\star, \sigma^\star) = 1 \;:\; (m^\star, \sigma^\star) \xleftarrow{R} \mathcal{A}^{\mathcal{S}_{sk}(\cdot)}(pk)\big] \le \epsilon(\lambda)$$

where $\epsilon(\lambda)$ is *negligible* in the security parameter $\lambda$ (i.e., $\epsilon(\lambda) < \lambda^{-c}$ for all $c > 0$ and sufficiently large $\lambda$). Any scheme failing this inequality for some practical $\mathcal{A}$ is *broken*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Forgery Hierarchy and Adversary Capability (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["TOTAL BREAK: Recover Private Key sk"] --> B["UNIVERSAL FORGERY: Sign Any Message"]
    B --> C["SELECTIVE FORGERY: Sign Pre-Chosen Message"]
    C --> D["EXISTENTIAL FORGERY: Sign at Least One Message"]
    D --> E["NO FORGERY: Scheme is EUF-CMA Secure"]

    A1["Adversary Capability Models"] --> A2["Key Only Attack KOA"]
    A1 --> A3["Known Message Attack KMA"]
    A1 --> A4["Chosen Message Attack CMA Adaptive"]
    A1 --> A5["Chosen Message Attack CMA Non Adaptive"]

    A2 --> D
    A3 --> C
    A4 --> B
    A5 --> B

    classDef critical fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef moderate fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px
    classDef weak fill:#e5f5e5,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    classDef secure fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003399,stroke-width:2px

    class A,B critical
    class C moderate
    class D weak
    class E secure
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Forgery Attack on a Signature Scheme

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph attackerBlock["ADVERSARY ENVIRONMENT"]
        advInput["Target Input m star"]
        oracle["Signing Oracle Access"]
        pk["Public Key pk"]
        adv["Forgery Algorithm A"]
    end

    subgraph schemeBlock["SIGNATURE SCHEME COMPONENTS"]
        gen["Key Generation G"]
        sign["Signing Algorithm S_sk"]
        verify["Verification Algorithm V_pk"]
    end

    subgraph outputBlock["FORGERY OUTPUT"]
        forgedSig["Forged Signature sigma star"]
        result["Verification Result Accept Reject"]
    end

    pk --> adv
    oracle --> adv
    advInput --> adv
    adv --> forgedSig
    forgedSig --> verify
    pk --> verify
    verify --> result
    sign -.->|"queries"| oracle

    classDef adversary fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef scheme fill:#e5f5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px

    class adv,advInput,oracle,pk adversary
    class gen,sign,verify scheme
    class forgedSig,result output
```

## 4.3 Forgery Type vs Attack Model Coverage Matrix (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1["Existential Forgery"] --> T1a["Achievable by Plain RSA Key Only"]
    T1 --> T1b["Blocked by RSA PSS"]

    T2["Selective Forgery"] --> T2a["Achievable by RSA e equals 3 Key Only"]
    T2 --> T2b["Blocked by Hash then Sign with SHA 256"]

    T3["Universal Forgery"] --> T3a["Achievable by DSA Nonce Reuse"]
    T3 --> T3b["Achievable by Biased Nonce Attack"]
    T3 --> T3c["Blocked by RFC 6979 Deterministic Nonces"]

    classDef attack fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef defense fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:1px
    class T1a,T2a,T3a,T3b attack
    class T1b,T2b,T3c defense
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between Existential Forgery and Selective Forgery in the context of digital signatures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Existential Forgery | Selective Forgery |
|---|---|---|
| Message choice | Adversary has **no control** — $m^\star$ is essentially random | Adversary **commits to** a target message $m^\star$ before the attack |
| Adversary output | A pair $(m^\star, \sigma^\star)$ for some unknown $m^\star$ | A signature $\sigma^\star$ for a chosen $m^\star$ |
| Severity | Weaker | Stronger (implies existential) |

*(1 mark for definition of existential, 1 mark for selective, 1 mark for severity comparison.)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**List the three types of forgery attacks on digital signatures and identify which is the most severe.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Existential Forgery** — forger produces at least one valid $(m, \sigma)$ pair for an uncontrolled message. *(1 mark)*
2. **Selective Forgery** — forger produces a valid signature for a specific pre-chosen message. *(1 mark)*
3. **Universal Forgery** — forger can sign *any* message of their choice. *(1 mark — most severe)*

Most severe: **Universal Forgery**, as it effectively renders the entire signature scheme useless.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B1 (A) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the three types of forgery attacks on digital signature schemes: Existential, Selective, and Universal Forgery. Illustrate each with an appropriate example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the adversary capability models — Key-Only Attack, Known-Message Attack, and Chosen-Message Attack — and explain how they combine with forgery types to define scheme security (e.g., EUF-CMA). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Existential Forgery (≈2 marks):**
- Definition: Adversary outputs some valid pair $(m^\star, \sigma^\star)$; $m^\star$ is uncontrolled.
- Example: Plain RSA existential forgery — pick random $\sigma^\star$, compute $m^\star = (\sigma^\star)^e \bmod n$. Verification succeeds trivially.
- Mitigation: Apply randomized padding (RSA-PSS).

**Step 2 — Selective Forgery (≈2 marks):**
- Definition: Adversary commits to a target $m^\star$ beforehand and produces valid $\sigma^\star$ for it.
- Example: Cube-root attack on RSA with $e=3$ and $m^\star < n^{1/3}$. Compute $\sigma^\star = \sqrt[3]{m^\star}$ over integers.
- Mitigation: Hash the message before signing (full-domain hash).

**Step 3 — Universal Forgery (≈2 marks):**
- Definition: Adversary can sign *any* message after the attack completes.
- Example: DSA nonce-reuse attack — recover $k$, then $x$, then sign arbitrarily.
- Mitigation: Use RFC 6979 deterministic nonces.

**Step 4 — Conclusion (≈1 mark):**
Universal Forgery > Selective > Existential in severity. Modern schemes target EUF-CMA as the minimum acceptable security level.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Key-Only Attack (KOA) (≈2 marks):**
Adversary possesses only the public key $pk$ and any algorithmically public parameters. Weakest realistic model. Example: Cube-root RSA attack above.

**Step 2 — Known-Message Attack (KMA) (≈2 marks):**
Adversary is given a set $\{(m_i, \sigma_i)\}$ of legitimate signatures. Cannot influence which messages were signed.

**Step 3 — Chosen-Message Attack (CMA) (≈2 marks):**
- Non-adaptive: Adversary submits all message queries in a single batch before seeing responses.
- Adaptive: Each query may depend on prior signatures — strongest practical model.
- Example: Bleichenbacher's attack on RSA-PKCS#1 v1.5 signatures used adaptive CMA.

**Step 4 — Combining to Define Security (≈1 mark):**
The notation **EUF-CMA** = Existential Unforgeability under Chosen-Message Attack = the gold-standard security goal. **sEUF-CMA** further requires that the signature itself be new. Schemes proven secure in the random oracle model (RSA-PSS, ECDSA) achieve EUF-CMA.

---

### Question B1 (B) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With reference to the RSA signature scheme, demonstrate mathematically how an existential forgery is possible on *plain* (un-hashed, un-padded) RSA. Show all derivation steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of strong existential unforgeability (sEUF-CMA). Why is it important for certain applications such as deterministic signatures (EdDSA) and Bitcoin transactions? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the RSA setup — public key $(n, e)$, private key $d$, signing: $\sigma = m^d \bmod n$, verification: $m \stackrel{?}{=} \sigma^e \bmod n$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Adversary's strategy —
- Choose arbitrary $\sigma^\star \in \mathbb{Z}_n^\star$.
- Compute $m^\star = (\sigma^\star)^e \bmod n$.
- Output $(m^\star, \sigma^\star)$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Verification check:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{V}_{pk}(m^\star, \sigma^\star) &= (m^\star)^e \bmod n \\
&= \big((\sigma^\star)^e\big)^e \bmod n \\
&= (\sigma^\star)^{e^2} \bmod n
\end{aligned}$$

By Euler's theorem, since $\gcd(\sigma^\star, n) = 1$ and $\sigma^\star \in \mathbb{Z}_n^\star$:

$$(\sigma^\star)^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$

Because $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, the verifier's check uses $d$, not $e^2$. The verification is:

$$\mathcal{V}_{pk}(m^\star, \sigma^\star) = (m^\star)^d \bmod n = ((\sigma^\star)^e)^d = \sigma^\star$$

The verifier compares $\sigma^\star$ with the original message reference — since the attacker constructed $m^\star$ to match $\sigma^\star$ under the public exponent, verification succeeds.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclusion — Plain RSA fails EUF-CMA; require randomized padding (PSS).

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Mitigation discussion — RSA-PSS uses a random salt and a hash to break the algebraic structure.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Define sEUF-CMA — the adversary must produce a new pair $(m^\star, \sigma^\star)$ where $\sigma^\star$ was *never* returned by the signing oracle, even on the same message $m^\star$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compare with EUF-CMA — EUF-CMA only requires $m^\star \notin \mathcal{Q}$ (not previously queried). sEUF-CMA additionally requires $\sigma^\star$ to be fresh.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Bitcoin / EdDSA relevance — In Bitcoin, each transaction signature uses a *unique* nonce deterministically derived (RFC 6979 for ECDSA, EdDSA-native). If an adversary could replay an existing signature on a modified transaction (same $m^\star$, different $\sigma^\star$), the malleability bug (CVE-2012-2459 and BIP 62/146) would enable transaction manipulation. sEUF-CMA prevents this.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclusion — Modern signature schemes (Ed25519) are explicitly sEUF-CMA in the random oracle / algebraic model.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — KTU Board Examiners Report These Annually:**
> 1. **Confusing forgery type with attack model** — Existential/Selective/Universal describes *message control*, while KOA/KMA/CMA describes *oracle access*. Students routinely swap these and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Writing plain RSA as "secure"** — Always explicitly state that plain RSA is existentially forgeable; security requires PSS padding.
> 3. **Forgetting the hash in RSA signature** — The signature equation must be $\sigma = H(m)^d \bmod n$, not $m^d \bmod n$.
> 4. **Skipping derivation steps in 14-mark answers** — Examiners award partial marks for intermediate algebraic transitions; missing steps can lose 3–4 marks even if the final answer is correct.
> 5. **Not citing the security goal explicitly** — Always conclude with "this fails/achieves EUF-CMA" or equivalent.
> 6. **Mixing DSA nonce-recovery formula** — Memorize $k = (H(m_1) - H(m_2))(s_1 - s_2)^{-1} \bmod q$ exactly; this is a frequently asked 7-mark derivation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- [ ] **Digital signature forgery** = producing a valid $(m^\star, \sigma^\star)$ without the private key.
- [ ] **Three forgery types in increasing severity:** Existential → Selective → Universal.
- [ ] **Existential forgery:** No control over message; classic example: plain RSA with chosen $\sigma^\star$.
- [ ] **Selective forgery:** Pre-chosen target message; example: RSA cube-root with $e=3$, small $m$.
- [ ] **Universal forgery:** Sign anything post-attack; example: DSA nonce reuse ⇒ recover $k$ ⇒ recover $x$.
- [ ] **Attack models:** KOA < KMA < CMA (non-adaptive) < CMA (adaptive).
- [ ] **EUF-CMA** = Existential Unforgeability under Adaptive CMA = industry gold-standard security goal.
- [ ] **sEUF-CMA** = strong EUF-CMA, signature itself must be new (critical for Bitcoin, EdDSA).
- [ ] **Plain RSA is broken** — always use hash + randomized padding (PSS).
- [ ] **DSA nonce reuse is catastrophic** — always use RFC 6979 or hardware RNG.
- [ ] **Higher forgery implies all lower forgeries** — universal ⇒ selective ⇒ existential.
- [ ] **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - $m^\star = (\sigma^\star)^e \bmod n$ (existential forgery on plain RSA)
  - $\sigma^\star = (m^\star)^{1/e}$ over integers if $m^\star < n^{1/e}$ (selective forgery)
  - $k = (H(m_1) - H(m_2))(s_1 - s_2)^{-1} \bmod q$ (DSA nonce recovery)
  - $x = (s \cdot k - H(m)) \cdot r^{-1} \bmod q$ (DSA private-key recovery)
- [ ] **Always state the security notion being attacked/defended** in exam answers.
- [ ] **Real-world CPS relevance:** forged firmware signatures can compromise vehicles, drones, SCADA — selective and universal forgery are *physical-safety* threats, not just cryptographic ones.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RSA Digital Signature Scheme - ElGamal Signature Scheme
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT523 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** RSA Digital Signature Scheme - ElGamal Signature Scheme

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Digital Signature — The Foundational Concept

A **Digital Signature** is a cryptographic primitive that provides **Authentication**, **Data Integrity**, and **Non-Repudiation** for a digital message. Unlike a handwritten signature, it is a mathematical value computed using the sender's **private key** and verified using the sender's **public key**. The security of modern signature schemes rests on the computational intractability of problems such as **Integer Factorization (IFP)** and the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style)**
> "A digital signature is a data string produced by a sender using their private key, which any party can verify using the corresponding public key, thereby authenticating the identity of the signer and confirming the message has not been altered in transit."

---

## 1.2 RSA Digital Signature Scheme — Formal Definition

The **RSA Signature Scheme** is the direct digital-signature analogue of the RSA public-key cryptosystem proposed by **Rivest, Shamir, and Adleman (1978)**. It is a **deterministic** signature scheme whose security reduces to the hardness of factoring the modulus **n = p × q**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Property of RSA Signatures**
> The scheme is **deterministic** — signing the same message twice with the same private key yields the **same signature**. This makes it vulnerable to existential forgery on messages of the attacker's choosing unless padding schemes (e.g., **PKCS#1 v1.5**, **PSS**) are applied.

---

## 1.3 ElGamal Signature Scheme — Formal Definition

The **ElGamal Signature Scheme**, proposed by **Taher ElGamal in 1985**, is a **probabilistic** signature scheme based on the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** in a finite multiplicative group. Unlike RSA, each signature on a given message is **non-deterministic** because a fresh random nonce is used for every signing operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Property of ElGamal Signatures**
> The scheme is **probabilistic** — signing the same message twice produces **two different valid signatures** due to a random per-message integer k. This randomness is essential: reusing k across two messages leaks the private key **x** catastrophically.

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **tamper-evident wax seal** on a medieval royal decree:

| Aspect | Wax Seal Analogy | Digital Signature Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Unique Mould** | King's personal signet ring (only the king possesses it) | Sender's **private key** (only the sender possesses it) |
| **Impression** | The wax imprint on the letter | The **signature value S** computed from the message |
| **Verification Tool** | A wax-impression reference kept by the herald | The sender's **public key** (published in a directory) |
| **Tamper Detection** | Broken seal = message was opened | **Hash mismatch** = message was altered |

> [!NOTE]
> **RSA** is analogous to a *single unique wax seal* — the same mould pressed under the same conditions always leaves the same impression. **ElGamal** is like a *seal that subtly changes its pattern each time* by mixing in a fresh pinch of colored wax from a hidden pouch (the random nonce **k**).

---

## 1.5 Why Both Schemes Matter in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)

| Threat in CPS | Mitigation via Digital Signature |
|---|---|
| Spoofed sensor data injected into SCADA network | RSA/ElGamal signature on every telemetry frame |
| Firmware update from a malicious actor pushed to PLC | Code-signing certificate using RSA-2048 or ECDSA |
| Replay of old signed commands | Timestamps + nonce inside the signed payload |
| Insider with valid credentials denying action | **Non-repudiation** of digitally signed audit logs |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 RSA Digital Signature Scheme — Three Algorithms

### 2.1.1 Key Generation (`KeyGen_RSA`)

The signer generates parameters once and uses them for all future signatures.

**Step 1 — Prime Selection:** Choose two large distinct primes **p** and **q** of roughly equal bit-length (e.g., 1024 bits each for RSA-2048).

**Step 2 — Modulus Composition:** Compute the public modulus

$$n = p \times q$$

**Step 3 — Euler's Totient:** Compute Euler's totient of n

$$\varphi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$$

**Step 4 — Public Exponent Selection:** Choose an integer **e** such that

$$1 < e < \varphi(n) \quad \text{and} \quad \gcd(e,\ \varphi(n)) = 1$$

Common choice: **e = 65537** (= 2¹⁶ + 1), a Fermat prime.

**Step 5 — Private Exponent Computation:** Compute **d** as the modular multiplicative inverse of e modulo φ(n)

$$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\varphi(n)}$$

**Output:**
- **Public Key:** `PK = (e, n)` — published in an X.509 certificate
- **Private Key:** `SK = (d, n)` — stored in HSM / TPM / secure enclave

---

### 2.1.2 Signing (`Sign_RSA`)

Given a message M, the signer produces the signature.

**Step 1 — Hash the Message:** Apply a cryptographic hash function H (e.g., SHA-256)

$$h = H(M)$$

**Step 2 — Modular Exponentiation with Private Key:** Compute

$$S = h^{\,d} \bmod n$$

**Output:** Signature **S** (typically 1024 / 2048 / 4096 bits long, equal to modulus size).

---

### 2.1.3 Verification (`Verify_RSA`)

The receiver uses the sender's public key to validate.

**Step 1 — Recompute Hash:** Compute **h' = H(M)** on the received message.

**Step 2 — Modular Exponentiation with Public Key:** Compute

$$M' = S^{\,e} \bmod n$$

**Step 3 — Comparison:** Accept the signature if and only if

$$M' \stackrel{?}{=} h' \quad \text{(mod } n\text{)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correctness Justification**
> Because e·d ≡ 1 (mod φ(n)), Euler's theorem guarantees that for any h coprime to n:
> $$S^{e} \bmod n = (h^{d})^{e} \bmod n = h^{de} \bmod n = h^{1 + k\varphi(n)} \bmod n = h \cdot (h^{\varphi(n)})^{k} \bmod n = h \bmod n$$

---

## 2.2 ElGamal Signature Scheme — Three Algorithms

### 2.2.1 Key Generation (`KeyGen_ElGamal`)

**Step 1 — Prime Field:** Choose a large prime **p** (≥ 1024 bits).

**Step 2 — Generator:** Choose **g**, a generator of the multiplicative group $\mathbb{Z}_{p}^{\*}$ (i.e., a primitive root modulo p).

**Step 3 — Secret Key:** Choose a random integer **x** such that

$$1 \le x \le p-2$$

**Step 4 — Public Key Component:** Compute

$$y = g^{\,x} \bmod p$$

**Output:**
- **Public Key:** `PK = (p, g, y)`
- **Private Key:** `SK = x`

---

### 2.2.2 Signing (`Sign_ElGamal`)

To sign a message M:

**Step 1 — Hash the Message:** Compute **h = H(M)** (e.g., SHA-256 interpreted as an integer mod (p−1)).

**Step 2 — Choose Per-Message Random Nonce:** Choose a fresh random integer **k** such that

$$1 \le k \le p-2 \quad \text{and} \quad \gcd(k,\ p-1) = 1$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Security Rule:** The value k must be (a) **truly random**, (b) **secret**, and (c) **never reused** across two different messages. Reuse of k leaks the private key x.

**Step 3 — Compute the First Signature Component:**

$$r = g^{\,k} \bmod p$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Second Signature Component:** Find the modular inverse of k mod (p−1) and compute

$$s = (h - x \cdot r) \cdot k^{-1} \bmod (p-1)$$

**Output:** Signature pair **(r, s)**.

---

### 2.2.3 Verification (`Verify_ElGamal`)

The verifier recomputes and compares.

**Step 1 — Recompute Hash:** Compute **h' = H(M)**.

**Step 2 — Compute Left-Hand Side:**

$$V_1 = y^{\,r} \cdot r^{\,s} \bmod p$$

**Step 3 — Compute Right-Hand Side:**

$$V_2 = g^{\,h'} \bmod p$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:** Accept the signature if and only if

$$V_1 \equiv V_2 \pmod{p}$$

---

### 2.2.4 Correctness Proof Sketch

$$V_1 = y^{r} \cdot r^{s} \bmod p = (g^{x})^{r} \cdot (g^{k})^{s} \bmod p = g^{\,xr} \cdot g^{\,ks} \bmod p$$

Substituting the signing equation $ks \equiv h - xr \pmod{p-1}$:

$$V_1 = g^{\,xr + ks} \bmod p = g^{\,h} \bmod p = V_2$$

using Fermat's Little Theorem for the exponent reduction modulo p−1.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **examination-critical**. Master the boxed quantities for any 7- or 14-mark derivation question.

| # | Scheme | Quantity | Formula | Domain / Constraints |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | RSA | Modulus | $n = p \cdot q$ | p, q distinct large primes |
| 2 | RSA | Totient | $\varphi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | valid only for semi-prime n |
| 3 | RSA | Key Equation | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\varphi(n)}$ | $\gcd(e, \varphi(n)) = 1$ |
| 4 | RSA | Sign | $S = h^{d} \bmod n$ | $h = H(M)$ |
| 5 | RSA | Verify | $M' = S^{e} \bmod n \stackrel{?}{=} h$ | modular equality |
| 6 | ElGamal | Public Key | $y = g^{x} \bmod p$ | $1 \le x \le p-2$ |
| 7 | ElGamal | Nonce Constraint | $\gcd(k, p-1) = 1$ | ensures k invertible |
| 8 | ElGamal | First Sign Comp | $r = g^{k} \bmod p$ | $0 < r < p$ |
| 9 | ElGamal | Second Sign Comp | $s = (h - x r) k^{-1} \bmod (p-1)$ | depends on private x |
| 10 | ElGamal | Verify LHS | $V_1 = y^{r} \cdot r^{s} \bmod p$ | uses public key |
| 11 | ElGamal | Verify RHS | $V_2 = g^{h} \bmod p$ | uses generator g |
| 12 | ElGamal | Acceptance | $V_1 \equiv V_2 \pmod{p}$ | accept iff equal |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **PKI / X.509 Certificates** | RSA signatures on CA-issued certs (TLS handshake) |
| **Code Signing** | Authenticating firmware for IoT / CPS devices |
| **Document Signing** | PDF, XML-DSig, JWT tokens (RS256 / PS256) |
| **Blockchain (legacy)** | Earlier ElGamal variants in academic chains |
| **DSA / DSS Standard** | ElGamal's direct descendant standardized as **DSA (FIPS 186)** |
| **Smart Grid Authentication** | Signed metering data to prevent false-injection attacks |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical Examples** are mandatory for KTU board examinations. The following two examples are graded to the depth expected in a 14-mark question.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example A — RSA Signature (Small Parameter Set)

### Given Parameters

$$p = 5, \quad q = 11, \quad M = 10 \text{ (treat as integer hash, no hashing for toy example)}$$

### Step 1 — Compute the Modulus

$$n = p \times q = 5 \times 11 = 55$$

### Step 2 — Compute Euler's Totient

$$\varphi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 4 \times 10 = 40$$

### Step 3 — Choose Public Exponent e

We need e such that $\gcd(e, 40) = 1$ and $1 < e < 40$.

Try $e = 7$:
$\gcd(7, 40) = 1$ ✓

So $e = 7$.

### Step 4 — Compute Private Exponent d

We must solve $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$.

Use the extended Euclidean algorithm:

$$\begin{aligned}
40 &= 5 \times 7 + 5 \\
7 &= 1 \times 5 + 2 \\
5 &= 2 \times 2 + 1 \\
2 &= 2 \times 1 + 0
\end{aligned}$$

Back-substitute:

$$\begin{aligned}
1 &= 5 - 2 \times 2 \\
  &= 5 - 2 \times (7 - 1 \times 5) = 3 \times 5 - 2 \times 7 \\
  &= 3 \times (40 - 5 \times 7) - 2 \times 7 = 3 \times 40 - 17 \times 7
\end{aligned}$$

So $-17 \times 7 \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$, meaning $d \equiv -17 \equiv 23 \pmod{40}$.

$$\boxed{d = 23}$$

### Step 5 — Sign the Message M = 10

$$S = M^{d} \bmod n = 10^{23} \bmod 55$$

Compute via repeated squaring:

$$\begin{aligned}
10^{1} &\equiv 10 \pmod{55} \\
10^{2} &\equiv 100 \equiv 45 \pmod{55} \\
10^{4} &\equiv 45^{2} = 2025 \equiv 2025 - 36 \times 55 = 2025 - 1980 = 45 \pmod{55} \\
10^{8} &\equiv 45^{2} = 2025 \equiv 45 \pmod{55} \\
10^{16} &\equiv 45^{2} = 2025 \equiv 45 \pmod{55}
\end{aligned}$$

Now combine exponents (23 = 16 + 4 + 2 + 1):

$$\begin{aligned}
10^{23} &\equiv 10^{16} \times 10^{4} \times 10^{2} \times 10^{1} \pmod{55} \\
        &\equiv 45 \times 45 \times 45 \times 10 \pmod{55} \\
        &\equiv 45 \times 45 \times 450 \pmod{55}
\end{aligned}$$

Compute $45 \times 45 = 2025 \equiv 45 \pmod{55}$.

Then $45 \times 450 = 20250 \pmod{55}$: $20250 / 55 = 368.18$, so $55 \times 368 = 20240$ and $20250 - 20240 = 10$.

$$\boxed{S = 10}$$

### Step 6 — Verify the Signature

Compute $M' = S^{e} \bmod n = 10^{7} \bmod 55$.

$$\begin{aligned}
10^{1} &\equiv 10 \\
10^{2} &\equiv 45 \\
10^{4} &\equiv 45
\end{aligned}$$

Now $7 = 4 + 2 + 1$:

$$10^{7} \equiv 10^{4} \times 10^{2} \times 10^{1} = 45 \times 45 \times 10 = 20250 \equiv 10 \pmod{55}$$

Since $M' = 10 = M$, the signature **verifies successfully**. ✓

---

## 3.2 Worked Example B — ElGamal Signature (Small Parameter Set)

### Given Parameters

$$p = 23, \quad g = 5, \quad x = 7, \quad M = 10$$

### Step 1 — Key Generation

Compute the public-key component:

$$y = g^{x} \bmod p = 5^{7} \bmod 23$$

Compute by repeated squaring:

$$\begin{aligned}
5^{1} &\equiv 5 \pmod{23} \\
5^{2} &\equiv 25 \equiv 2 \pmod{23} \\
5^{4} &\equiv 2^{2} = 4 \pmod{23} \\
5^{7} &\equiv 5^{4} \times 5^{2} \times 5^{1} = 4 \times 2 \times 5 = 40 \equiv 17 \pmod{23}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Public Key} = (p = 23,\ g = 5,\ y = 17), \quad \text{Private Key} = x = 7}$$

### Step 2 — Signing — Choose Nonce

Pick $k = 3$ such that $\gcd(3, p-1) = \gcd(3, 22) = 1$ ✓

### Step 3 — Compute r

$$r = g^{k} \bmod p = 5^{3} \bmod 23 = 125 \bmod 23$$

$23 \times 5 = 115$, so $125 - 115 = 10$.

$$\boxed{r = 10}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Modular Inverse $k^{-1} \bmod (p-1)$

Find $k^{-1}$ such that $3 \cdot k^{-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{22}$.

Test: $3 \times 15 = 45 = 2 \times 22 + 1$ ✓

$$\boxed{k^{-1} = 15}$$

### Step 5 — Compute s

$$s = (M - x \cdot r) \cdot k^{-1} \bmod (p-1)$$

Compute $x \cdot r = 7 \times 10 = 70 \equiv 70 - 3 \times 22 = 70 - 66 = 4 \pmod{22}$.

Then $M - x \cdot r = 10 - 4 = 6 \pmod{22}$.

Finally, $s = 6 \times 15 \bmod 22 = 90 \bmod 22 = 90 - 4 \times 22 = 90 - 88 = 2$.

$$\boxed{s = 2}$$

The signature on message M = 10 is the pair **(r, s) = (10, 2)**.

### Step 6 — Verification — Compute $V_1$

$$V_1 = y^{r} \cdot r^{s} \bmod p = 17^{10} \cdot 10^{2} \bmod 23$$

**Compute $17^{10} \bmod 23$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
17^{1} &\equiv 17 \pmod{23} \\
17^{2} &\equiv 289 \pmod{23},\quad 23 \times 12 = 276,\quad 289 - 276 = 13 \\
17^{4} &\equiv 13^{2} = 169 \pmod{23},\quad 23 \times 7 = 161,\quad 169 - 161 = 8 \\
17^{8} &\equiv 8^{2} = 64 \pmod{23},\quad 23 \times 2 = 46,\quad 64 - 46 = 18 \\
17^{10} &\equiv 17^{8} \times 17^{2} = 18 \times 13 = 234 \pmod{23},\quad 23 \times 10 = 230,\quad 234 - 230 = 4
\end{aligned}$$

**Compute $10^{2} \bmod 23$:**

$$10^{2} = 100 \equiv 100 - 4 \times 23 = 100 - 92 = 8 \pmod{23}$$

**Combine:**

$$V_1 = 4 \times 8 = 32 \equiv 32 - 23 = 9 \pmod{23}$$

$$\boxed{V_1 = 9}$$

### Step 7 — Verification — Compute $V_2$

$$V_2 = g^{M} \bmod p = 5^{10} \bmod 23$$

$$\begin{aligned}
5^{1} &\equiv 5 \\
5^{2} &\equiv 2 \\
5^{4} &\equiv 4 \\
5^{8} &\equiv 16 \\
5^{10} &\equiv 5^{8} \times 5^{2} = 16 \times 2 = 32 \equiv 9 \pmod{23}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{V_2 = 9}$$

### Step 8 — Compare

Since $V_1 = 9 \equiv V_2 = 9 \pmod{23}$, the signature **verifies successfully**. ✓

---

## 3.3 Side-by-Side Analytical Matrix

| Property | RSA Signature | ElGamal Signature |
|---|---|---|
| **Hard Problem** | Integer Factorization | Discrete Logarithm |
| **Key Output Size** | One pair (e, n) | Public tuple (p, g, y) |
| **Signature Length** | $\vert n \vert$ bits (1 component) | $2 \cdot \vert p \vert$ bits (2 components) |
| **Determinism** | Deterministic | Probabilistic (per-message nonce) |
| **Signature Size vs Message** | Same size as modulus | Roughly double the modulus size |
| **Signing Cost** | 1 modular exponentiation | 2 modular exponentiations |
| **Verification Cost** | 1 modular exponentiation | 3 modular exponentiations |
| **Key Reuse Across Users** | Independent per user | Independent per user |
| **Standardised Form** | PKCS#1 v1.5, RSA-PSS | DSA, ECDSA (elliptic variant) |
| **Vulnerability** | Bleichenbacher-style padding attacks | Nonce reuse → private key leak |
| **Recovery of M from S** | Yes (signature recovers message) | No (message required separately) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RSA Signature Scheme — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start RSA Signature Process] --> A1[Sender Selects Two Large Primes p and q]
    A1 --> A2[Compute n = p times q]
    A2 --> A3[Compute phi n equals p-1 times q-1]
    A3 --> A4[Select e with gcd e phi n equals 1]
    A4 --> A5[Compute d such that e times d mod phi n equals 1]
    A5 --> A6[Publish Public Key e and n]
    A6 --> A7[Keep Private Key d and n Secret]
    A7 --> A8[Sender Hashes Message h equals H of M]
    A8 --> A9[Compute Signature S equals h power d mod n]
    A9 --> A10[Transmit Message M and Signature S]
    A10 --> A11[Receiver Hashes Received M to get h prime]
    A11 --> A12[Compute M double prime equals S power e mod n]
    A12 --> A13{Compare h prime and M double prime mod n}
    A13 -->|Equal| A14[Signature ACCEPTED Message Authentic]
    A13 -->|Not Equal| A15[Signature REJECTED Tampered or Forged]
```

## 4.2 ElGamal Signature Scheme — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Start ElGamal Signature Process] --> B1[Choose Large Prime p and Generator g]
    B1 --> B2[Choose Private Key x in 1 to p-2]
    B2 --> B3[Compute Public Component y equals g power x mod p]
    B3 --> B4[Publish Public Key p g y]
    B4 --> B5[Keep Private Key x Secret]
    B5 --> B6[Sender Hashes Message h equals H of M]
    B6 --> B7[Choose Fresh Random k with gcd k p-1 equals 1]
    B7 --> B8[Compute r equals g power k mod p]
    B8 --> B9[Compute Inverse k_inv of k mod p-1]
    B9 --> B10[Compute s equals h minus x times r times k_inv mod p-1]
    B10 --> B11[Transmit Message M and Signature Pair r s]
    B11 --> B12[Receiver Hashes Received M to get h prime]
    B12 --> B13[Compute V1 equals y power r times r power s mod p]
    B13 --> B14[Compute V2 equals g power h prime mod p]
    B14 --> B15{Compare V1 and V2 mod p}
    B15 -->|Equal| B16[Signature ACCEPTED]
    B15 -->|Not Equal| B17[Signature REJECTED]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Key Roles in CPS Deployment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sender_Side
        MSG[Sensor Reading or Firmware M] --> HSIG[Hash Function SHA-256]
        HSIG --> SIG[Signature Algorithm RSA or ElGamal]
        KPRIV[Private Key in HSM] --> SIG
    end
    SIG --> CHAN[Insecure CPS Network Channel]
    CHAN --> VREC[Verifier in SCADA Gateway]
    KPUB[Public Key in X.509 Cert] --> VREC
    VREC --> DEC{Decision Accept or Reject}
    DEC -->|Accept| ACK[Authenticated Telemetry to Controller]
    DEC -->|Reject| DROP[Quarantine and Alert SOC]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In a **Cyber-Physical System**, the *sender* is typically a sensor, PLC, or field device; the *verifier* is a SCADA gateway, edge controller, or cloud aggregator. The private key never leaves the device's **Hardware Security Module (HSM)** or **TPM 2.0** chip.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, June 2024 Style]**
> List any **three differences** between the RSA signature scheme and the ElGamal signature scheme.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per valid difference):**

1. **Hardness Assumption:** RSA security relies on the **Integer Factorization Problem**, whereas ElGamal security relies on the **Discrete Logarithm Problem**. *(1 mark)*

2. **Determinism:** RSA is **deterministic** — the same message always produces the same signature; ElGamal is **probabilistic** — each signing uses a fresh random nonce k, producing different signatures for the same message. *(1 mark)*

3. **Signature Size:** RSA produces a **single component** signature of length equal to the modulus n, whereas ElGamal produces a **two-component** signature (r, s), roughly doubling the output length. *(1 mark)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Style]**
> Why must the per-message integer **k** in the ElGamal signature scheme be unique, secret, and coprime to **p − 1**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Coprime to p−1:** This condition $\gcd(k, p-1) = 1$ guarantees that k has a **modular multiplicative inverse** $k^{-1}$ modulo (p−1), which is essential for computing s. If k shared a factor with p−1, s could not be uniquely determined. *(1 mark)*

- **Uniqueness (No Reuse):** If the same k is used to sign two different messages $M_1$ and $M_2$, an attacker can algebraically eliminate k and recover the private key **x**. Hence k must be freshly generated for every signature. *(1 mark)*

- **Secrecy:** The value k appears in the verification equation only through the public component r = gᵏ mod p. Knowledge of k allows an attacker to forge signatures. Hence k must be destroyed immediately after signing. *(1 mark)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **one full question** out of a choice. We provide **Question A** and **Question B** as alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — RSA Full Lifecycle

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3 Weightage]**

**(a)** In an RSA signature scheme, a user chooses $p = 7$ and $q = 11$. Compute the public and private keys, selecting $e = 13$. Explain why this choice of e is valid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using your keys from part (a), sign the message $M = 15$ (treated as an integer hash) and then verify the signature, showing every modular arithmetic step. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q-A (a) — Key Generation

**Step 1 — Modulus:**

$$n = p \times q = 7 \times 11 = 77$$

**Step 2 — Euler's Totient:**

$$\varphi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 6 \times 10 = 60$$

**Step 3 — Validity of e = 13:**

Check the GCD: $\gcd(13, 60) = ?$

Apply Euclidean algorithm:
- $60 = 4 \times 13 + 8$
- $13 = 1 \times 8 + 5$
- $8 = 1 \times 5 + 3$
- $5 = 1 \times 3 + 2$
- $3 = 1 \times 2 + 1$
- $2 = 2 \times 1 + 0$

The last non-zero remainder is 1, so $\gcd(13, 60) = 1$. ✓

Therefore $e = 13$ is a valid public exponent because it is coprime to $\varphi(n) = 60$, ensuring a unique inverse $d$ exists. *(2 marks for GCD verification)*

**Step 4 — Compute d via Extended Euclidean Algorithm:**

Solve $13d \equiv 1 \pmod{60}$.

From the back-substitution above:

$$\begin{aligned}
1 &= 3 - 1 \times 2 \\
  &= 3 - 1 \times (5 - 1 \times 3) = 2 \times 3 - 1 \times 5 \\
  &= 2 \times (8 - 1 \times 5) - 1 \times 5 = 2 \times 8 - 3 \times 5 \\
  &= 2 \times 8 - 3 \times (13 - 1 \times 8) = 5 \times 8 - 3 \times 13 \\
  &= 5 \times (60 - 4 \times 13) - 3 \times 13 = 5 \times 60 - 23 \times 13
\end{aligned}$$

Hence $-23 \times 13 \equiv 1 \pmod{60}$, giving $d \equiv -23 \equiv 37 \pmod{60}$.

$$\boxed{\text{Public Key} = (e, n) = (13, 77), \quad \text{Private Key} = (d, n) = (37, 77)}$$

*(5 marks for full derivation of d, n, φ(n))*

---

#### Solution to Q-A (b) — Sign and Verify

**Signing M = 15:**

$$S = M^{d} \bmod n = 15^{37} \bmod 77$$

Compute powers of 15 mod 77:

$$\begin{aligned}
15^{1} &\equiv 15 \pmod{77} \\
15^{2} &\equiv 225 \pmod{77},\quad 77 \times 2 = 154,\quad 225 - 154 = 71 \\
15^{4} &\equiv 71^{2} = 5041 \pmod{77},\quad 77 \times 65 = 5005,\quad 5041 - 5005 = 36 \\
15^{8} &\equiv 36^{2} = 1296 \pmod{77},\quad 77 \times 16 = 1232,\quad 1296 - 1232 = 64 \\
15^{16} &\equiv 64^{2} = 4096 \pmod{77},\quad 77 \times 53 = 4081,\quad 4096 - 4081 = 15 \\
15^{32} &\equiv 15^{2} = 71 \pmod{77}
\end{aligned}$$

Now express $37 = 32 + 4 + 1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
15^{37} &\equiv 15^{32} \times 15^{4} \times 15^{1} \pmod{77} \\
        &\equiv 71 \times 36 \times 15 \pmod{77} \\
        &\equiv 71 \times 540 \pmod{77}
\end{aligned}$$

Compute $71 \times 540$: $71 \times 540 = 38340$.

$38340 / 77 = 497.92$, so $77 \times 497 = 38269$, and $38340 - 38269 = 71$.

$$\boxed{S = 71}$$

*(3 marks for sign computation)*

**Verification:**

Compute $M' = S^{e} \bmod n = 71^{13} \bmod 77$.

$$\begin{aligned}
71^{1} &\equiv 71 \pmod{77} \\
71^{2} &\equiv 5041 \equiv 36 \pmod{77} \text{ (as above)} \\
71^{4} &\equiv 36^{2} = 1296 \equiv 64 \pmod{77} \\
71^{8} &\equiv 64^{2} = 4096 \equiv 15 \pmod{77}
\end{aligned}$$

Now express $13 = 8 + 4 + 1$:
