## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Two SAs per Bidirectional Session

Let $S$ be a single Security Association. By IETF definition, an SA is a **simplex** relationship — it defines security services for traffic flowing in **one direction only**.

**Step 1:** Consider two hosts, $A$ and $B$, exchanging data.

**Step 2:** For traffic $A \rightarrow B$, we need one SA — call it $S_{AB}$ — with SPI value $\text{SPI}_{AB}$.

**Step 3:** For the reverse traffic $B \rightarrow A$, a *separate* SA $S_{BA}$ is required because:
   - Different SPI values (the receiving end chooses SPI).
   - Different destination IP address (when viewed from receiver's SAD).
   - Possibly different keys (forward and reverse keys in IKEv2 are derived independently).
   - Different anti-replay sequence number state.

**Step 4:** Therefore, the total number of SAs for a full-duplex AH session = 2, and for ESP = 2. If both AH and ESP are used together, then:

$$
N_{\text{SAs}} = 2 \times N_{\text{protocols}} = 2 \times 2 = 4
$$

**Conclusion:** A bidirectional IPSec session between two peers using both AH and ESP requires **four SAs** in the SAD.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: HMAC-Based ICV Computation for AH

Let $M$ denote the IP packet bytes over which the ICV is computed (with mutable fields zeroed). Let $K$ be the secret authentication key (typically 128 bits for HMAC-MD5 or 256 bits for HMAC-SHA-256).

The ICV is computed as:

$$
\text{ICV} = \text{HMAC}_K(M)
$$

Where HMAC is defined (RFC 2104) as:

$$
\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H \left( (K \oplus opad) \; \Vert \; H((K \oplus ipad) \; \Vert \; M) \right)
$$

With:
- $H$ = underlying hash function (MD5 or SHA-256).
- $ipad$ = `0x36` repeated to block length $B$ (64 bytes for MD5/SHA-256).
- $opad$ = `0x5C` repeated to block length $B$.

**Step 1 — Key Padding:** If $|K| < B$, pad $K$ to $B$ bytes with zeros:

$$
K^+ = K \; \Vert \; 0^{B - \vert K \vert}
$$

**Step 2 — Inner Hash:** Compute:

$$
H_{\text{inner}} = H(K^+ \oplus ipad \; \Vert \; M)
$$

**Step 3 — Outer Hash:** Compute:

$$
\text{HMAC} = H((K^+ \oplus opad) \; \Vert \; H_{\text{inner}})
$$

**Step 4 — Truncation:** For HMAC-MD5-96, truncate the 128-bit output to **96 bits** (the first 96 bits of the output):

$$
\text{ICV} = \text{HMAC}[0:96]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The 96-bit ICV is the *minimum mandatory* length per RFC 4303. Modern deployments use 128-bit or 256-bit ICVs.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Anti-Replay Sliding Window

Let $W = 64$ be the window size. Let $N_{\max}$ be the highest sequence number received so far.

**Step 1 — Window Boundaries:**

$$
W_{\text{right}} = N_{\max}
$$

$$
W_{\text{left}} = N_{\max} - W + 1
$$

**Step 2 — Packet Handling Rule** for an incoming packet with sequence number $N$:

- **If $N > W_{\text{right}}$:** Packet is *new*. Update $W_{\text{right}} \leftarrow N$ and shift the bitmap.
- **If $W_{\text{left}} \leq N \leq W_{\text{right}}$:** Check bitmap. If not yet received, accept and mark; else **DISCARD as replay**.
- **If $N < W_{\text{left}}$:** **DISCARD as replay** (packet is too old).

---

### 3.4 Derivation: DDoS Bandwidth Aggregation (Botnet Flood Model)

Let a botnet consist of $n$ zombie machines. Each zombie generates attack traffic at rate $r_i$ (packets per second) with average packet size $s_i$ bytes.

**Step 1 — Per-Zombie Throughput:**

$$
B_i = r_i \times s_i \quad \text{(bytes per second)}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Attack Bandwidth:**

$$
B_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} B_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i \cdot s_i
$$

**Step 3 — Amplification Factor for Reflective Attacks:**

Let $R$ = response size (bytes) and $Q$ = request size (bytes):

$$
\text{AF} = \frac{R}{Q}
$$

**Step 4 — Effective Attack Throughput with Amplification:**

$$
B_{\text{effective}} = B_{\text{total}} \times \text{AF}
$$

**Example:** DNS amplification uses AF $\approx$ 28–54. A modest 10 Gbps botnet can therefore produce an effective 280–540 Gbps attack against the target.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: AH vs ESP Comparison

| Criterion | Authentication Header (AH) | Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP) |
|---|---|---|
| **Integrity** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Authentication** | ✅ Yes (origin) | ✅ Yes (origin) |
| **Confidentiality** | ❌ No encryption | ✅ Yes (AES, 3DES, ChaCha20) |
| **Anti-Replay** | ✅ Yes (sequence number) | ✅ Yes (sequence number) |
| **IP Header Protection** | ✅ Yes (partial — excludes mutable fields) | ❌ No (in Transport Mode); ✅ Yes (in Tunnel Mode, encrypted) |
| **NAT Traversal** | ❌ Fails (checksum/hash breaks) | ✅ Works with ESP-NULL or NAT-T (UDP 4500) |
| **Protocol Number (IP)** | **51** | **50** |
| **Typical Use** | Integrity-only scenarios (rare today) | Default choice for VPNs and most deployments |
| **Overhead** | ~24 bytes + ICV (96+ bits) | ~10 bytes header + ICV + padding |
| **IPSec Mode Support** | Transport + Tunnel | Transport + Tunnel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often think "AH is more secure because it protects the IP header." Reality: AH's IP header protection **breaks NAT traversal**, so ESP is almost universally preferred in real-world deployments. AH is largely deprecated for VPN use but remains in the syllabus for theoretical completeness.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: IPSec Modes Visualized

| Mode | AH Coverage | ESP Coverage | New IP Header Added? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Transport Mode AH** | IP-Hdr + AH + Payload | N/A | ❌ No |
| **Transport Mode ESP** | N/A | ESP-Hdr + Encrypted(Payload) + ESP-Auth | ❌ No |
| **Tunnel Mode AH** | New-IP + AH + Old-IP + Payload | N/A | ✅ Yes |
| **Tunnel Mode ESP** | N/A | New-IP + ESP-Hdr + Encrypted(Old-IP + Payload) + ESP-Auth | ✅ Yes |

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix: DDoS Defense Strategies

| Attack Type | Detection Method | Mitigation Strategy | IPSec Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SYN Flood** | Unusually high half-open connections | SYN cookies, rate-limiting, firewalls | ESP prevents spoofed source IPs |
| **UDP Flood** | Bandwidth saturation monitoring | Traffic scrubbing, upstream filtering | AH/ESP authenticates source |
| **DNS Amplification** | Sudden DNS response spike | Response Rate Limiting (RRL), BCP 38 | ESP-NULL for DNS query authentication |
| **HTTP GET/POST Flood** | Anomalous request patterns | CAPTCHA, WAF, behavioral analysis | HTTPS via ESP protects URL privacy |
| **Smurf Attack** | ICMP echo replies from many hosts | Disable directed broadcast (RFC 2644) | AH prevents ICMP payload tampering |
| **NTP Amplification** | Large UDP packets on port 123 | Upgrade to NTPv4, monitor monlist | ESP authenticates NTP servers |

---

### 3.8 Step-by-Step: SYN Flood Attack Mechanism

1. **Attacker Action:** Attacker sends a flood of TCP **SYN packets** to the target server, each with a **spoofed source IP address**.
2. **Server Response:** Server allocates a **half-open connection** in its TCP state table and replies with a **SYN-ACK** to the spoofed address.
3. **No Completion:** Since the spoofed host never replies with an **ACK**, the half-open connection remains.
4. **Resource Exhaustion:** The server's TCP state table fills up; legitimate SYN requests are refused → **Denial of Service**.
5. **Amplification:** Each SYN consumes ~**2 KB of kernel memory** in the TCB (Transmission Control Block). With 100,000 half-open connections, this is ~200 MB of wasted memory.

**Defense:** **SYN Cookies** (encode TCP state in the SYN-ACK sequence number) and **IPSec AH** (which authenticates source IP, making spoofing harder — though not impossible without end-to-end deployment).

---

### 3.9 Step-by-Step: ESP Encryption Process (AES-CBC Mode)

Given plaintext block $P_i$ (128 bits for AES), key $K$, and initialization vector $IV$:

**Step 1:** For the first block, XOR with the IV:

$$
X_1 = P_1 \oplus IV
$$

**Step 2:** Encrypt with AES using key $K$:

$$
C_1 = E_K(X_1) = E_K(P_1 \oplus IV)
$$

**Step 3:** For subsequent blocks, use the previous ciphertext:

$$
C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1}), \quad \text{for } i \geq 2
$$

**Step 4:** ESP adds padding to ensure the final plaintext block is a multiple of the cipher's block size (16 bytes for AES).

**Step 5:** The receiver decrypts by reversing the process:

$$
P_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1}
$$

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define IPSec. List the two main protocols of IPSec and state one key difference between them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** IPSec (Internet Protocol Security) is a suite of IETF-standardized protocols (RFC 4301–4303) that operate at the Network Layer (Layer 3) of the OSI model to secure IP communications by providing confidentiality, data integrity, authentication, and anti-replay protection.

**Two main protocols (1 mark):**
1. **Authentication Header (AH)** — Protocol number 51.
2. **Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP)** — Protocol number 50.

**Key difference (1 mark):** AH provides only **authentication and integrity** (no encryption), whereas ESP provides **confidentiality, authentication, and integrity** (i.e., encryption is mandatory in ESP).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is a DDoS attack? Name any two types of DDoS attacks.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack is a coordinated attack in which multiple compromised systems (called *zombies* or *bots*) flood a target server, service, or network with massive traffic, rendering it unavailable to legitimate users.

**Two types (2 marks):**
1. **SYN Flood Attack** — A protocol attack that exhausts the target's TCP state table by sending numerous spoofed SYN packets.
2. **DNS Amplification Attack** — A volume-based attack that exploits open DNS resolvers to multiply traffic by a factor of 28–54× toward the target.

*(Acceptable alternatives: UDP Flood, ICMP Flood, HTTP GET Flood, Smurf Attack, Ping of Death, Slowloris.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **Authentication Header (AH)** protocol of IPSec in detail with a neat diagram of the AH header format. Discuss how AH provides **data integrity and authentication** using HMAC. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **Tunnel Mode** in IPSec? With a diagram, explain how an **ESP-protected packet in Tunnel Mode** differs from a packet in **Transport Mode**. List **two advantages** of Tunnel Mode. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a):**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
The Authentication Header (AH) is an IPSec protocol (IP Protocol 51) defined in RFC 4302. It provides:
- **Data Origin Authentication** — proves the sender's identity.
- **Connectionless Data Integrity** — detects any modification in transit.
- **Optional Anti-Replay Protection** — via the 32-bit Sequence Number.

**Step 2 — AH Header Format (2 marks):**

| Field | Size |
|---|---|
| Next Header | 8 bits |
| Payload Length | 8 bits |
| Reserved | 16 bits |
| SPI | 32 bits |
| Sequence Number | 32 bits |
| Authentication Data (ICV) | Variable (≥ 96 bits) |

**Diagram:**

```
+----------+-------------+-----------+--------+----------+----------+
| Next Hdr | Payload Len | Reserved  |  SPI   | Seq Num  |   ICV    |
| 8 bits   |  8 bits     | 16 bits   | 32 bits| 32 bits  | ≥ 96 bits|
+----------+-------------+-----------+--------+----------+----------+
```

**Step 3 — HMAC Computation (2 marks):**
AH uses **HMAC** (Hash-based Message Authentication Code) to compute the ICV. The ICV is calculated over:
- The **IP header** (with mutable fields like TTL zeroed out).
- The **AH header** itself (with ICV field set to zero during computation).
- The **upper-layer payload** (TCP/UDP header + data).

The formula is:

$$
\text{ICV} = \text{HMAC}_K(M)
$$

Where $K$ is a secret key shared between sender and receiver (negotiated via IKE), and $M$ is the message. Common hash functions: HMAC-MD5-96, HMAC-SHA-1-96, **HMAC-SHA-256** (modern standard).

**Step 4 — Why HMAC? (1 mark):**
HMAC is used because it:
- Is resistant to **length-extension attacks** (unlike plain $H(K \Vert M)$).
- Provides **provable security** based on the underlying hash function's strength.
- Is **fast** and widely hardware-accelerated.

**Step 5 — Verification at Receiver (1 mark):**
The receiver recomputes the HMAC over the same fields using the same shared key and compares it to the received ICV. If they match, integrity and authenticity are verified; otherwise, the packet is **dropped silently** (to prevent oracle attacks).

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(b):**

**Step 1 — Definition of Tunnel Mode (2 marks):**
In **Tunnel Mode**, the **entire original IP datagram** (including the original IP header) is encapsulated within a *new* IP packet that has a *new* outer IP header. The IPSec header (AH or ESP) is inserted *between* the new outer IP header and the original inner IP header. This mode is typically used between **security gateways** (e.g., VPN concentrators, firewalls) to create site-to-site VPNs.

**Step 2 — Diagram Comparison (2 marks):**

**Transport Mode ESP:**

```
[Orig IP Hdr][ESP Hdr][Encrypted Payload][ESP Trailer][ESP Auth]
```

**Tunnel Mode ESP:**

```
[NEW IP Hdr][ESP Hdr][Encrypted (Orig IP Hdr + Payload)][ESP Trailer][ESP Auth]
```

**Step 3 — Key Differences (1 mark):**
- Transport Mode preserves the original IP header; Tunnel Mode adds a new outer IP header.
- In Transport Mode, only the **payload** is encrypted. In Tunnel Mode, the **original IP header is also encrypted**, hiding source/destination addresses from eavesdroppers.

**Step 4 — Two Advantages of Tunnel Mode (2 marks):**
1. **Privacy of Endpoints:** The original source and destination IP addresses are encrypted, so an external observer cannot determine which two hosts are communicating. This is essential for VPN privacy.
2. **Gateway Interoperability:** Tunnel Mode allows hosts that do *not* themselves run IPSec to benefit from IPSec protection — the security gateways handle the encapsulation. Thus, a non-IPSec-aware device behind a VPN gateway can still have its traffic protected.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Explain the **Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP)** protocol of IPSec in detail. Discuss the structure of an **ESP packet** and explain how ESP provides **confidentiality using AES in CBC mode**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **DDoS attack**? Explain the **SYN Flood attack** in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss **four defense mechanisms** against SYN Flood attacks. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(a):**

**Step 1 — Definition of ESP (1 mark):**
ESP is an IPSec protocol (IP Protocol 50) defined in RFC 4303. It provides:
- **Confidentiality** (encryption — mandatory)
- **Data Integrity** (HMAC-based ICV)
- **Data Origin Authentication**
- **Anti-replay protection**

ESP can be used in both **Transport Mode** and **Tunnel Mode**.

**Step 2 — ESP Packet Structure (2 marks):**

| Component | Contents |
|---|---|
| **ESP Header** | SPI (32 bits) + Sequence Number (32 bits) |
| **ESP Payload** | Original IP datagram (encrypted) + padding |
| **ESP Trailer** | Padding (0–255 bytes) + Pad Length (8 bits) + Next Header (8 bits) |
| **ESP Auth Trailer** | ICV (Integrity Check Value, ≥ 96 bits) |

**Diagram:**

```
+--------+----------------+-----------------+--------+----------+
|ESP Hdr |  Encrypted      |  ESP Trailer    | ESP Auth|
| SPI    |  Original       |  Padding        |  ICV    |
| SeqNum |  IP Datagram    |  PadLen | NextH  |         |
+--------+----------------+-----------------+--------+----------+
```

**Step 3 — ESP Header & Sequence Number (1 mark):**
- **SPI (32 bits):** Identifies the Security Association at the receiver.
- **Sequence Number (32 bits):** Monotonically increasing counter for anti-replay.

**Step 4 — ESP Trailer (1 mark):**
The trailer contains **padding** (to align the ciphertext to a multiple of the cipher's block size — 16 bytes for AES), the **Pad Length** field, and the **Next Header** field (which tells the receiver which upper-layer protocol is contained in the payload, e.g., TCP = 6, IPv4 = 4).

**Step 5 — Confidentiality via AES-CBC (2 marks):**
ESP typically uses **AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) in CBC (Cipher Block Chaining) mode** for encryption:

$$
C_1 = E_K(P_1 \oplus IV)
$$

$$
C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1}), \quad \text{for } i = 2, 3, \ldots
$$

Where:
- $E_K$ = AES-128/192/256 block encryption.
- $IV$ = random Initialization Vector.
- $C_{i-1}$ = previous ciphertext block (provides diffusion — identical plaintext blocks produce different ciphertext blocks).

**Step 6 — Modern Recommendation:**
Newer deployments use **AES-GCM (Galois/Counter Mode)**, which combines encryption and authentication in a single AEAD (Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data) operation — providing both confidentiality and integrity in one pass, with better performance.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(b):**

**Step 1 — DDoS Definition (1 mark):**
A **Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack** is a malicious attempt by an attacker to make a target server, service, or network unavailable to its intended users by overwhelming it with a flood of Internet traffic generated from **multiple distributed compromised systems** (a *botnet*).

**Step 2 — SYN Flood Explanation (2 marks):**
A **SYN Flood** is a **Layer 4 (Transport Layer) DDoS attack** that exploits the **TCP three-way handshake**. In a normal handshake:

1. Client → Server: **SYN**
2. Server → Client: **SYN-ACK**
3. Client → Server: **ACK** (connection established)

In a SYN flood, the attacker sends thousands of **SYN packets with spoofed source IP addresses** to the target server.

**Step 3 — Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Attacker/Bot (Spoofed IP)                    Target Server
        |                                          |
        |-------- (1) SYN (src=spoofed) --------->|  [Server allocates TCB]
        |                                          |
        |<------- (2) SYN-ACK --------------------|  [Sent to spoofed IP - lost]
        |                                          |
        |   X   (3) ACK  -- never arrives          |  [Half-open connection lingers]
        |                                          |
        |-------- (1) SYN (src=spoofed2) --------->|  [Another TCB allocated]
        |                                          |
        |-------- (1) SYN (src=spoofed3) --------->|  [TCB table fills up]
        |                                          |
  ... thousands of half-open connections ...
        |                                          |
  LEGITIMATE USER:                                |
        |-------- (1) SYN ----------------------->|  [REFUSED - TCB table full!]
```

**Step 4 — Four Defense Mechanisms (2 marks = 0.5 each):**

1. **SYN Cookies:** The server does *not* allocate a TCB upon receiving a SYN. Instead, it encodes the connection state into the **SYN-ACK sequence number** as an encrypted hash. Only when a valid ACK returns is the TCB allocated. This eliminates resource exhaustion.

2. **Rate Limiting / Connection Rate Throttling:** Firewalls or IDS systems limit the number of new SYN packets accepted per second from a single source IP or subnet, preventing floods from consuming all TCB slots.

3. **Increase TCB Table Size & Reduce Timeout:** Although not a true fix, increasing the backlog queue and reducing the SYN-RECEIVED timeout (e.g., from 75s to 15s) allows the server to recover faster.

4. **IPSec Authentication Header (AH):** AH authenticates the source IP of every packet, making **IP spoofing harder** — but not impossible, as spoofing must be blocked at the *ISP level* via **BCP 38 / uRPF (Unicast Reverse Path Forwarding)** filtering.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in IPSec Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing protocol numbers:** AH = **51**, ESP = **50**. Mixing these up loses 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Saying "AH encrypts"** — AH does *not* encrypt. This is a fatal error worth **-2 marks**.
> 3. **Drawing ESP without the trailer** — the ESP *trailer* (with Pad Length and Next Header) is often forgotten by students. Always include it in diagrams.
> 4. **Claiming "an SA is bidirectional"** — SA is **simplex (unidirectional)**. A full session needs **two SAs**. This is a frequently tested 1-mark question.
> 5. **Forgetting Transport vs Tunnel difference** — Transport Mode adds the IPSec header *after* the original IP header; Tunnel Mode wraps the *entire* packet in a new IP packet. Drawing them identically costs 2–3 marks.
> 6. **SYN Flood: forgetting to mention the "spoofed source IP"** — without the spoofing, it is just a normal TCP handshake, not a flood. Examiners deduct marks for incomplete explanation.
> 7. **Writing only one example DDoS attack** — the question always expects **classification + example**. Skipping the category loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 4 — IPSec & DDoS)**

- [x] **IPSec** = Layer 3 security framework providing confidentiality, integrity, authentication, anti-replay.
- [x] **Two main protocols:** AH (Protocol 51 — integrity only) and ESP (Protocol 50 — encryption + integrity).
- [x] **AH Header = 24 bytes** (Next Hdr + Payload Len + Reserved + SPI + Seq No); ICV is variable ≥ 96 bits.
- [x] **ESP Header** = SPI + Seq No; **ESP Trailer** = Padding + Pad Len + Next Hdr; **ESP Auth** = ICV.
- [x] **Security Association (SA)** is **simplex**, identified by **(SPI, Destination IP, Protocol)**.
- [x] **Two SAs per bidirectional session; four SAs if both AH and ESP used.**
- [x] **Modes:** Transport Mode (end-to-end, host-to-host) vs Tunnel Mode (gateway-to-gateway, entire packet encapsulated).
- [x] **ICV computation:** $\text{ICV} = \text{HMAC}_K(M)$; modern: HMAC-SHA-256.
- [x] **Anti-replay window:** Default 64 packets; sequence number is 32-bit.
- [x] **AH covers IP header (excluding mutable fields); ESP does not (in Transport Mode).**
- [x] **NAT traversal fails for AH; ESP works with NAT-T (UDP 4500).**
- [x] **DDoS = Distributed Denial of Service** from multiple compromised *zombie* systems in a *botnet*.
- [x] **Three DDoS categories:** Volume-based, Protocol-based, Application-layer.
- [x] **SYN Flood** is a **Protocol attack** exploiting TCP 3-way handshake with spoofed source IPs.
- [x] **SYN Cookies** are the canonical defense for SYN Flood.
- [x] **Amplification Factor** = Response Size / Request Size; DNS amplification has AF ≈ 28–54.
- [x] **Real-world CPS use cases:** SCADA/ICS VPNs, 5G N2/N3 interfaces, IIoT gateways.
- [x] **Standard IKEv2** uses UDP port 500; NAT-T uses UDP port 4500.
- [x] **DDoS defense layers:** RTBH → Scrubbing Center → Rate Limiting → WAF.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Secure Electronic Transaction - Payment Processing - Dual Signature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT523 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Secure Electronic Transaction - Payment Processing - Dual Signature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Secure Electronic Transaction (SET) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SET (Secure Electronic Transaction)** is an open encryption and security specification jointly developed by **Visa** and **Mastercard** in **1996** to protect credit/debit card transactions over open networks such as the Internet. SET is built on top of the **X.509v3 public-key certificate standard** and uses a combination of symmetric encryption (DES for bulk data), asymmetric encryption (RSA for key exchange and digital signatures), and the **Dual Signature** mechanism to ensure confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation of all parties involved — the **Cardholder (Customer)**, the **Merchant**, the **Acquirer Bank**, the **Issuer Bank**, and the **Payment Gateway (Certificate Authority)**.

The major participants in a SET transaction are:

| Symbol | Entity | Role |
|:------:|:-------|:-----|
| C | Cardholder / Customer | Initiates purchase |
| M | Merchant | Sells goods/services |
| P | Payment Gateway | Bridge between M and Bank |
| CA | Certificate Authority | Issues X.509 certificates |
| I | Issuer Bank | Issues card to C |
| A | Acquirer Bank | Holds merchant's account |

## 1.2 Payment Processing — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Payment Processing in SET** is the multi-stage protocol by which a customer's purchase order, payment instructions, authorization, and fund-capture are securely exchanged among the Cardholder, Merchant, and Banks. It is divided into four transaction phases: **Purchase Request, Payment Authorization, Payment Authorization Response, and Payment Capture**, each using message digests, digital envelopes, and dual signatures to maintain end-to-end security.

## 1.3 Dual Signature — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dual Signature (DS)** is a cryptographic construct defined in the SET specification that cryptographically binds **two distinct messages** (one intended for the **Merchant** and one for the **Bank**) without revealing one message to the unintended party. It enables the receiver of either message to verify that the two messages are linked and were signed by the same Cardholder, while keeping the content of the other message confidential.

The two messages involved are:
- **Order Information (OI)** — sent to Merchant (contains purchase details like item list, price, quantity)
- **Payment Information (PI)** — sent to Bank (contains card number, amount, etc.)

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Sealed Envelope Trick:**
> Imagine you are buying a house. You write two letters:
> - **Letter A (OI)**: "I want to buy House #42 for ₹50 lakhs" → goes to the **Seller (Merchant)**
> - **Letter B (PI)**: "Please transfer ₹50 lakhs from my bank account" → goes to the **Bank**
>
> The Seller must NOT know your bank details, and the Bank must NOT know which house you are buying. **But the Bank must be sure the ₹50 lakhs it releases corresponds to the very same house the Seller is offering**, and vice-versa.
>
> **The Dual Signature is like a unique "fingerprint stamp"** you press on the outside of *both* sealed envelopes. The stamp is mathematically generated using *both* letters, but anyone who sees only one letter can still verify the stamp matches both. The Seller can confirm that the Bank has approved *this specific purchase*, and the Bank can confirm that *this specific order* was authorized — without ever opening the other envelope.

## 1.5 Key Properties Guaranteed by Dual Signature

1. **Confidentiality** — Merchant cannot see PI; Bank cannot see OI.
2. **Integrity** — Both OI and PI cannot be tampered with.
3. **Authentication** — The Cardholder is verified to be the originator.
4. **Linkage** — The two messages are cryptographically tied together.
5. **Non-Repudiation** — Cardholder cannot later deny authorizing the transaction.

## 1.6 Standard Cryptographic Algorithms Used in SET

| Function | Algorithm | Key Length / Mode |
|:---------|:----------|:------------------|
| Bulk data encryption | **DES (Data Encryption Standard)** | **56-bit** keys, **CBC** mode |
| Digital signatures | **RSA (Rivest-Shamir-Adleman)** | **1024-bit** minimum, **2048-bit** recommended |
| Message digest | **SHA-1 (Secure Hash Algorithm)** | **160-bit** output |
| Key exchange | **RSA key transport** | Per-session symmetric keys |
| Certificate format | **X.509v3** | Hierarchical CA structure |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Problem Solved by Dual Signature

In any credit card transaction, three crucial pieces of information must be exchanged:

1. **Order Information (OI)** — what is being bought, by whom, shipping address
2. **Payment Information (PI)** — credit card number, expiry, amount to charge
3. **The Binding** — proof that the OI and PI belong to the *same* transaction

The naive solution of sending both OI and PI to one party (e.g., the merchant) breaks confidentiality. The Dual Signature solves this elegantly.

## 2.2 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.2.1 Dual Signature Generation (at the Cardholder's side)

**Step 1:** The Cardholder composes the two plaintexts.
- $OI$ = Order Information (e.g., product list, total price, shipping)
- $PI$ = Payment Information (e.g., card number, expiry, amount)

**Step 2:** Compute the two message digests using SHA-1.

$$H(OI) = \text{SHA-1}(OI)$$

$$H(PI) = \text{SHA-1}(PI)$$

**Step 3:** Concatenate the two hashes in a specific canonical order: $H(PI) \Vert H(OI)$.

> [!NOTE]
> The canonical order **PI-hash first, then OI-hash** is mandated by the SET specification so that both parties reconstruct the same combined hash.

**Step 4:** Hash the concatenated value to produce a single combined message digest.

$$H(H(PI) \Vert H(OI))$$

**Step 5:** Sign this combined digest with the Cardholder's RSA private key $PR_C$.

$$DS = E(PR_C, \, H(H(PI) \Vert H(OI)))$$

This $DS$ is the **Dual Signature**.

### 2.2.2 Dual Signature Verification

To verify the DS, a party needs:
- The dual signature $DS$
- The Cardholder's public key $PU_C$ (from their X.509 certificate)
- The hash of the message they possess
- The hash of the message they do **not** possess (delivered separately)

**Verification Algorithm (used by both Merchant and Bank):**

**Step V1:** Decrypt DS using Cardholder's public key to recover the original combined hash.

$$H(H(PI) \Vert H(OI)) = D(PU_C, \, DS)$$

**Step V2:** Hash the locally available message to obtain $H(\text{message})$.

**Step V3:** Combine the locally-computed hash with the externally-received hash, hash the combination, and compare with Step V1 result.

> **If equal ⇒ signature is valid AND messages are linked.**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol / Operation | Formula | Meaning | Used By |
|:-:|:-------------------|:--------|:--------|:--------|
| 1 | Message digest of OI | $H(OI)$ | Hash of order info | Cardholder, Merchant |
| 2 | Message digest of PI | $H(PI)$ | Hash of payment info | Cardholder, Bank |
| 3 | Combined hash | $H(H(PI) \Vert H(OI))$ | Hash of concatenated PI-hash and OI-hash | All three |
| 4 | Dual Signature (DS) | $DS = E(PR_C, \, H(H(PI) \Vert H(OI)))$ | RSA encryption of combined hash with Cardholder's private key | Cardholder |
| 5 | DS Verification Step 1 | $H_{\text{claimed}} = D(PU_C, \, DS)$ | RSA decryption using Cardholder's public key | Merchant, Bank |
| 6 | DS Verification Step 2 | Compare $H_{\text{claimed}} \stackrel{?}{=} H(H(PI) \Vert H(OI))$ | Recompute locally and compare | Merchant, Bank |
| 7 | Digital Envelope | $E(PU_X, K_S) \Vert E(K_S, M)$ | Encrypted symmetric key + encrypted message | Cardholder → Merchant/Bank |
| 8 | Purchase Request (PI digest included) | $OI \Vert H(PI) \Vert DS$ | Sent to Merchant (encrypted) | Cardholder |
| 9 | Purchase Request (OI digest included) | $PI \Vert H(OI) \Vert DS$ | Sent to Bank (encrypted) | Cardholder |
| 10 | SHA-1 output | **160 bits** | Length of message digest | — |
| 11 | RSA minimum key | **1024 bits** | Recommended **2048 bits** | CA, Cardholder |

> **Notation Key:** $\Vert$ denotes concatenation; $E(K, M)$ denotes encryption of $M$ with key $K$; $D(K, C)$ denotes decryption; $PR$ = private key; $PU$ = public key; $K_S$ = symmetric session key; $H(\cdot)$ = SHA-1.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility and Engineering Significance

1. **E-Commerce Security** — Used in any B2C scenario where a customer, vendor, and financial institution need to transact without mutual trust.
2. **Privacy-Preserving Multi-Party Computation** — The Dual Signature is a primitive example of how a single signature can commit to multiple data items and selectively disclose verification.
3. **Block-chain and Smart Contracts** — Conceptual ancestors of dual signatures appear in **multisig schemes** and **ring signatures** where a transaction links inputs to outputs without exposing private details.
4. **Health / Government Records** — Schema where a patient record links to an insurance claim without revealing full medical data to the insurer.
5. **IoT and CPS** — In Cyber-Physical Systems, the dual-signature pattern enables a sensor to commit to both telemetry data (sent to a control center) and billing data (sent to a payment system) with one signature.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Dual Signature Generation

Let the Cardholder $C$ wish to purchase an item priced at ₹5,000 from Merchant $M$ using Bank $B$.

**Inputs (known to Cardholder only):**
- $OI$ — Order Information plaintext
- $PI$ — Payment Information plaintext
- $PR_C$ — Cardholder's RSA private key
- $PU_M$ — Merchant's RSA public key (from merchant's X.509 cert)
- $PU_B$ — Bank's RSA public key (from bank's X.509 cert)

### Step 1 — Compute individual digests

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(OI) &= \text{SHA-1}(OI) \\
H(PI) &= \text{SHA-1}(PI)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** SHA-1 produces a **160-bit** fixed-length output for arbitrarily long input. This makes the digest efficient to sign and resistant to collisions.

### Step 2 — Concatenate the two digests

$$
H(PI) \;\Vert\; H(OI)
$$

**Explanation:** Concatenation joins the two 160-bit digests into a 320-bit string. Order matters: PI-hash first, OI-hash second, per SET specification.

### Step 3 — Hash the concatenation (second-level hash)

$$
H(H(PI) \Vert H(OI))
$$

**Explanation:** The second hash compresses the 320-bit concatenation back to 160 bits, and crucially makes it **computationally infeasible** to find a different $(OI', PI')$ pair that produces the same dual signature.

### Step 4 — Apply RSA signing with Cardholder's private key

$$
DS = E(PR_C, \; H(H(PI) \Vert H(OI)))
$$

**Explanation:** This is the standard RSA signature operation: the digest is treated as an integer and raised to the $e$-th (or $d$-th) power modulo $n$. Output is a 1024-bit (or 2048-bit) signature.

### Step 5 — Assemble the two message envelopes

The Cardholder prepares two envelopes:

**Envelope to Merchant (Encrypted to Merchant's public key):**
$$
E(PU_M, K_{S1}) \;\Vert\; E(K_{S1}, \; OI \;\Vert\; H(PI) \;\Vert\; DS)
$$

**Envelope to Bank (Encrypted to Bank's public key):**
$$
E(PU_B, K_{S2}) \;\Vert\; E(K_{S2}, \; PI \;\Vert\; H(OI) \;\Vert\; DS)
$$

**Explanation:** Each envelope contains the random symmetric session key encrypted with the recipient's public key, plus the actual message encrypted with that session key using DES-CBC. The *hash of the other party's message* is included so that each receiver has the two pieces needed to verify the dual signature.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Dual Signature Verification

### 3.2.1 Verification at the Merchant Side

The Merchant receives $OI$, $H(PI)$, and $DS$ (all inside the decrypted symmetric envelope).

**Step M1 — Recover the original combined hash:**

$$
H_{\text{claimed}} = D(PU_C, \; DS)
$$

**Explanation:** Decryption with the Cardholder's public key inverts the RSA signing operation and yields the digest that was signed.

**Step M2 — Recompute the combined hash locally:**

$$
H_{\text{recomputed}} = H(H(PI) \;\Vert\; H(OI))
$$

**Explanation:** The Merchant already has $OI$, computes $H(OI)$, and uses the $H(PI)$ it received from the Bank (forwarded in a separate signed block) to reconstruct the concatenation and re-hash.

**Step M3 — Compare and decide:**

$$
H_{\text{claimed}} \stackrel{?}{=} H_{\text{recomputed}}
$$

$$
\begin{cases}
\text{EQUAL} \Rightarrow \text{DS valid AND OI linked to PI (linkage confirmed)} \\
\text{NOT EQUAL} \Rightarrow \text{Reject transaction}
\end{cases}
$$

### 3.2.2 Verification at the Bank Side

The Bank receives $PI$, $H(OI)$, and $DS$.

**Step B1 — Recover combined hash:**

$$
H_{\text{claimed}} = D(PU_C, \; DS)
$$

**Step B2 — Recompute:**

$$
H_{\text{recomputed}} = H(H(PI) \;\Vert\; H(OI))
$$

**Step B3 — Compare and decide** (same logic as M3).

> [!TIP]
> **Crucial Insight for Exam:** The Merchant can verify $OI$ was committed by $C$ *and* linked to a $PI$, but **cannot** learn the card number. The Bank can verify $PI$ was committed by $C$ *and* linked to an $OI$, but **cannot** learn what was bought. This is the elegance of Dual Signature.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Various Attacks on Dual Signature

| Attack | Goal | Effect on DS | Reason |
|:-------|:-----|:-------------|:-------|
| Modify OI | Change purchase details | Detected by Merchant | $H(OI)$ changes, recomputed combined hash mismatches $D(PU_C, DS)$ |
| Modify PI | Change payment amount | Detected by Bank | $H(PI)$ changes, combined hash mismatches |
| Replay old OI/PI | Reuse a previous authorization | Detected by timestamps & nonce | SET includes transaction IDs and freshness tokens |
| Impersonate Cardholder | Sign as $C$ | Detected by PKI | Attacker lacks $PR_C$ |
| Swap $H(OI)$ between transactions | Link OI of one transaction to PI of another | Detected by both | The combined hash includes both digests, so swapping breaks linkage |
| Eavesdrop on OI | Learn purchase | Prevented by symmetric encryption with $K_{S1}$ | $K_{S1}$ is encrypted to $PU_M$ only |
| Eavesdrop on PI | Learn card number | Prevented by symmetric encryption with $K_{S2}$ | $K_{S2}$ is encrypted to $PU_B$ only |

## 3.4 End-to-End Payment Processing Walk-Through

The SET Purchase Process consists of **five flows** (per SET Book 3, May 1997):

### Flow 1: Cardholder → Merchant — **Purchase Request**

- Cardholder sends Purchase Request containing:
  - $E(PU_M, K_{S1}) \;\Vert\; E(K_{S1}, OI \;\Vert\; H(PI) \;\Vert\; DS)$
  - $E(PU_B, K_{S2}) \;\Vert\; E(K_{S2}, PI \;\Vert\; H(OI) \;\Vert\; DS)$
  - Cardholder's X.509 certificate $\text{Cert}_C$

### Flow 2: Merchant → Payment Gateway → Bank — **Payment Authorization Request**

- Merchant verifies Cardholder's certificate and dual signature.
- Forwards the Bank-bound envelope to the Payment Gateway unchanged.
- Adds Authorization Request signed with $PR_M$.

### Flow 3: Bank → Payment Gateway → Merchant — **Authorization Response**

- Bank verifies Dual Signature on PI.
- Checks card, debit limit, and risk model.
- If approved, generates Authorization Response with $DS_B$ and sends back.

### Flow 4: Merchant → Cardholder — **Purchase Response**

- Merchant confirms transaction with Cardholder and delivers goods/services.

### Flow 5: Merchant → Bank — **Payment Capture Request**

- Merchant requests actual fund transfer from Bank, citing the earlier Authorization token.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SET Payment Processing — End-to-End Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef entity fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef process fill:#065f46,stroke:#34d399,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef data fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fb923c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef verify fill:#581c87,stroke:#c084fc,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    nodeC[Cardholder C]:::entity
    nodeM[Merchant M]:::entity
    nodePG[Payment Gateway PG]:::entity
    nodeB[Issuer Bank B]:::entity
    nodeCA[Certificate Authority CA]:::entity

    step1["Step 1: Purchase Request - OI + PI + DS + Certs"]:::process
    step2["Step 2: Verify C cert and DS at M"]:::verify
    step3["Step 3: Payment Auth Request forwarded to B via PG"]:::process
    step4["Step 4: Verify PI and DS at B"]:::verify
    step5["Step 5: Auth Response - Approved or Declined"]:::data
    step6["Step 6: Purchase Response - Goods Delivered"]:::data
    step7["Step 7: Payment Capture - Funds transferred"]:::data

    data1["OI Encrypted to PU_M + PI Encrypted to PU_B + Dual Signature"]:::data
    data2["PI + H(OI) + DS forwarded"]:::data
    data3["Auth Code + Digital Receipt"]:::data

    nodeC -->|"Initiates transaction"| data1
    data1 --> step1
    step1 --> step2
    step2 -->|"If valid"| step3
    step3 --> data2
    data2 --> step4
    step4 -->|"If valid"| step5
    step5 --> data3
    data3 --> step6
    step6 --> step7

    nodeCA -.->|"Issues X.509v3 certs"| nodeC
    nodeCA -.->|"Issues X.509v3 certs"| nodeM
    nodeCA -.->|"Issues X.509v3 certs"| nodeB
    nodePG -.->|"Relays encrypted messages"| nodeB
```

## 4.2 Dual Signature Generation — Detailed Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef input fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,color:#ffffff
    classDef hash fill:#065f46,stroke:#34d399,color:#ffffff
    classDef crypto fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fb923c,color:#ffffff
    classDef output fill:#581c87,stroke:#c084fc,color:#ffffff

    inOI[Order Information OI]:::input
    inPI[Payment Information PI]:::input
    inKey[Cardholder Private Key PR_C]:::input

    hash1["H(OI) = SHA-1 of OI"]:::hash
    hash2["H(PI) = SHA-1 of PI"]:::hash
    concat["Concatenate: H(PI) || H(OI)"]:::crypto
    hash3["Second Hash: H(H(PI) || H(OI))"]:::hash
    sign["RSA Sign: DS = E(PR_C, H(H(PI) || H(OI)))"]:::crypto

    outDS[Dual Signature DS - 1024 or 2048 bits]:::output

    inOI --> hash1
    inPI --> hash2
    hash1 --> concat
    hash2 --> concat
    concat --> hash3
    hash3 --> sign
    inKey --> sign
    sign --> outDS
```

## 4.3 Dual Signature Verification — Side-by-Side Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef merch fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,color:#ffffff
    classDef bank fill:#065f46,stroke:#34d399,color:#ffffff
    classDef joint fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fb923c,color:#ffffff

    subgraph MERCHANT_SIDE["MERCHANT VERIFICATION"]
        M_OI["Has: OI"]:::merch
        M_HPI["Receives: H(PI) from Bank"]:::merch
        M_DS["Receives: DS from Cardholder"]:::merch
        M_DEC["D(PU_C, DS) = H(H(PI) || H(OI)) claimed"]:::merch
        M_REC["Locally compute: H(H(PI) || H(OI)) using H(OI)"]:::merch
        M_CMP{"Compare digests"}:::merch
        M_OK[Valid and Linked]:::merch
        M_NO[Reject Transaction]:::merch
    end

    subgraph BANK_SIDE["BANK VERIFICATION"]
        B_PI["Has: PI"]:::bank
        B_HOI["Receives: H(OI) from Merchant"]:::bank
        B_DS["Receives: DS from Cardholder"]:::bank
        B_DEC["D(PU_C, DS) = H(H(PI) || H(OI)) claimed"]:::bank
        B_REC["Locally compute: H(H(PI) || H(OI)) using H(PI)"]:::bank
        B_CMP{"Compare digests"}:::bank
        B_OK[Valid and Linked]:::bank
        B_NO[Reject Transaction]:::bank
    end

    M_DS --> M_DEC
    M_OI --> M_REC
    M_HPI --> M_REC
    M_DEC --> M_CMP
    M_REC --> M_CMP
    M_CMP -->|Match| M_OK
    M_CMP -->|Mismatch| M_NO

    B_DS --> B_DEC
    B_PI --> B_REC
    B_HOI --> B_REC
    B_DEC --> B_CMP
    B_REC --> B_CMP
    B_CMP -->|Match| B_OK
    B_CMP -->|Mismatch| B_NO
```

## 4.4 SET Message Exchange — Sequential Block Architecture

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Cardholder
    participant M as Merchant
    participant PG as Payment Gateway
    participant B as Bank

    Note over C,B: 1. PURCHASE REQUEST
    C->>M: Purchase Request<br/>OI encrypted to PU_M<br/>PI encrypted to PU_B<br/>DS + Certs
    Note over C: DS = E(PR_C, H(H(PI) || H(OI)))

    Note over M,B: 2. PAYMENT AUTHORIZATION
    M->>M: Verify Cert_C and Dual Signature on OI
    M->>PG: Payment Auth Request<br/>PI encrypted to PU_B forwarded
    PG->>B: Forward to Bank

    Note over B: 3. AUTHORIZATION
    B->>B: Verify Cert_C and Dual Signature on PI
    B->>B: Check card, balance, fraud rules
    B->>PG: Auth Response (Approved/Declined)
    PG->>M: Auth Response

    Note over C,M: 4. PURCHASE RESPONSE
    M->>C: Purchase Response<br/>Confirm transaction

    Note over M,B: 5. PAYMENT CAPTURE
    M->>PG: Payment Capture Request
    PG->>B: Capture Funds
    B-->>M: Capture Response
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Q:** Define **Dual Signature** in SET and state why it is required.

**Model Answer:**

Dual Signature is a cryptographic construct in the Secure Electronic Transaction (SET) protocol that binds two distinct messages — Order Information (OI) and Payment Information (PI) — using a single RSA signature, allowing the Merchant and the Bank to independently verify the linkage between the two messages without seeing each other's content.

It is **required** because:
1. It enables **confidentiality** — the Merchant does not see the card number and the Bank does not see the purchase details.
2. It provides **linkage** — the Bank can confirm the PI corresponds to a specific OI, and the Merchant can confirm the OI has a corresponding payment authorization.
3. It ensures **non-repudiation** — the Cardholder cannot later deny authorizing the exact transaction.

The formula is:
$$DS = E(PR_C, \; H(H(PI) \;\Vert\; H(OI)))$$

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]`

> **Q:** List the **major participants** in a SET transaction and write the **full form of OI and PI**.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Participant | Full Form |
|:-:|:------------|:----------|
| 1 | C | **Cardholder** |
| 2 | M | **Merchant** |
| 3 | P | **Payment Gateway** |
| 4 | CA | **Certificate Authority** |
| 5 | I | **Issuer Bank** |
| 6 | A | **Acquirer Bank** |

- **OI** = **Order Information** (purchase details: items, quantity, price, shipping address)
- **PI** = **Payment Information** (card number, expiry date, amount, card brand)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Q:**
> **(a)** Explain the **complete payment processing** in SET with a neat flow diagram and message contents. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Describe **dual signature generation and verification** in detail with all the cryptographic steps and equations. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part A(a) — Model Solution

The **SET Payment Processing** involves five sequential message flows:

**Flow 1 — Purchase Request (Cardholder → Merchant):**
The Cardholder constructs OI and PI, computes the Dual Signature, then creates two digital envelopes:
- Envelope to Merchant: $E(PU_M, K_{S1}) \;\Vert\; E(K_{S1}, \; OI \;\Vert\; H(PI) \;\Vert\; DS)$
- Envelope to Bank: $E(PU_B, K_{S2}) \;\Vert\; E(K_{S2}, \; PI \;\Vert\; H(OI) \;\Vert\; DS)$

The Cardholder also sends their X.509 certificate containing $PU_C$.

**Flow 2 — Payment Authorization Request (Merchant → Bank via Payment Gateway):**
The Merchant verifies the Cardholder's certificate with the CA, verifies the Dual Signature on OI, then forwards the bank-bound envelope unchanged. The Merchant adds its own Authorization Request signed with $PR_M$.

**Flow 3 — Authorization Response (Bank → Merchant):**
The Bank verifies Cardholder's certificate, verifies the Dual Signature on PI, and checks the card's validity and balance. If approved, it generates an Authorization Response with a digital signature and an auth code.

**Flow 4 — Purchase Response (Merchant → Cardholder):**
The Merchant confirms the transaction and delivers the goods/services to the Cardholder.

**Flow 5 — Payment Capture (Merchant → Bank):**
The Merchant submits a Capture Request to claim the actual funds from the Bank, using the previously obtained Authorization token.

#### Part A(b) — Model Solution

**Dual Signature Generation** (at the Cardholder):

**Step 1:** Compute the two SHA-1 message digests.
$$H(OI) = \text{SHA-1}(OI), \quad H(PI) = \text{SHA-1}(PI)$$

**Step 2:** Concatenate $H(PI)$ followed by $H(OI)$.
$$H(PI) \;\Vert\; H(OI)$$

**Step 3:** Hash the concatenation.
$$H(H(PI) \;\Vert\; H(OI))$$

**Step 4:** Sign with the Cardholder's RSA private key $PR_C$.
$$DS = E(PR_C, \; H(H(PI) \;\Vert\; H(OI)))$$

**Dual Signature Verification** (at the Merchant and Bank):

**Step 1:** Decrypt the Dual Signature using the Cardholder's public key $PU_C$.
$$H_{\text{claimed}} = D(PU_C, \; DS)$$

**Step 2:** Recompute the combined hash locally.
$$H_{\text{recomputed}} = H(H(PI) \;\Vert\; H(OI))$$

**Step 3:** Compare.
$$
H_{\text{claimed}} \stackrel{?}{=} H_{\text{recomputed}}
$$

If equal, the signature is **valid** AND the two messages are **cryptographically linked**.

> **Security Properties Achieved:** Confidentiality (each party sees only its own message), Integrity (any modification is detected), Authentication (Cardholder's identity verified by certificate), Linkage (Bank and Merchant can both verify the OI and PI are tied), and Non-Repudiation (Cardholder cannot deny).

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Q:**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **concept, motivation, and use** of the Dual Signature in the SET protocol. How does it ensure that the Merchant cannot deny the order? **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss the **security attacks** possible on the SET protocol. Explain how the Dual Signature and other mechanisms in SET counter these attacks. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part B(a) — Model Solution

**Concept:** The Dual Signature in SET is a single RSA signature computed over a combined hash of two independent message digests $H(OI)$ and $H(PI)$. It is generated by the Cardholder and distributed to both the Merchant and the Bank along with their respective messages.

**Motivation:** In a typical credit card transaction, three requirements conflict:
1. The Merchant must receive the order details (OI) to ship goods.
2. The Bank must receive the payment details (PI) to authorize payment.
3. Neither party should see the other party's message, **yet** the two must be provably linked.

The Dual Signature satisfies all three simultaneously.

**Use:** It allows:
- The **Merchant** to verify the Cardholder's commitment to a specific order linked to a payment.
- The **Bank** to verify the Cardholder's commitment to a payment linked to a specific order.
- **Either party** to detect any tampering of the messages.

**Non-Repudiation by the Merchant:** When the Merchant receives the Dual Signature, OI, and the Cardholder's certificate, the Merchant can:
1. Verify the certificate with the CA → authenticates the Cardholder's public key.
2. Compute $H(H(PI) \;\Vert\; H(OI))$ locally using $H(PI)$ forwarded by the Bank.
3. Compare with $D(PU_C, DS)$.

If valid, the Merchant holds cryptographic proof that the Cardholder:
- Agreed to the exact terms in OI.
- Authorized a corresponding PI in the same transaction.

This proof, signed by the Cardholder's private key, **cannot be repudiated**. Furthermore, the Bank's authorization response (signed by the Bank) is non-repudiable by the Bank, and the Merchant's capture request is non-repudiable by the Merchant.

#### Part B(b) — Model Solution

**Possible Security Attacks on SET:**

| # | Attack | Description | SET Counter-Mechanism |
|:-:|:-------|:------------|:---------------------|
| 1 | **Eavesdropping** | Intercept OI/PI in transit | DES-CBC bulk encryption with RSA-encrypted session keys |
| 2 | **Tampering** | Modify OI/PI contents | SHA-1 digest + Dual Signature integrity check |
| 3 | **Impersonation** | Fake Cardholder identity | X.509v3 certificates issued by trusted CA |
| 4 | **Repudiation** | Customer denies purchase | Dual Signature + transaction IDs + cert chain |
| 5 | **Replay Attack** | Resubmit old authorization | Transaction IDs, nonces, timestamps |
| 6 | **Man-in-the-Middle** | Intercept and alter messages | RSA-encrypted session keys; certificates bind keys to identities |
| 7 | **Cross-Link Attack** | Link an OI of one transaction to PI of another | Dual Signature binds *both* hashes; any mismatch invalidates |
| 8 | **Card Theft / Forgery** | Use stolen card data | PI is encrypted only to Bank's public key; merchant never sees it |
| 9 | **Merchant Fraud** | Deliver goods without payment, or charge without delivery | Bank authorization + capture separated into distinct flows |
| 10 | **Disclosure of Card Info to Merchant** | Merchant learns card number | Card number is in PI, sent only to Bank; Merchant only sees $H(PI)$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong hash order:** Students often write $H(H(OI) \;\Vert\; H(PI))$ instead of $H(H(PI) \;\Vert\; H(OI))$. SET specifies PI-hash **first**. Wrong order ⇒ **3 marks deduction**.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention hash propagation:** Many students say "Merchant and Bank verify DS" but forget to mention that the **Merchant needs $H(PI)$ from the Bank** and the **Bank needs $H(OI)$ from the Merchant**. ⇒ **2 marks deduction**.
> 3. **Confusing SET with SSL/TLS:** SET is a **payment-specific application-layer protocol**, not a transport security protocol. Do not equate it to HTTPS. ⇒ **1–2 marks deduction**.
> 4. **Omitting confidentiality requirement:** Dual Signature is *not* the only mechanism for confidentiality — **symmetric encryption of OI/PI** is also required. ⇒ **1 mark deduction**.
> 5. **Skipping certificate verification step:** Many answers forget that the public key $PU_C$ must be validated using CA's signature. ⇒ **1 mark deduction**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **⚡ High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ☐ **SET** is a **payment-specific application-layer protocol** developed by **Visa and Mastercard in 1996**.
- ☐ SET uses **DES (symmetric)** for bulk data, **RSA (asymmetric)** for signatures and key transport, and **SHA-1** for digests.
- ☐ SET has **six participants**: Cardholder, Merchant, Payment Gateway, Issuer Bank, Acquirer Bank, Certificate Authority.
- ☐ **Payment Processing** consists of **5 flows**: Purchase Request, Payment Authorization, Authorization Response, Purchase Response, Payment Capture.
- ☐ **OI** (Order Information) → sent to **Merchant**; **PI** (Payment Information) → sent to **Bank**.
- ☐ **Dual Signature (DS) formula**: $DS = E(PR_C, \, H(H(PI) \;\Vert\; H(OI)))$.
- ☐ **Canonical order in concatenation**: **$H(PI)$ FIRST, $H(OI)$ SECOND** (memorize!).
- ☐ **Verification**: Decrypt DS with $PU_C$ to recover combined hash; recompute combined hash locally; **compare for equality**.
- ☐ The **Merchant sees OI but not PI**; the **Bank sees PI but not OI**; yet both can verify **linkage**.
- ☐ The Merchant obtains $H(PI)$ from the Bank; the Bank obtains $H(OI)$ from the Merchant — these are **forwarded via signed headers**.
- ☐ **Properties guaranteed**: Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Linkage, Non-Repudiation.
- ☐ **Cardholder certificate** binds identity to $PU_C$, signed by the CA.
- ☐ SET **does NOT use SSL/TLS** as its transport — it is **self-contained** application-layer security.
- ☐ **SHA-1** output length = **160 bits**; **DES** key = **56 bits**; **RSA** minimum = **1024 bits** (recommended 2048).
- ☐ Dual Signature is a **conceptual ancestor of modern linkable ring signatures** and **threshold signatures** used in blockchain.
- ☐ Failure to remember the **canonical concatenation order** ($PI$ first) is the most common exam pitfall.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Firewalls - Firewall Design Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT523 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Firewalls - Firewall Design Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Firewall** is a network security device (hardware, software, or a hybrid combination) that monitors and filters incoming and outgoing network traffic based on an organization's previously established security policies. Functionally, it acts as a **security perimeter** that establishes a barrier between a secured, trusted internal network and untrusted external networks (such as the Internet).

$$ \text{Firewall} = f(\text{Packet Inspection},\ \text{Policy Ruleset},\ \text{State Tracking}) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Expected Answer):**
> A firewall is a collection of components placed between two networks that collectively implement a **security policy** and provide **access control**, **authentication**, **packet filtering**, **logging**, and **alarming** services. It is the **first line of defense** in any network security architecture.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your home is a **trusted internal network**, and the street outside is the **untrusted Internet**.

- A **firewall** is like a **security guard stationed at your front door**.
- The guard has a **rulebook** (security policy) listing who may enter, when, and what they may bring inside.
- The guard does **not let strangers** (unauthorized packets) in by default.
- Authorized guests (legitimate traffic) are checked against an **ID list** (access control list).
- Anyone acting suspiciously (malformed or malicious packets) is **denied entry** and reported (logged).

In an enterprise, the "guard" is a **dedicated appliance or software** sitting at the network boundary, inspecting every packet before allowing it to pass.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology to Remember:**
> - **Trusted Network:** The internal corporate or private LAN whose traffic is assumed safe.
> - **Untrusted Network:** External networks (Internet, partner networks) whose traffic must be scrutinized.
> - **DMZ (Demilitarized Zone):** A buffer subnet that hosts public-facing services while keeping the internal LAN isolated.
> - **Bastion Host:** A hardened, exposed system placed in the DMZ as the only entry point.

### 1.3 Why Are Firewalls Needed?

| Threat | Firewall Mitigation |
|---|---|
| Unauthorized remote access | Filters inbound connection requests |
| Malware propagation | Blocks known malicious ports/protocols |
| Data exfiltration | Inspects outbound traffic patterns |
| Network reconnaissance | Hides internal IP topology via NAT |
| Denial of Service (DoS) | Rate limiting, connection thresholds |

### 1.4 Characteristics of an Effective Firewall

- **All traffic must pass through it** (single chokepoint).
- **Only authorized traffic** is permitted (default deny principle).
- The firewall itself is **immune to penetration** (hardened OS, minimal services).
- It must be **designed to withstand attacks** on itself (fault tolerance, logging).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Firewall Design Principles

A robust firewall design is built on **four foundational principles** that any KTU examiner will expect you to enumerate and explain.

#### Principle 1: Default-Deny Stance (Least Privilege)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Everything that is not explicitly permitted is denied."**

This is the **single most important design principle** and the highest-yield keyword for KTU answers.

$$ \forall \text{ packet } p : \big( p \in \text{Allowed} \iff \exists \text{ rule } r \in \text{Ruleset} : \text{match}(p, r) = \text{TRUE} \big) $$

If no rule matches, the packet is **silently dropped** (or rejected with an ICMP unreachable message).

#### Principle 2: Chokepoint Concept

All traffic between the protected network and the outside world must **physically and logically pass through the firewall**. This forces attackers to face a single hardened point of resistance.

#### Principle 3: Defense in Depth

A firewall is **never the only control**. It works in conjunction with:
- **IDS/IPS** (Intrusion Detection/Prevention Systems)
- **VPNs** (Virtual Private Networks)
- **Anti-malware gateways**
- **Authentication servers** (RADIUS, TACACS+)

#### Principle 4: Least Common Mechanism

The firewall should share as few services as possible with the systems it protects, reducing the attack surface.

### 2.2 Types of Firewalls (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Firewall Type | OSI Layer | Inspection Level | Strength | Weakness | KTU Mark Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Packet Filtering** | Network (L3) / Data-Link (L2) | Header fields only | High speed, low cost | Stateless, easily spoofed | 3 marks |
| **Stateful Inspection** | L3–L4 | Header + connection state table | Tracks sessions, blocks invalid packets | Slower than stateless, more memory | 4 marks |
| **Circuit-Level Gateway** | Session (L5) | TCP handshake validity | Hides internal IP, validates sessions | Does not inspect payload | 2 marks |
| **Application-Level Gateway (Proxy)** | Application (L7) | Full payload content | Deep inspection, protocol-aware | Per-application setup, latency | 4 marks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When a question asks "compare" the firewall types, always present them in a **tabular form** with **OSI layer, advantages, and disadvantages**. This is the examiner's preferred answer pattern.

### 2.3 Packet Filtering — Rule Processing Logic

A packet filter examines each packet against a sequential **Access Control List (ACL)**:

$$ \text{Decision}(p) = \begin{cases} \text{ALLOW} & \text{if } \exists r_i \in \text{ACL} : \text{match}(p, r_i) = \text{TRUE and } r_i.\text{action} = \text{PERMIT} \\ \text{DENY} & \text{otherwise (default)} \end{cases} $$

**Matching Fields (Exam Favorite):**
- **Source IP address** (32 bits for IPv4)
- **Destination IP address**
- **Source Port** (16 bits, range **0–65535**)
- **Destination Port**
- **Protocol flag** (TCP = 6, UDP = 17, ICMP = 1)
- **ACK bit** (for inbound filtering)
- **ICMP type/code**

### 2.4 Stateful Inspection — Connection State Tracking

A stateful firewall maintains a **State Table** that records every legitimate connection:

$$ \text{State Table} = \{ (src\_IP, src\_port, dst\_IP, dst\_port, protocol, state) \} $$

Valid states per TCP lifecycle: `NEW → ESTABLISHED → RELATED → FIN_WAIT → CLOSED`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stateful Rule:**
> An **inbound** packet is allowed **only if** it matches an existing connection entry (i.e., it is a response to an internal-initiated request). This blocks **unsolicited SYN packets** (the basis of SYN-flood DoS attacks).

### 2.5 Application-Level Gateway (Proxy Firewall)

A proxy acts as a **middleman**: the client never speaks directly to the server.

$$ \text{Client} \longleftrightarrow \text{Proxy} \longleftrightarrow \text{Server} $$

The proxy can:
- Re-authenticate users
- Inspect application-layer payload (e.g., HTTP headers, MIME types)
- Block malicious URLs, scripts, or virus signatures
- Cache content for performance

### 2.6 Bastion Host

A **bastion host** is a computer that is **fully exposed to attack** (positioned in the DMZ) but **hardened** against them. It runs only essential services (e.g., a single proxy, SSH). It is the **only machine** the firewall permits to be addressed from outside.

### 2.7 Firewall Configuration Topologies (KTU Must-Know)

| Topology | Diagram Idea | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Screened Host** | Single firewall + bastion host | Small organizations |
| **Screened Subnet (DMZ)** | Two firewalls sandwich a DMZ | Medium/large enterprise |
| **Dual-Homed Host** | Host with two NICs, routing disabled | Quasi-firewall |
| **Back-to-Back VPN** | Internal firewall + external firewall | VPN termination |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise networks** use Next-Generation Firewalls (NGFW) like **Palo Alto, Fortinet, Cisco ASA**.
- **Cloud environments** rely on Security Groups and Network ACLs (AWS, Azure).
- **Industrial CPS (Cyber-Physical Systems)** use firewalls to segment **OT (Operational Technology)** from **IT networks** as mandated by **IEC 62443**.
- **Embedded devices** employ lightweight **iptables/nftables** rules in Linux-based IoT gateways.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> This section provides exhaustive analytical derivations of firewall decision logic, rule ordering proofs, and port-based access control mathematics. Every logical transition is explicitly written out per the V10 protocol.

### 3.1 Derivation: Rule Ordering and First-Match Semantics

**Given:** A packet $p$ with header $H_p = (sIP, dIP, sPort, dPort, proto)$ and an ordered ACL with $n$ rules.

**Step 1:** Define the matching predicate for rule $r_i$:

$$
\text{match}(p, r_i) = \big( sIP \in r_i.\text{src\_range} \big) \wedge \big( dIP \in r_i.\text{dst\_range} \big) \wedge \big( sPort \in r_i.\text{sp\_range} \big) \wedge \big( dPort \in r_i.\text{dp\_range} \big) \wedge \big( proto = r_i.\text{protocol} \big)
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **first-match** evaluation in order $r_1 \rightarrow r_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow r_n$:

$$
\text{Decision}(p) = \text{FIRST}\big( \{ r_i.\text{action} \mid \text{match}(p, r_i) = \text{TRUE},\ i = 1, 2, \ldots, n \} \big)
$$

**Step 3:** If no rule matches (empty set), apply default-deny:

$$
\text{Decision}(p) = \text{DENY} \quad \text{if}\ \forall i,\ \text{match}(p, r_i) = \text{FALSE}
$$

**Conclusion:** The order of ACL rules is **non-commutative** — a more specific rule placed *below* a general `PERMIT ANY` rule is **never reached**. This is the foundation of firewall rulebase auditing tools.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: TCP Connection State Machine for Stateful Filter

**Given:** A TCP packet $p$ with flags $F_p \subseteq \{SYN, ACK, FIN, RST, URG, PSH\}$.

**Step 1:** Define legal TCP flag transitions during a three-way handshake:

$$
\text{HS}_1 : \text{CLIENT} \xrightarrow{SYN=1} \text{SERVER} \quad (\text{state} = \text{SYN\_SENT})
$$

$$
\text{HS}_2 : \text{SERVER} \xrightarrow{SYN=1, ACK=1} \text{CLIENT} \quad (\text{state} = \text{SYN\_RECEIVED})
$$

$$
\text{HS}_3 : \text{CLIENT} \xrightarrow{ACK=1} \text{SERVER} \quad (\text{state} = \text{ESTABLISHED})
$$

**Step 2:** Stateful firewall decision rule:

$$
\text{Allow}(p) = \begin{cases}
\text{TRUE} & \text{if}\ F_p = \{SYN\}\ \text{and}\ p.\text{dst} \in \text{InternalNetwork} \\
\text{TRUE} & \text{if}\ p \text{matches an entry in StateTable} \\
\text{TRUE} & \text{if}\ F_p \supseteq \{ACK\}\ \text{and}\ p \text{belongs to established connection} \\
\text{FALSE} & \text{otherwise (drop unsolicited inbound SYN)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3:** Anti-spoofing: inbound packet with $sIP \in$ InternalRange is **automatically denied** (RFC 2827 best practice):

$$
\text{Allow}(p) = \text{FALSE}\ \ \text{if}\ \ p.\text{src} \in \text{InternalSubnet} \ \wedge\ \ p.\text{arrived\_on} = \text{ExternalInterface}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Port Number Space for Service Filtering

The TCP/UDP port range spans 16 bits:

$$
\text{Port Space} = \{ 0, 1, 2, \ldots, 65535 \} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2^{16} = 65{,}536\ \text{ports}
$$

Standard service ports (IANA assignment):

| Port | Service | Protocol |
|---|---|---|
| **20, 21** | FTP (Data, Control) | TCP |
| **22** | SSH | TCP |
| **23** | Telnet | TCP |
| **25** | SMTP | TCP |
| **53** | DNS | TCP/UDP |
| **80** | HTTP | TCP |
| **110** | POP3 | TCP |
| **143** | IMAP | TCP |
| **443** | HTTPS | TCP |
| **3389** | RDP | TCP |

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** When asked "Why is Telnet (port 23) typically blocked by firewalls?" — answer: *"Telnet transmits credentials in plaintext, making it vulnerable to packet sniffing. Firewalls block it in favor of SSH (port 22), which provides encrypted authentication."*

---

### 3.4 Derivation: DMZ Mathematics — Subnet Address Calculation

**Given:** A corporate network with public IP block `203.0.113.0/24` and a need to segment into:
- **External zone** (untrusted, no public servers)
- **DMZ** (public-facing web/mail servers)
- **Internal zone** (employees, databases)

**Step 1:** Subnet the /24 into three equal subnets using 2 extra bits:

$$
2^2 = 4\ \text{subnets},\quad \text{new prefix} = /26,\quad \text{hosts per subnet} = 2^{6} - 2 = 62
$$

**Step 2:** Compute subnet boundaries:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DMZ}        &: \ \ 203.0.113.0/26  \quad \text{range: } 203.0.113.0 \rightarrow 203.0.113.63 \\
\text{Internal}  &: \ \ 203.0.113.64/26 \quad \text{range: } 203.0.113.64 \rightarrow 203.0.113.127 \\
\text{Guest WiFi}&: \ \ 203.0.113.128/26\quad \text{range: } 203.0.113.128\rightarrow 203.0.113.191 \\
\text{Reserved}  &: \ \ 203.0.113.192/26\quad \text{range: } 203.0.113.192\rightarrow 203.0.113.255
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Filter placement: External firewall between ISP ↔ DMZ; Internal firewall between DMZ ↔ Internal LAN. **Dual-firewall architecture.**

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance Parameters

| Metric | Packet Filter | Stateful Filter | Proxy Firewall | NGFW |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Throughput** | ~10 Gbps | ~5 Gbps | ~1 Gbps | ~2 Gbps |
| **Latency added** | < 10 μs | ~50 μs | ~1 ms | ~200 μs |
| **CPU cost per packet** | Low | Medium | High | Medium-High |
| **State table size** | None | 100k–1M flows | Per session | 1M+ flows |
| **Application awareness** | None | Limited | Full | Full + DPI |
| **Cost (approx.)** | Low | Medium | High | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Screened Subnet (DMZ) — Dual Firewall Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Internet["INTERNET Untrusted Network"]:::untrusted
    ExtFW["EXTERNAL FIREWALL Packet Filter"]:::fw
    Bastion["BASTION HOST Public Web Server"]:::dmz
    MailSrv["MAIL SERVER Port 25 110"]:::dmz
    IntFW["INTERNAL FIREWALL Stateful Filter"]:::fw
    LAN["INTERNAL LAN Trusted Workstations DB Servers"]:::trusted

    Internet -->|HTTP 80 HTTPS 443| ExtFW
    ExtFW --> Bastion
    ExtFW --> MailSrv
    Bastion --> IntFW
    MailSrv --> IntFW
    IntFW --> LAN

    classDef untrusted fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef trusted fill:#d6f0ff,stroke:#005080,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fw fill:#fff4b3,stroke:#806000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef dmz fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#00802b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **External Firewall** filters raw Internet traffic and permits only ports 80/443/25/110 to reach the DMZ.
- The **Bastion Host** hosts the public web server; it is hardened and continuously patched.
- The **Internal Firewall** blocks the DMZ from initiating connections to the internal LAN.
- An attacker compromising the bastion host still faces the internal firewall to reach critical data.

---

### 4.2 Screened Host Firewall (Single Firewall Variant)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Net["EXTERNAL NETWORK Internet"]:::untrusted
    Router["SCREENING ROUTER Packet Filter"]:::fw
    Bastion2["BASTION HOST Application Proxy"]:::bastion
    Inside["INTERNAL NETWORK Trusted LAN"]:::trusted

    Net -->|"SYN -> port 80 allowed"| Router
    Router -->|"filtered traffic"| Bastion2
    Bastion2 -->|"authenticated proxied requests"| Inside
    Inside -.->|"outbound responses"| Bastion2
    Bastion2 -.->|"filtered responses"| Router
    Router -.->|"permitted packets"| Net

    classDef untrusted fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef trusted fill:#d6f0ff,stroke:#005080,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fw fill:#fff4b3,stroke:#806000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bastion fill:#e0d6ff,stroke:#400080,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

### 4.3 Stateful Inspection — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Pkt["INCOMING PACKET"] --> ChkProto{"Protocol = TCP?"}
    ChkProto -->|No| UDPP["Check UDP/ICMP Rule against ACL"]:::node
    ChkProto -->|Yes| FlagChk{"SYN=1 and ACK=0?"}
    FlagChk -->|Yes| NewConn["Is destination a permitted service?"]:::node
    FlagChk -->|No| StateLookup{"Entry exists in StateTable?"}
    StateLookup -->|Yes| AllowPkt["ALLOW Matched Connection"]:::allow
    StateLookup -->|No| CheckACL{"Matches an explicit ACL rule?"}
    CheckACL -->|Yes| AllowPkt2["ALLOW by ACL"]:::allow
    CheckACL -->|No| DropPkt["DENY Default Drop"]:::deny
    NewConn -->|No| DropPkt
    NewConn -->|Yes| CreateEntry["Create StateTable Entry then ALLOW"]:::allow

    classDef allow fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#00802b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef deny fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#eef,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Proxy Firewall (Application-Level Gateway) Operation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Internal Client
    participant P as Proxy Firewall L7
    participant S as External Web Server
    C->>P: HTTP GET www.example.com/index.html
    Note over P: Inspect URL, headers, MIME, payload
    P->>P: Apply policy URL allowlist malware scan
    P->>S: HTTP GET example.com/index.html
    S-->>P: HTTP 200 OK with content
    P-->>C: HTTP 200 OK with content
    Note over C,S: Client and Server NEVER exchange packets directly
```
