## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> 1. **Do not confuse stateful inspection with deep packet inspection (DPI).** Stateful = tracks connection state; DPI = inspects payload content. Mixing them costs full marks.
> 2. **Always state "default-deny"** when describing packet filtering design — generic answers like "blocks malicious traffic" earn only partial credit.
> 3. **Do NOT list proxy firewalls as "slow"** without justification — mention "per-application processing overhead."
> 4. **In diagrams, label the DMZ explicitly.** Examiners deduct marks for unlabeled zones.
> 5. **Rule order matters.** If you write `PERMIT ANY` before a specific deny rule, the specific rule is dead code — call this out in your answer.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain the four characteristics that a firewall should possess. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
1. **All traffic must pass through the firewall** — The system must be the single chokepoint enforcing policy. (1 mark)
2. **Only authorized traffic is allowed** — The firewall implements the *default-deny* principle, blocking everything except explicitly permitted flows. (1 mark)
3. **The firewall itself must be immune to penetration** — It runs a hardened OS with minimum services and is itself a security-aware host. (0.5 mark)
4. **The firewall must be designed to withstand attacks on itself** — It must support fault tolerance, redundant components, and detailed audit logging. (0.5 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between packet filtering firewall and stateful inspection firewall. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Packet Filtering | Stateful Inspection |
|---|---|---|
| **Inspection depth** | Header fields only | Header + connection state |
| **State awareness** | Stateless — each packet treated in isolation | Maintains a state table of all active connections |
| **Protection against** | Basic header-based attacks | SYN floods, session hijacking, ACK scans |
| **Performance** | Faster, less memory | Slightly slower, more memory |
| **Example** | Linux iptables basic rules | Cisco ASA, pfSense with state tracking |

(1 mark for each valid point, maximum 3 marks.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (Answer ANY ONE, 14 Marks)

---

#### ❑ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **design principles of a firewall** with reference to the default-deny policy and chokepoint concept. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Default-Deny Policy (4 marks):**
   - The default-deny principle states: *"Everything not explicitly allowed is denied."*
   - It is implemented via an ordered Access Control List (ACL). Each packet is matched sequentially; if no rule permits it, the packet is **dropped** (or rejected with ICMP unreachable).
   - Mathematically: $\text{Decision}(p) = \text{ALLOW}$ iff $\exists r \in \text{ACL}: \text{match}(p, r) = \text{TRUE}$ and $r.\text{action} = \text{PERMIT}$; otherwise, $\text{Decision}(p) = \text{DENY}$.
   - **Advantages:** (i) Closes unknown attack vectors by default. (ii) Forces explicit policy documentation. (iii) Eases audit and compliance.
   - **Disadvantage:** If a required service is not in the ruleset, it is silently broken — requires proactive management.

2. **Chokepoint Concept (3 marks):**
   - All inbound and outbound traffic between the protected network and external networks must pass through the firewall — it is the **single point of enforcement**.
   - This requires **physical topology design** (firewall sits on the only path to the Internet router).
   - Provides a single point for **logging, monitoring, and intrusion detection**.
   - If the chokepoint fails, the network is unprotected — hence **redundant firewalls** (active-passive or active-active HA pairs) are deployed.

---

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Screened Subnet (DMZ) firewall configuration** and discuss why a **dual-firewall** setup is preferred over a single firewall. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram (to be drawn by student):**

```
                Internet
                   │
            ┌──────▼──────┐
            │  External   │  (Packet Filter)
            │  Firewall   │
            └──┬───────┬──┘
               │       │
       ┌───────▼─┐  ┌──▼───────┐
       │  Web   │  │   Mail   │  (Bastion Hosts in DMZ)
       │ Server │  │  Server  │
       └───┬────┘  └────┬─────┘
           │            │
            ┌───▼────▼───┐
            │  Internal  │  (Stateful Inspection)
            │  Firewall  │
            └─────┬──────┘
                  │
            Internal LAN
```

**Explanation (4 marks):**
- The **DMZ** is a buffer network segment that hosts public-facing servers (web, mail, DNS).
- The **External Firewall** allows Internet → DMZ traffic on specific service ports (80, 443, 25, 110) and blocks all other inbound traffic to the internal LAN.
- The **Internal Firewall** allows internal LAN → DMZ (for updates) and DMZ → Internet (outbound responses) but **blocks DMZ → Internal LAN** initiation.
- Bastion hosts in the DMZ are hardened, run minimal services, and are continuously patched.

**Why dual-firewall is preferred (3 marks):**
1. **Defense in depth:** Compromise of the external firewall does not automatically expose the internal LAN.
2. **Policy separation:** Different rule sets for ingress and egress — easier to manage and audit.
3. **Failure isolation:** Hardware/software failure in one firewall does not collapse the entire security perimeter.

---

#### ❑ Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the **four types of firewalls** with their OSI layer, advantages, and disadvantages. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Packet Filtering Firewall (1.5 marks):**
   - OSI Layer: Network (L3) and Data-Link (L2).
   - Inspects each packet's header — source/destination IP, source/destination port, protocol.
   - *Advantage:* High speed, low cost, transparent to users.
   - *Disadvantage:* Stateless, vulnerable to IP spoofing, cannot inspect payload.

2. **Stateful Inspection Firewall (2 marks):**
   - OSI Layer: L3–L4.
   - Tracks the state of every TCP/UDP session in a state table.
   - Allows only packets matching an established or related connection.
   - *Advantage:* Defeats SYN flood, session hijacking; intelligent filtering.
   - *Disadvantage:* Higher memory footprint, slightly slower than stateless filters.

3. **Circuit-Level Gateway (1.5 marks):**
   - OSI Layer: Session (L5).
   - Validates the TCP three-way handshake and acts as a relay; does not inspect payload.
   - *Advantage:* Hides internal IP topology (NAT-like), low overhead.
   - *Disadvantage:* No deep content inspection.

4. **Application-Level Gateway (Proxy) (2 marks):**
   - OSI Layer: Application (L7).
   - Acts as a middleman — terminates and re-establishes the connection on behalf of the client.
   - *Advantage:* Full payload inspection, user authentication, URL filtering, malware scanning.
   - *Disadvantage:* Per-application configuration, higher latency, single point of failure.

---

**(b)** Explain the **default-deny policy** and the **bastion host** concept. How does a bastion host differ from an ordinary server? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Default-Deny Policy (3.5 marks):**
- *Definition:* A security policy in which all traffic is denied unless explicitly permitted by a rule.
- *Implementation:* Through an ordered Access Control List (ACL). The firewall evaluates each packet against rules in sequence; the first match decides the action. If no rule matches, the packet is **silently dropped**.
- *Mathematical form:* $\forall p,\ \text{Action}(p) = \text{ALLOW} \iff \exists r_i : \text{match}(p, r_i) = \text{TRUE and } r_i.\text{action} = \text{PERMIT}$.
- *Why preferred:* Minimizes attack surface, ensures unknown services are blocked by default, simplifies compliance audits.

**Bastion Host (3.5 marks):**
- *Definition:* A computer that is **exposed to the Internet** (placed in the DMZ) but **hardened** to resist attacks. It is the only machine directly addressable from outside the firewall.
- *Characteristics:*
  1. Runs a **minimal, hardened OS** with unnecessary services disabled.
  2. Has **only one application** (e.g., a web proxy, SSH server) — single-purpose.
  3. Undergoes **regular patching and security audits**.
  4. All unnecessary ports are **closed**; only required ports are open.
  5. Logs every action for audit.

**Difference from an ordinary server (included within above):**
- An ordinary server hosts multiple applications and may have several open ports. A **bastion host** is **single-purpose, hardened, and continuously monitored** — it is the **first target** for attackers and must therefore be the most resilient system on the network.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Remember Bullets for Last-Minute Revision:**

- **Firewall definition:** A network security device enforcing access control between trusted and untrusted networks.
- **Four characteristics:** Chokepoint, default-deny, immune-to-penetration, withstands attack on itself.
- **Four design principles:** Default-deny, chokepoint, defense in depth, least common mechanism.
- **Four firewall types:** Packet filter, Stateful inspection, Circuit-level gateway, Application-level gateway.
- **Default-deny formula:** $\text{Action}(p) = \text{ALLOW} \iff \exists r \in \text{ACL} : \text{match}(p, r) \land r.\text{action} = \text{PERMIT}$.
- **TCP state lifecycle:** `SYN_SENT → SYN_RECEIVED → ESTABLISHED → FIN_WAIT → CLOSED`.
- **Bastion host = hardened, single-purpose, DMZ-resident, exposed system.**
- **DMZ** = buffer subnet hosting public servers between two firewalls.
- **Screened subnet** uses two firewalls; **Screened host** uses one firewall + bastion.
- **Anti-spoofing rule:** Inbound packets with internal source IP on the external interface are **dropped** (RFC 2827).
- **Port space:** 16 bits = 65,536 ports (0–65535).
- **Well-known services to memorize for KTU:** FTP=20/21, SSH=22, Telnet=23, SMTP=25, DNS=53, HTTP=80, POP3=110, IMAP=143, HTTPS=443, RDP=3389.
- **NGFW** = Stateful + DPI + IPS + Application awareness.
- **CPS context (PECPT523):** Firewalls in IEC 62443 segment **OT** from **IT** in industrial systems.
- **Diagram rule:** Always label **DMZ, Bastion Host, External FW, Internal FW, and the three zones** — unmarked diagrams lose marks.
- **Default-deny keyword = 2 marks** guaranteed in any short note — never omit it.
